يَٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌۭ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًۭا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًۭا

“O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, "Three"; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs.” (Quran 4:171)

Here the Quran affirms the exalted titles — the Messiah, a messenger of God, His word, a soul from Him — and in the same breath draws the boundary: do not commit excess in religion; do not say “Three”; God is one, exalted above having a son. The very next verse explains why servanthood is no insult to the Messiah:

لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًۭا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًۭا

“Never would the Messiah disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels near [to Him]. And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant - He will gather them to Himself all together.” (Quran 4:172)

To be a servant of God is, in the Quran’s vision, the highest station a creature can hold. The Messiah does not disdain it — he embodies it.

Finally, the Quran points to something disarmingly simple about Jesus and his mother:

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

“The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded.” (Quran 5:75)

The Messiah and his truthful mother “both used to eat food.” Whoever eats depends on nourishment, and whoever depends on anything is a creature, not the self-sufficient Creator. For the Quran, Jesus’s messiahship and his full humanity sit together without tension.

sections.title: The Biblical Perspective: The Christ in the Gospels
sections.body:
The New Testament was written in Greek, and it translates the Hebrew title mashiach with the Greek word christos. One of its own verses shows the equivalence explicitly. When Andrew meets Jesus and runs to tell his brother, the Gospel of John pauses to explain the Hebrew title for its Greek-speaking readers:

“He first found his own brother, Simon, and said to him, “We have found the Messiah!” (which is, being interpreted, Christ).” (John 1:41, World English Bible)

“Messiah” and “Christ” are one word in two languages. The most famous confession of the title comes from Peter. When Jesus asks his disciples who they say he is, Peter answers:

“Simon Peter answered, “You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.”” (Matthew 16:16)

Christians have historically read “the Son of the living God” as a confession of Jesus’s divinity, joining the two halves of Peter’s sentence into one: the Messiah is the divine Son. Readers should know, however, that scholars of early Judaism note that “son of God” in a first-century Jewish setting was also a familiar way of speaking about the anointed king — a title of appointment rather than of shared divine essence. Which reading is correct is taken up in this library’s article on whether Jesus claimed to be God.

Jesus himself, in the Gospels, accepts the title of Messiah plainly. To a Samaritan woman who says she knows the Messiah is coming, he replies directly:

“The woman said to him, “I know that Messiah comes, he who is called Christ. When he has come, he will declare to us all things.” Jesus said to her, “I am he, the one who speaks to you.”” (John 4:25-26)

And in the synagogue at Nazareth, the Gospel of Luke records Jesus defining his own anointing by reading aloud from the scroll of the prophet Isaiah:

““The Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to preach good news to the poor. He has sent me to heal the broken hearted, to proclaim release to the captives, recovering of sight to the blind, to deliver those who are crushed, and to proclaim the acceptable year of the Lord.” He closed the book, gave it back to the attendant, and sat down. The eyes of all in the synagogue were fastened on him. He began to tell them, “Today, this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing.”” (Luke 4:18-21)

This passage repays attention. Jesus says the Spirit of the Lord is on him because God “has anointed” him — to preach good news, to heal, to proclaim release. The anointing is something done to Jesus by God, for a mission of preaching and mercy. For Christians, this scene opens a messiahship that culminates in the cross and resurrection: mainstream Christian teaching holds that Jesus fulfilled and transformed the messianic hope by dying for humanity’s sins and rising again, and “Christ” carries all of that within it.

sections.title: Comparing the Two Understandings
sections.body:
Set side by side, the agreement is real — and so is the difference.

Both faiths agree that Jesus is the Messiah, the anointed one promised to Israel; that the title was announced from God’s side, not seized as human ambition; and that it sets Jesus apart with unique honor. A Muslim can read Peter’s “You are the Christ” and Andrew’s “We have found the Messiah!” and affirm those confessions as far as the words themselves go.

The difference lies in what each tradition pours into the title. In Christian understanding, “Christ” came to mean the eternal Son of God made flesh, whose anointed mission was to die an atoning death and rise again. In Islamic understanding, al-Masih means what the word itself says: one anointed by God — chosen, purified, and commissioned as His servant and messenger to the Children of Israel.

Islam’s case is straightforward. The very grammar of the title implies servanthood: an anointed one is, by definition, anointed by someone above him. That is exactly what the synagogue scene in Luke shows — “The Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to preach good news to the poor.” The Anointer is God; the anointed is His servant. The Quran’s declaration that “Never would the Messiah disdain to be a servant of Allah” (Quran 4:172) is, on this reading, not a demotion of Jesus but a return to the title’s plain sense.

Honesty requires naming where the disagreement runs deepest. Christian scholarship holds that Jesus redefined the messianic hope around his death and resurrection, and historians of antiquity — religious and secular alike — overwhelmingly accept that Jesus was crucified. The Quran denies that the crucifixion befell Jesus, and Muslims accept this on the authority of revelation rather than by claiming the historical sources say otherwise; that question is examined honestly in this library’s article on the crucifixion. But Islam’s position on the meaning of “Messiah” does not depend on that dispute: the title, in its origin and on Jesus’s own lips at Nazareth, describes a human being anointed by God — precisely what the Quran affirms.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A first misconception: “Muslims deny that Jesus is the Messiah.” The opposite is true. The Quran names him al-Masih again and again, conferring the title through the angels before his birth (Quran 3:45). Denying it would contradict the Quran itself.

A second: “Messiah means a divine being, so calling Jesus the Messiah is calling him God.” Linguistically and historically, that is not what the word means. “Anointed one” was a title of Israel’s human kings and priests, and the Hebrew Bible applies it even to the Persian king Cyrus. Divinity entered the meaning of “Christ” through later Christian doctrine about Jesus — not through the word itself.

A third: “The Quran uses al-Masih as an empty borrowed name.” In fact the Quran deploys the title deliberately: it bestows it at the annunciation (3:45), joins it to Jesus’s role as God’s messenger and word (4:171), and reaches for it precisely where servanthood is affirmed (4:172) — as if to say that the anointed one, of all people, is God’s servant.

A fourth: “Accepting Jesus as the Messiah logically requires accepting the Trinity.” The title functioned for centuries in a strictly monotheistic Jewish setting where the anointed one was expected to be human. The Quran confesses Jesus as Messiah, messenger, and word of God, and immediately adds, “Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son” (Quran 4:171).

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Islam’s conclusion begins with agreement. Yes — Jesus, son of Mary, is the Messiah. The Quran says so in the voice of the angels. He is God’s word cast to Mary, honored in this world and the next, strengthened with miracles, sent to the Children of Israel with the Gospel. A Muslim who did not honor the Messiah would not be a Muslim.

But Islam invites Christians and seekers alike to ask what the title meant before later councils defined it. An anointed one is anointed by Another. The kings of Israel were anointed servants of God; the awaited deliverer was expected to be the same; and Jesus at Nazareth read aloud the words “he has anointed me to preach good news to the poor” — a mission received, not a deity disclosed. The Quran’s teaching that “Never would the Messiah disdain to be a servant of Allah” (Quran 4:172) asks to be heard not as a denial of Jesus’s greatness but as its truest description.

To the sincere seeker, Islam offers the shared title with what it holds to be its original content: Jesus the Messiah — the anointed prophet sent to Israel, born of a virgin as a sign to the worlds, who ate food, prayed, and worshipped the One who anointed him. On this understanding, honoring the Messiah and worshipping God alone are the same act of faithfulness that Jesus himself modeled.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus is the Messiah?
A: Yes. The Quran itself names him al-Masih — the Messiah — and no other figure in the Quran bears this title. Affirming it is part of mainstream Islamic belief.

Q: Does the word “Messiah” itself mean “God”?
A: No. It means “anointed one” and was used in the Hebrew Bible for human kings and priests set apart by God. The belief that the Messiah is divine is a later theological claim about Jesus, not the meaning of the word.

Q: Why does the Quran call Jesus “a word from Him”?
A: Muslims understand this as pointing to Jesus’s miraculous origin — created by God’s direct command, without a father. The same verse insists that God is one (Quran 4:171), so “word” is an honor, not a claim that Jesus is an eternal divine person.

Q: Is the “false messiah” in Islamic teaching related to this title?
A: Linguistically, yes. Narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim describe an end-times deceiver called al-Masih ad-Dajjal, “the false messiah,” whom Jesus will confront after his return. That Arabic can attach masih even to a deceiver shows the word is a title, not a statement of divinity.

Q: Do Muslims believe the Messiah will return?
A: Yes. Mainstream Islamic teaching, drawing on the Quran’s allusions and well-known narrations in the major hadith collections, holds that Jesus will return before the end of history. See this library’s article on the second coming of Jesus.

Q: Can someone accept Jesus as the Messiah without accepting the Trinity?
A: Islam answers yes — that is precisely the Muslim position, and it mirrors the original Jewish expectation of a human, divinely appointed Messiah.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
For readers who want to go deeper, the following books are widely available. Tarif Khalidi’s The Muslim Jesus gathers the sayings and stories about Jesus preserved in classical Islamic literature and shows the reverence Muslim civilization has held for him. Mustafa Akyol’s The Islamic Jesus is an accessible modern account of how Jesus is understood in Islam. Geza Vermes’s Jesus the Jew is a classic historical study that places Jesus and his titles — including “Messiah” and “son of God” — within first-century Judaism. The Study Quran, edited by Seyyed Hossein Nasr, provides a full translation with commentary on the verses discussed in this article. For a concise statement of how Christian scholarship reads Jesus, Richard Bauckham’s Jesus: A Very Short Introduction is a helpful starting point.

As always in this library, we encourage readers to read both sides carefully and to weigh the evidence for themselves.

relatedArticles.title: The Crucifixion Question
relatedArticles.subtitle: What happened to Jesus at the end? The Christian account, the historians' verdict, and the Quran's answer
relatedArticles.summary: Did Jesus die on the cross? This is the sharpest single disagreement between Christianity and Islam. Christianity places the crucifixion and resurrection at the very center of its faith, and mainstream historians widely accept that the crucifixion happened. The Quran declares that Jesus was neither killed nor crucified, but that it was made to appear so, and that God raised him to Himself. This article presents all three positions fairly and explains what is really at stake: whether God's forgiveness requires a blood sacrifice, or whether He forgives directly out of His mercy.

sections.title: The Short Answer
sections.body:
Did Jesus die on the cross? On almost no other question do Christianity and Islam disagree so sharply. Christianity teaches that Jesus was crucified, died for humanity's sins, and rose from the dead — and makes this event the center of the faith. The Quran teaches that Jesus was neither killed nor crucified, but that it was made to appear so, and that God raised him to Himself.

Between the two stands a third voice: historians without religious commitments generally accept that the crucifixion happened. An honest comparison cannot ignore this, and this article does not.

What follows presents each position as its own scholars state it, then explains why Muslims hold theirs with confidence — and what is at stake: whether forgiveness requires the blood of an innocent, or flows directly from God's mercy.

sections.title: Introduction: Where Two Faiths Part Ways
sections.body:
Islam and Christianity agree about Jesus to a surprising degree. Both affirm his virgin birth, his miracles, his title of Messiah, and his return before the end of history. A Muslim cannot be a Muslim without honoring Jesus.

Yet the two faiths tell different stories about how his earthly mission ended. For Christians the crucifixion is the heart of everything: it stands at the center of worship and of the doctrine of salvation. For Muslims, the Quran's declaration that Jesus was not killed and not crucified is equally non-negotiable, because it comes as the direct speech of God.

The subject deserves gentleness. For Christians the cross carries their deepest experience of grace; for Muslims, God's rescue of His messenger expresses His justice and power. This article will not mock either conviction. It states each position from its own sources, admits honestly where historical scholarship stands, and then makes the Islamic case openly rather than pretending the disagreement is smaller than it is.

sections.title: What Exactly Is Being Asked?
sections.body:
The crucifixion question is really three questions folded into one.

First, a textual question: what do the scriptures claim? The New Testament: Jesus was crucified, died, was buried, and was raised. The Quran: his enemies neither killed nor crucified him, it was made to appear otherwise, and God raised him to Himself. Neither text is vague about its own claim.

Second, a historical question: what can historians, using ordinary methods of evidence and probability, conclude about what happened outside Jerusalem in the first century? That question has a mainstream answer, stated plainly below.

Third — and here lie the deepest stakes — a theological question: how are human beings forgiven? Christianity's answer runs through the cross, an atoning sacrifice. Islam's answer runs directly from the sinner to God: repentance met by mercy, no blood required. The crucifixion question is finally a question about the character of salvation.

sections.title: The Historical Setting and the Historians' Verdict
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century in Galilee and Judea, then governed as part of the Roman Empire. Crucifixion was a Roman method of public execution — slow, deliberately degrading, used especially against slaves and those accused of rebellion. Its purpose was not only to kill but to warn.

The Gospels devote long narratives to Jesus' arrest, his condemnation under the Roman governor Pontius Pilate, and his crucifixion. The letters of Paul, generally regarded as the earliest surviving Christian documents, already preach the crucifixion and resurrection as settled proclamation, and references to Jesus' execution appear in later non-Christian sources as well.

Here honesty requires a plain statement. In mainstream historical-critical scholarship — including scholars who are neither Christian nor Muslim — the crucifixion is regarded as among the most widely accepted facts about Jesus' life. Historians reason that early Christians were unlikely to invent a shameful execution for the man they proclaimed Messiah, and that multiple early sources report it.

The same honesty requires a second statement. That method treats the resurrection not as an establishable fact but as a faith claim, because the historian's tools weigh probabilities of ordinary events and cannot certify miracles. What the method affirms, and what it cannot see, will both matter when we weigh the Quranic account.

sections.title: What the Quran Says
sections.body:
The Quran addresses the crucifixion directly in Surah An-Nisaa, rebuking those who boasted of having killed Jesus. Quran 4:157 reads:

وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا ٱلْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ لَفِى شَكٍّۢ مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا ٱتِّبَاعَ ٱلظَّنِّ ۚ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًۢا

"And [for] their saying, "Indeed, we have killed the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, the messenger of Allah." And they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them. And indeed, those who differ over it are in doubt about it. They have no knowledge of it except the following of assumption. And they did not kill him, for certain." (Quran 4:157, Saheeh International)

Notice what the verse does and does not deny. It does not deny that an event took place or that onlookers believed they had crucified Jesus. It denies that they killed him and that they crucified him, explaining the gap in one phrase: shubbiha lahum — it was made to appear so to them. It even asserts that those involved were confused: those who differ over it, it says, are in doubt about it.

The next verse states what actually happened. Quran 4:158:

بَل رَّفَعَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًۭا

"Rather, Allah raised him to Himself. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise." (Quran 4:158, Saheeh International)

How was it made to appear so? The Quran does not say, and beginners should know that everything beyond this point is interpretation, not scripture. Many classical commentators understood shubbiha lahum as substitution: another person, made to resemble Jesus, was crucified in his place. Others placed the weight on appearance itself: the crowd, the chaos, and God's intervention left observers certain of something that had not happened. The identities proposed for a substitute come from commentary literature of varying reliability, not from the Quran, and no version is required belief.

A related verse, Quran 3:55, records God's promise to Jesus:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

"[Mention] when Allah said, "O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve and make those who follow you [in submission to Allah alone] superior to those who disbelieve until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ." (Quran 3:55, Saheeh International)

The phrase rendered "I will take you" (mutawaffika) has been read in more than one way: the classical majority held that God took Jesus up alive, his death deferred until his return, while a minority read it as a peaceful death before the raising. On either reading, the decisive point stands — Jesus did not die on a cross at his enemies' hands.

Finally, Quran 4:159 points forward:

وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِۦ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا

"And there is none from the People of the Scripture but that he will surely believe in Jesus before his death. And on the Day of Resurrection he will be against them a witness." (Quran 4:159, Saheeh International)

Most commentators connect this to Jesus' return at the end of history, and narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim describe Jesus, son of Mary, descending near the end of time — a hope Muslims share, in their own form, with Christians.

sections.title: What the New Testament Says
sections.body:
Fairness requires letting Christianity speak in its strongest voice, and that voice is Paul's. Writing to the church in Corinth, he summarizes the message he says he received:

"For I delivered to you first of all that which I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures," (1 Corinthians 15:3-4, World English Bible)

This compact formula — died for our sins, buried, raised — is widely regarded by Christian scholars as one of the earliest summaries of Christian preaching. In the same chapter Paul argues that if Christ was not raised, the faith itself is emptied. The crucifixion and resurrection are not one doctrine among many; they are the load-bearing wall of the whole structure.

The Gospels narrate the event with stark brevity:

"It was the third hour, and they crucified him." (Mark 15:25, World English Bible)

Readers have long noticed that John's Gospel appears to place the final sentencing at a later hour, a detail Christian interpreters and critical scholars discuss in different ways; but all four Gospels treat the crucifixion as the climax of the story.

Two sayings from these narratives deserve special attention. The first is the cry from the cross:

"About the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice, saying, “Eli, Eli, lima sabachthani?” That is, “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”" (Matthew 27:46, World English Bible)

Christian interpreters note that these words open Psalm 22 and read the cry as Jesus bearing the weight of human sin while trusting the psalm's arc toward vindication. Muslim readers ask a different question: how could one who is himself God cry out that God has forsaken him? To Muslims the cry fits a beloved prophet in extremity far more naturally than an incarnate deity.

The second comes from Gethsemane, the night before:

"He said, “Abba, Father, all things are possible to you. Please remove this cup from me. However, not what I desire, but what you desire.”" (Mark 14:36, World English Bible)

Christian theology reads this as the human will of Jesus submitting perfectly to the divine plan. Muslims read it as something simpler: a righteous prophet, in anguish, begging his Lord — for whom all things are possible — to be spared. And the Quran's answer in 4:158, Muslims note, is that this prayer was heard: God raised him to Himself.

sections.title: Three Positions, Weighed Honestly
sections.body:
Set the three positions side by side. Christianity: Jesus was crucified, died for sins, and rose — the center of the faith. Historical scholarship: the crucifixion is the most securely attested event of Jesus' life, but the resurrection lies beyond what the method can affirm. Islam: Jesus was neither killed nor crucified; it was made to appear so; God raised him alive.

Each position rests on a different authority: apostolic testimony, probabilistic reasoning over sources, and the Quran received as God's direct speech. That is why the debate cannot be settled simply by announcing the historians' verdict.

Consider what historical method can see. Its conclusion follows from weighing sources under a working rule that excludes miracles from explanation. But the Quranic claim is precisely a claim of divine intervention in what observers perceived. A miracle of appearance is, by definition, invisible to a method that assumes appearances were normal — and every chain of testimony runs back through the very eyewitnesses the Quran says were deceived and confused. A Muslim can grant everything the method genuinely shows — an execution event, communities convinced Jesus had died — while denying the one link it cannot secure: that the man on the cross was Jesus.

Honesty requires admitting the argument cuts both ways. The Muslim accepts a miracle on the authority of revelation, which the secular historian will not. But the Christian does the same at the decisive point, since the resurrection — the actual center of the faith — is likewise held on revelation and testimony, not neutral history. At the moment that matters most, both faiths ask for trust in a revelation; the real question is which revelation deserves it, which is why the transmission of the Quran and of the Bible are examined elsewhere in this library. And beneath the historical question lies the theological one: if Jesus did not die for sins, forgiveness must come — as Islam says it does — from God's own direct mercy.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
First: the Quran does not deny that Jesus existed or that a dramatic public event took place. It denies that Jesus was the one killed and crucified, and states that observers were made to perceive otherwise. The position is not that nothing happened, but that what happened is not what they thought.

Second: it is often said that Muslims believe Judas was crucified instead. No name appears in the Quran. Proposed identities for a substitute come from later commentary of uneven reliability, and none is an article of faith. The Quran leaves the mechanism with God.

Third: the denial is not hostility toward Christianity. The Quran affirms Jesus' virgin birth, miracles, messiahship, and return. Muslims understand the denial of the crucifixion as God's vindication of Jesus — a rescue, not a rejection.

Fourth, a misconception in the other direction: that Christians believe God simply died on the cross. Mainstream Christian theology is more careful: it holds that the divine nature cannot die, and that Christ died in his human nature. Muslims find the two-natures framework untenable — that critique belongs to the article on the Trinity — but fairness requires stating the doctrine as Christians hold it.

Fifth: some academic writers compare Quran 4:157 to early sects called docetists, who taught that Christ only seemed to suffer because he was too divine to have a real body. The comparison fails at the crucial point: the Quran insists Jesus was fully human — born of a mother, eating food, praying. Its claim is not that Jesus had no real body, but that God protected His human messenger from that death. Muslims receive the verse as revelation correcting the record, not as an echo of anyone's speculation.

sections.title: The Islamic Case: Rescue, Not Sacrifice
sections.body:
The Islamic position is not an evasion of history; it is a claim about God, resting on three convictions.

The first is God's justice. Guilt is not transferable: no innocent can justly be punished so that the guilty go free. The Quran states the principle in Surah Faatir:

وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَإِن تَدْعُ مُثْقَلَةٌ إِلَىٰ حِمْلِهَا لَا يُحْمَلْ مِنْهُ شَىْءٌۭ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰٓ ۗ إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ وَمَن تَزَكَّىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَتَزَكَّىٰ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

"And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And if a heavily laden soul calls [another] to [carry some of] its load, nothing of it will be carried, even if he should be a close relative. You can only warn those who fear their Lord unseen and have established prayer. And whoever purifies himself only purifies himself for [the benefit of] his soul. And to Allah is the [final] destination." (Quran 35:18, Saheeh International)

If no soul bears another's burden, salvation cannot run through the execution of an innocent man, even a willing one. It must run through each soul's own turning to God.

The second is God's mercy. Christianity reasons that sin against an infinite God demands an infinite payment that only the cross could supply. Islam replies that forgiveness is not a debt to be settled but a gift to be granted: the Lord who commands His servants to forgive freely does not need blood before doing the same. The sinner repents, and God forgives directly — nothing stands between the servant and the Master. This is explored fully in the article on forgiveness in Islam and Christianity.

The third is God's faithfulness to His messengers. Across the Quran, God rescues His prophets as their enemies close in; read in that light, the ending of Jesus' story is the pattern's most glorious case. The prophet who prayed "all things are possible to you. Please remove this cup from me" was answered by the God for whom all things are possible: "Rather, Allah raised him to Himself. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise." (Quran 4:158). Where the cross would say God's beloved was forsaken, the Quran says he was honored and taken up — and, per Quran 4:159, his story is not finished.

A Muslim therefore faces this question without anxiety. The historical debate is real, and this article has stated it honestly. But where every party must finally trust a testimony, the Muslim trusts the speech of God, and finds in it a conclusion worthy of both God's justice and His mercy: no scapegoat, no forsaken prophet, no debt collected in blood — a rescue, a raising, and a door of forgiveness open to every soul that knocks.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus was crucified?
A: No. The Quran states that Jesus was neither killed nor crucified but that it was made to appear so (4:157), and that God raised him to Himself (4:158). Muslims receive this as God's direct testimony.

Q: If it was not Jesus on the cross, who was it?
A: The Quran does not say. Many classical commentators spoke of a substitute made to resemble Jesus, but no identification is part of the Quranic text or required belief. Muslims are content to leave the mechanism with God.

Q: Don't historians agree the crucifixion happened?
A: Mainstream scholarship does regard it as among the best-attested facts of Jesus' life, and Muslims should acknowledge that honestly. But historical method excludes divine intervention by design, while the Quran's claim is precisely that God intervened in what observers perceived. The same method also declines to affirm the resurrection — so both faiths ultimately rest on revelation at the decisive point.

Q: Without the cross, how are sins forgiven in Islam?
A: Directly. The sinner repents sincerely and God forgives out of His mercy — no intermediary, no sacrifice, no transferred guilt, for no soul bears the burden of another (Quran 35:18).

Q: Is Jesus alive now, according to Islam?
A: The majority view is that God raised him alive and that he will return before the Day of Resurrection — a hope described in narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim, and connected by most commentators to Quran 4:159.

Q: Does denying the crucifixion dishonor Jesus?
A: The opposite. In the Islamic account the denial is God's rescue and vindication of a beloved messenger, whom Muslims may never mention except with reverence.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below cover the ground from several angles: a scholarly history of Muslim interpretation of Quran 4:157; comparative studies of Jesus in the Quran and in Christian doctrine; the image of Jesus in the wider Islamic tradition; and a reconstruction of Jesus' life by a prominent non-Christian historian, useful for seeing how the secular case is actually argued.

As with every article in this library, the list exists so you can check the claims for yourself. Read the strongest version of each position, and weigh them with the seriousness the question deserves.

relatedArticles.title: What Is Tawhid?
relatedArticles.subtitle: The Oneness of God: Islam's Foundational Doctrine and the Message of Every Prophet
relatedArticles.summary: Tawhid is the Islamic doctrine of the oneness of God: He alone is Lord, He alone deserves worship, and nothing is like Him. Muslims believe it was the message of every prophet, from Noah and Abraham to Moses and Jesus. This article defines Tawhid and its opposite, shirk, in plain language for beginners. It then quotes the Quran and the Bible side by side, including the Shema of Moses and the words of Jesus in the Gospel of Mark.

sections.title: In Plain Words
sections.body:
Tawhid (pronounced taw-HEED) is the Arabic word for the oneness of God, and it is the single most important teaching in Islam. It means that there is only one God, that He alone has the right to be worshipped, that He alone is the Lord and Sustainer of everything, and that nothing in creation is like Him.

Muslims believe Tawhid was not invented in the seventh century. They believe it was the one message carried by every prophet God ever sent, from Noah and Abraham to Moses, Jesus, and finally Muhammad, peace be upon them all.

This article explains what Tawhid means in plain language, introduces its opposite, called shirk, and then lets the texts speak for themselves: first the Quran, and then the Bible, where the same theme of God's exclusive oneness appears in the words of Moses, Isaiah, and Jesus himself.

sections.title: Introduction: The Center of Islam
sections.body:
Every religion has a center of gravity. In Islam, that center is not a ritual, a place, or even a prophet. It is a statement about God: la ilaha illa Allah, "there is no deity worthy of worship except God." The doctrine behind that statement is called Tawhid, from an Arabic root meaning "to make one" or "to affirm as one."

Muslim scholars have traditionally unpacked Tawhid into three connected aspects, and learning them makes everything else in Islam easier to understand. The first is the oneness of God's lordship (in Arabic, tawhid al-rububiyyah): God alone creates, sustains, gives life, and governs the universe, with no helper, rival, or committee. The second is the oneness of worship (tawhid al-uluhiyyah): because God alone is Lord, He alone deserves prayer, devotion, sacrifice, and ultimate love. Worship, in other words, is God's exclusive right. The third is the oneness of His names and attributes (tawhid al-asma wa-l-sifat): God is described by the most beautiful names, such as the Most Merciful and the All-Knowing, and His perfection is His alone; nothing in creation resembles Him.

These three aspects are teaching categories, not three separate doctrines. In the Quran they are woven together into a single, seamless picture of one God who made you, who sustains you, and who asks for your heart undivided.

sections.title: The Question: What Does "One God" Really Mean?
sections.body:
The question this article answers is simple to ask and profound to think through: what exactly does Islam mean when it says God is one, and was that oneness the original message of all the prophets, including Moses and Jesus?

"One" here is not merely a number, as if the only issue were counting. Tawhid means God is one in an exclusive and absolute sense: no partners who share His power, no intermediaries who receive a portion of worship, no offspring who inherit His nature, and no image or likeness that captures Him. It is oneness of being, oneness of authority, and oneness of the right to be worshipped, all at once.

Tawhid also has an opposite, and the Quran treats it with the utmost seriousness. It is called shirk, which means associating partners with God: directing any act of worship to something other than Him, believing that anything shares His lordship, or giving His unique attributes to a created being. Shirk can be as obvious as bowing to a statue or as subtle as making wealth, power, or one's own desires the true object of devotion. In Islam it is the gravest of all sins, because it gives away the one thing that belongs to God alone.

sections.title: One Message, Many Prophets
sections.body:
The Quran does not present Islam as a new religion founded in seventh-century Arabia. It presents Islam as the restoration of the original faith of humanity: surrender to the one God. In the Quran's telling, history is a long chain of prophets, each sent to his own people, each carrying the same core message of Tawhid, and each followed, in time, by communities that drifted from it.

The Hebrew Bible preserves that same core message in some of its most famous lines. The declaration known as the Shema, from the Book of Deuteronomy, is the closest thing Judaism has to a creed; observant Jews have recited it in daily prayer for many centuries, and it declares in a single breath that God is one. The Ten Commandments open not with a rule about honesty or family, but with God's exclusive claim to worship. The prophet Isaiah repeats the theme with striking force: there is no god besides the one God.

The Gospels then record something remarkable for this discussion. When Jesus was asked which commandment is the greatest of all, he did not announce a new doctrine of God. He recited the Shema of Moses. Centuries after Jesus, church councils formulated the doctrine of the Trinity, the teaching that the one God exists as three persons; Christians regard this as the correct interpretation of their scriptures. Islam, arriving after those debates, called people back to what it presents as the older, simpler creed: the unqualified oneness proclaimed by every prophet. The sections below quote the texts on which that claim rests.

sections.title: Tawhid in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's most concentrated statement of Tawhid is its 112th chapter, Surah al-Ikhlas, "Purity of Faith." It is only four verses long, and Muslim tradition holds that it summarizes the Quran's entire teaching about who God is. Its first verse reads (the Arabic below opens with the basmala, "In the name of God, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful," the phrase with which the chapter begins):

بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ

"Say, "He is Allah, [who is] One," (Quran 112:1)

ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ

"Allah, the Eternal Refuge." (Quran 112:2)

The word translated "Eternal Refuge" is al-Samad: the One on whom all creation depends, while He depends on nothing and no one. Then come two verses that draw the boundary lines of Tawhid with complete precision:

لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ

"He neither begets nor is born," (Quran 112:3)

وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ

"Nor is there to Him any equivalent."" (Quran 112:4)

God has no parents and no offspring, no origin and no heirs, and nothing anywhere is comparable to Him. In four short verses, the chapter rules out idolatry, polytheism, and any doctrine that gives God a son or an equal.

Is this oneness unique to the Quran's own message? The Quran says no. It insists that Tawhid was the message of every messenger God ever sent:

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدُونِ

"And We sent not before you any messenger except that We revealed to him that, "There is no deity except Me, so worship Me."" (Quran 21:25)

وَلَقَدْ بَعَثْنَا فِى كُلِّ أُمَّةٍۢ رَّسُولًا أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلطَّٰغُوتَ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ ٱلضَّلَٰلَةُ ۚ فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ

"And We certainly sent into every nation a messenger, [saying], "Worship Allah and avoid Taghut." And among them were those whom Allah guided, and among them were those upon whom error was [deservedly] decreed. So proceed through the earth and observe how was the end of the deniers." (Quran 16:36)

The word Taghut in this verse means false objects of worship: anything served or deified in place of God. According to these verses, no nation on earth was left without a messenger, and every messenger preached the same two-part call that defines Tawhid: worship God, and renounce whatever is worshipped besides Him.

The Quran is equally careful about how we may describe God. He is utterly unlike His creation, yet He is not an abstraction; He hears and sees:

فَاطِرُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًۭا وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَٰمِ أَزْوَٰجًۭا ۖ يَذْرَؤُكُمْ فِيهِ ۚ لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِۦ شَىْءٌۭ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ

"[He is] Creator of the heavens and the earth. He has made for you from yourselves, mates, and among the cattle, mates; He multiplies you thereby. There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the Hearing, the Seeing." (Quran 42:11)

That single verse contains the balance Muslim theology strives to keep: nothing is like God, and yet He is genuinely knowable through the names and attributes He reveals about Himself. The Quran calls these the most beautiful names:

ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ

"Allah - there is no deity except Him. To Him belong the best names." (Quran 20:8)

One passage near the end of Surah al-Hashr gathers many of these names into a crescendo that Muslims often recite:

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, Knower of the unseen and the witnessed. He is the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful." (Quran 59:22)

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

"He is Allah, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller, the Superior. Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him." (Quran 59:23)

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

"He is Allah, the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner; to Him belong the best names. Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." (Quran 59:24)

Notice what these verses accomplish together. They keep God transcendent, exalted above whatever is associated with Him, and at the same time intimately describable: merciful, sovereign, pure, creating, fashioning, wise. Tawhid is not the worship of a distant force; it is a personal relationship with the one Lord who has told us who He is.

Finally, the Quran states the seriousness of Tawhid's opposite in the plainest possible terms:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱفْتَرَىٰٓ إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا

"Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills. And he who associates others with Allah has certainly fabricated a tremendous sin." (Quran 4:48)

Shirk, the association of partners with God, is the one sin the Quran singles out this way, because it violates the very purpose for which, in Islam, human beings exist. The door of repentance remains open in this life to anyone who turns back to God; the warning concerns those who die still insisting on it.

sections.title: The Oneness of God in the Bible
sections.body:
A reader who opens the Bible looking for the theme of God's oneness does not have to search long. The Torah, the Prophets, and the Gospels each contain declarations that Muslims immediately recognize as the language of Tawhid. All quotations below are from the World English Bible translation.

The most famous is the Shema, the verse at the heart of Jewish faith and prayer: "Hear, Israel: Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one." (Deuteronomy 6:4). One God, stated without qualification or elaboration. This is the creed Moses delivered to Israel.

The Ten Commandments begin the same way, with God's exclusive claim on worship: "“I am Yahweh your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage. “You shall have no other gods before me." (Exodus 20:2-3). Before any command about human conduct comes the command that defines all the others: no other gods. Worship belongs to God alone, which is precisely the second aspect of Tawhid described earlier.

The prophet Isaiah presses the point to its logical end, ruling out not just the worship of other gods but their very existence: "I am Yahweh, and there is no one else. Besides me, there is no God. I will strengthen you, though you have not known me;" (Isaiah 45:5).

Then comes the passage that matters most for a conversation between Muslims and Christians. In the Gospel of Mark, a scribe asks Jesus which commandment is the greatest of all. Mark records: "Jesus answered, “The greatest is, ‘Hear, Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one:" (Mark 12:29). The verse continues into the command to love God with all one's heart, soul, mind, and strength. Asked to name the very core of faith, Jesus answered with the Shema of Moses: the Lord our God, the Lord is one.

Christians, of course, hold all of these verses sacred and read them in harmony with the doctrine of the Trinity, which they understand as monotheism: one God in three persons, not three gods. That reading deserves to be stated fairly, and it is examined on its own terms elsewhere in this library. The observation made here is more modest: the verses themselves, as they stand, proclaim God's oneness in simple, exclusive, unqualified terms, and that is exactly what Islam means by Tawhid.

sections.title: Side by Side: Tawhid and the Trinity
sections.body:
It helps to state each position honestly and side by side.

Islam holds that God is one in an absolute and undifferentiated sense: one divine being, with no distinction of persons within Him, no incarnation in human flesh, and no partner, offspring, or equal. Worship of anyone or anything besides Him, including any prophet or saint, is shirk. On this view, Jesus was a mighty messenger of God who taught the same creed as Moses, and devotion belongs to the God Jesus worshipped.

Mainstream Christianity holds that God is one being in three co-equal, co-eternal persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Christians insist, and have always insisted, that this is monotheism, not the worship of three gods; the Trinity is presented as a deeper description of the one God's inner life, with the Son becoming incarnate in Jesus.

The texts quoted in this article say: "Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is one"; "You shall have no other gods before me"; "I am Yahweh, and there is no one else"; and, from the lips of Jesus, "the Lord our God, the Lord is one." On the Quran's side: "He is Allah, [who is] One"; "He neither begets nor is born, nor is there to Him any equivalent"; "There is no deity except Me, so worship Me."

The Islamic argument is straightforward. Read on their own, these texts express oneness that needs no further doctrinal machinery: no persons to distinguish, no natures to unite, no councils required to settle the definition. A Muslim can affirm every word of the Shema, the first commandment, Isaiah's declaration, and Jesus's answer to the scribe, exactly as written. Tawhid, Muslims argue, is not a rival to the message of the Bible's prophets; it is that message, preserved in its original simplicity.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few misunderstandings come up so often that they deserve direct answers.

First: "Allah is a different god from the God of the Bible." Allah is simply the Arabic word for God, related to the Hebrew Elohim and the Aramaic Alaha. Arabic-speaking Christians and Jews use the word Allah for God in their own prayers and Bibles. When Muslims say Allah, they mean the God of Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus: the Creator of the heavens and the earth.

Second: "A God so transcendent must be cold and distant." The same Quran that says nothing is like God also names Him the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful, the Hearing, the Seeing. Tawhid removes intermediaries precisely so that every person can turn to God directly, with no priesthood or broker in between.

Third: "Muslims accuse Christians of worshipping three gods." That is not the careful Islamic position, and this library does not argue that way. Christian doctrine explicitly affirms one God, and honesty requires saying so. The Islamic critique is different: that the Trinity, however sincerely intended as monotheism, goes beyond the simple oneness that the quoted texts proclaim, and that the safer path is to stay with what Moses and Jesus actually said.

Fourth: "Shirk just means bowing to idols." Idolatry is its clearest form, but shirk includes any act of worship directed to other than God: praying to saints, seeking from the dead what only God can give, or making money, status, or desire one's functional god.

Fifth: "Muslims worship the Kaaba in Mecca." Muslims pray toward the Kaaba as a single direction that unites worshippers worldwide, the way the Bible describes prophets praying toward Jerusalem. No Muslim worships the building; worship is for God alone.

sections.title: Conclusion: One God, One Message
sections.body:
Tawhid is Islam's answer to the oldest and most important question a human being can ask: who deserves my worship? The answer is the One who made you, sustains you every second, and will one day call you back: God alone, without partner, without son, without equal.

What gives that answer its force is its consistency. The Quran proclaims it: there is no deity except Him. The Torah proclaims it: the Lord is one; you shall have no other gods. The prophets proclaim it: besides Me there is no God. And when Jesus himself was asked to name the greatest commandment, he reached for the same creed. Islam asks the reader to take that pattern seriously. Either the prophets shared one message, or they did not; the texts, quoted throughout this article, speak with one voice.

For the Muslim, Tawhid is not an abstract doctrine but a way of standing in the world: one God to please rather than a thousand masters to appease, one door to knock on, one judge whose verdict matters. It is, Muslims believe, the faith of Abraham in its purest form, open to anyone willing to say with conviction that there is no deity worthy of worship except God. The invitation of this entire library begins here, because in Islam everything else follows from this.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: What does the word Tawhid literally mean?
A: Tawhid comes from an Arabic root meaning "to make one" or "to affirm as one." As a doctrine, it means affirming that God is one in His lordship, one in His exclusive right to worship, and unique in His names and attributes.

Q: Is Allah the same God that Jews and Christians worship?
A: Allah is simply the Arabic word for God, and Arabic-speaking Jews and Christians use it themselves. Muslims understand Allah to be the God of Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. The three faiths describe God differently in doctrine, but the reference is to the Creator worshipped by the biblical prophets.

Q: What is shirk, and why is it so serious in Islam?
A: Shirk means associating partners with God: giving worship, ultimate devotion, or God's unique attributes to anything created. The Quran calls it the one sin God does not forgive for a person who dies upon it without repenting, because it gives away the sole right for which humans were created. Sincere repentance in this life wipes it away.

Q: Did Jesus teach the oneness of God?
A: According to the Gospel of Mark, when Jesus was asked which commandment is the greatest, he recited the Shema: "Hear, Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one." Muslims see in this the same creed of Tawhid taught by Moses and every prophet, while Christians read it in harmony with later Trinitarian doctrine.

Q: Do Muslims believe Christians worship three gods?
A: No. Christian doctrine explicitly teaches one God in three persons, and fairness requires representing it that way. The Islamic critique is not that Christians intend polytheism, but that the Trinity adds distinctions to God's oneness that the plain declarations of the Torah, the Prophets, and Jesus do not contain.

Q: What are the three categories of Tawhid?
A: Scholars traditionally teach Tawhid in three aspects: oneness of lordship (God alone creates and sustains), oneness of worship (God alone deserves devotion), and oneness of names and attributes (God's perfection is unique and incomparable). They are three angles on a single truth, not three separate doctrines.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following well-known works are offered as further reading for readers who want to go deeper. They are listed for orientation, not as sources for specific claims made in this article.

Abu Ameenah Bilal Philips — The Fundamentals of Tawheed: a widely read English-language introduction to the categories of Tawhid and the forms of shirk.

Imam Abu Ja'far al-Tahawi — The Creed of Imam al-Tahawi (al-Aqidah al-Tahawiyyah): a classical summary of Muslim beliefs about God, accepted across the major Sunni schools.

Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab — Kitab al-Tawhid: a classical thematic collection on the oneness of worship and warnings against shirk.

Hamza Andreas Tzortzis — The Divine Reality: a contemporary case for God's existence and oneness addressed to modern readers, including atheists and agnostics.

relatedArticles.title: Who Follows Jesus More Closely?
relatedArticles.subtitle: The recorded habits of Jesus — prostration, fasting, the greeting of peace — and where they live on today
relatedArticles.summary: The Gospels record Jesus praying with his face to the ground, fasting, giving secret charity, greeting with peace, keeping the food laws, and teaching that only those who do God's will truly follow him. Muslims today keep strikingly similar practices as daily obligations, while mainstream Christianity understands following Jesus as faith in his person rather than replication of his customs. This article quotes the Quran and the Bible verbatim on each practice, presents the Christian understanding fairly, and then makes the Islamic case from the texts themselves.

sections.title: In a Nutshell
sections.body:
Open the Gospels and watch how Jesus lived from day to day. He fell with his face to the ground when he prayed. He fasted, and assumed his followers would fast. He greeted his disciples with "Peace be to you." He taught secret giving to the poor, and warned that calling him "Lord" means nothing without doing the will of God.

A practicing Muslim today prostrates with the face to the ground in the five daily prayers, fasts a month every year, greets with "peace be upon you" (as-salamu alaykum), gives a fixed share of wealth to the poor, avoids pork, and says "God willing" (insha'Allah) about tomorrow's plans.

Mainstream Christianity does not deny these habits of Jesus; it teaches that following him means faith in his person, not replication of his customs. This article quotes the texts on both sides, presents the Christian understanding fairly, and then makes the Islamic case.

sections.title: Why Ask This Question?
sections.body:
First, a word about tone. This article is not an attempt to score points against sincere Christians, hundreds of millions of whom love Jesus deeply and order their lives around him. Nothing here mocks that devotion.

Every community that honors Jesus claims to follow him. Rather than argue over the word, this article looks at something checkable: the specific, recorded habits of Jesus in the Gospels, set beside the daily religious lives of Christians and Muslims today. Each faith's position is presented as its own scholarship states it, and every quotation is verbatim — the Quran in the Saheeh International translation, the Bible in the World English Bible.

sections.title: What Does It Mean to Follow Someone?
sections.body:
"Following" a person can mean two things: trusting him — believing what he says and giving him your loyalty — or walking the way he walked: praying as he prayed, fasting as he fasted, greeting as he greeted, eating as he ate.

Mainstream Christianity centers on the first meaning: faith in Jesus as Lord and Savior, and growth into his character. His outward customs, on this view, belonged to the covenant era he came to complete. Islam affirms the second meaning as well, while differing sharply about who Jesus is: Muslims honor him as one of God's greatest messengers — not as God — and find that their daily practice matches his recorded practice to a striking degree. So the question becomes precise: line up the recorded habits of Jesus beside the everyday lives of the two communities, and see.

sections.title: The World Jesus Lived In
sections.body:
Jesus was born, lived, and taught as a Jew, among Jews, in a Jewish homeland under Roman rule. On this, Christian, Jewish, and secular historians agree. The Gospels show him attending synagogue, keeping the festivals of his people, and quoting the Law of Moses and the Hebrew prophets as scripture.

The piety of that world had a recognizable shape: bowing and falling on one's face before God, as the Hebrew Bible repeatedly describes prophets doing; fasting; almsgiving as a core duty; the Torah's food laws, which excluded pork outright; the everyday greeting of peace, shalom; and speech that placed future plans under God's will.

The Gospels never show Jesus stepping outside this pattern: he is never recorded eating what the Torah forbids or abandoning the prayer and fasting of his people. The question is what happened afterward. Christianity came to teach that the ceremonial law was fulfilled in Christ and no longer binding, while the practices themselves continue as daily obligations in the worship of Muslims. The rest of this article documents both halves of that sentence from the sources.

sections.title: The Quran on Jesus and His Followers
sections.body:
The Quran speaks of Jesus — Isa in Arabic — with love and reverence, and holds up his first disciples as a model Muslims are told to imitate:

يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوٓا۟ أَنصَارَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَا قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ لِلْحَوَارِيِّۦنَ مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَـَٔامَنَت طَّآئِفَةٌۭ مِّنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَكَفَرَت طَّآئِفَةٌۭ ۖ فَأَيَّدْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ عَلَىٰ عَدُوِّهِمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ ظَٰهِرِينَ

"O you who have believed, be supporters of Allah, as when Jesus, the son of Mary, said to the disciples, "Who are my supporters for Allah?" The disciples said, "We are supporters of Allah." And a faction of the Children of Israel believed and a faction disbelieved. So We supported those who believed against their enemy, and they became dominant." (Quran 61:14)

The "disciples" translates al-hawariyyun, the Quran's name for Jesus's close companions. An earlier surah records the same scene with the disciples' own description of themselves:

۞ فَلَمَّآ أَحَسَّ عِيسَىٰ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ قَالَ مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ ٱللَّهِ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ

"But when Jesus felt [persistence in] disbelief from them, he said, "Who are my supporters for [the cause of] Allah?" The disciples said, "We are supporters for Allah. We have believed in Allah and testify that we are Muslims [submitting to Him]." (Quran 3:52)

The word translated "Muslims" is muslimun, Arabic for "those who submit" to God — a description before it is anyone's proper name, and the Quran applies it to the followers of Jesus. A third passage repeats it in their own voice:

وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى ٱلْحَوَارِيِّۦنَ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِى وَبِرَسُولِى قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّنَا مُسْلِمُونَ

"And [remember] when I inspired to the disciples, "Believe in Me and in My messenger Jesus." They said, "We have believed, so bear witness that indeed we are Muslims [in submission to Allah]."" (Quran 5:111)

And what did the way of Jesus involve? In Surah Maryam, the infant Jesus speaks miraculously from the cradle:

وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّۭا

"And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive" (Quran 19:31)

Prayer (salah) and almsgiving (zakah) are exactly what the Quranic Jesus says God enjoined on him for life. The Quran also ties love of God to following His messenger:

قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱللَّهَ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى يُحْبِبْكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌۭ رَّحِيمٌۭ

"Say, [O Muhammad], "If you should love Allah, then follow me, [so] Allah will love you and forgive you your sins. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."" (Quran 3:31)

Even the small habit of saying "God willing" is commanded:

وَلَا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَا۟ىْءٍ إِنِّى فَاعِلٌۭ ذَٰلِكَ غَدًا

"And never say of anything, "Indeed, I will do that tomorrow,"" (Quran 18:23)

إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ وَقُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّى لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هَٰذَا رَشَدًۭا

"Except [when adding], "If Allah wills." And remember your Lord when you forget [it] and say, "Perhaps my Lord will guide me to what is nearer than this to right conduct."" (Quran 18:24)

This is why a Muslim says insha'Allah before speaking of any plan. Keep the phrase in mind; it reappears, almost word for word, in the New Testament below.

sections.title: The Gospel Record of Jesus's Practice
sections.body:
Now the Gospel record, quoted from the World English Bible. Begin in Gethsemane, at the most intense moment of prayer the Gospels describe:

"He went forward a little, fell on his face, and prayed, saying, “My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass away from me; nevertheless, not what I desire, but what you desire.”" (Matthew 26:39, WEB)

He "fell on his face, and prayed." Anyone who has watched Muslim prayer will recognize the posture: the sujud, the prostration that anchors every cycle of the five daily prayers.

In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus takes it for granted that his followers fast; his instruction is only about sincerity:

"“Moreover when you fast, don’t be like the hypocrites, with sad faces. For they disfigure their faces, that they may be seen by men to be fasting. Most certainly I tell you, they have received their reward. But you, when you fast, anoint your head, and wash your face;" (Matthew 6:16-17, WEB)

"When you fast" — not "if." Moments earlier he gives charity the same treatment:

"But when you do merciful deeds, don’t let your left hand know what your right hand does, so that your merciful deeds may be in secret, then your Father who sees in secret will reward you openly." (Matthew 6:3-4, WEB)

After the resurrection appearances begin, John records Jesus's greeting to his gathered disciples:

"When therefore it was evening, on that day, the first day of the week, and when the doors were locked where the disciples were assembled, for fear of the Jews, Jesus came and stood in the middle, and said to them, “Peace be to you.”" (John 20:19, WEB)

"Peace be to you" was the ordinary greeting of Jesus's world — and is the Muslim world's today: as-salamu alaykum. On the Law of Moses that shaped these habits, the Sermon on the Mount reports him saying:

"“Don’t think that I came to destroy the law or the prophets. I didn’t come to destroy, but to fulfill." (Matthew 5:17, WEB)

Jesus also states his own criterion for real following, preserved in two Gospels:

"Not everyone who says to me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter into the Kingdom of Heaven; but he who does the will of my Father who is in heaven." (Matthew 7:21, WEB)

"“Why do you call me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ and don’t do the things which I say?" (Luke 6:46, WEB)

By his own test, following is a matter of doing. Finally, the Letter of James — attributed by long Christian tradition to the brother of the Lord — teaches the very speech habit the Quran commands:

"For you ought to say, “If the Lord wills, we will both live, and do this or that.”" (James 4:15, WEB)

"If the Lord wills": in Arabic, insha'Allah.

sections.title: Practice by Practice
sections.body:
Set the two columns side by side, practice by practice.

Prayer posture. Jesus "fell on his face, and prayed" (Matthew 26:39). Muslims prostrate with the face to the ground in every prayer; in most Christian worship the posture is standing, sitting, or kneeling upright, with full prostration surviving only in rare moments such as ordination rites.

Fasting. Jesus fasted and said "when you fast" (Matthew 6:16-17). Muslims fast every day of Ramadan as a pillar of the faith. In fairness, fasting never disappeared from Christianity: Orthodox fasting seasons and Catholic Lent remain, though in much modern Protestant practice it is optional.

The greeting of peace. Jesus greeted his disciples with "Peace be to you" (John 20:19). As-salamu alaykum is the standard Islamic greeting, exchanged countless times a day; among Christians the greeting of peace survives chiefly inside the liturgy.

Almsgiving. Jesus commanded secret, sincere giving (Matthew 6:3-4). Islam makes almsgiving a pillar: zakah, a fixed yearly share of wealth owed to the poor. Christian generosity is real and often remarkable; the difference is that Islam retains giving as a precisely defined obligation.

Modesty and dress. Christian art has always depicted the world of Jesus and his mother Mary in flowing garments, with Mary's head covered. Islamic norms of modest dress, including the headscarf, sit inside that same older pattern.

Food. The Torah forbids pork, and the Gospels never show Jesus eating it. Muslims still abstain; most Christians eat it, understanding the food laws to have been set aside after Jesus's ministry.

Speech. James 4:15 counsels "If the Lord wills"; the Quran commands the same (18:23-24). Muslims say insha'Allah as a reflex; most Christians' daily speech has no fixed equivalent.

The claim, stated carefully, is not that Christians do none of these things. It is that in ordinary Muslim life the recorded habits of Jesus persist in close to their original form, as daily obligations rather than seasonal or liturgical echoes.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
Misconception one: "Christians simply ignore Jesus's example." False and unfair. The imitation of Christ is one of the richest themes in Christian history — Thomas a Kempis's classic by that very name has been read for centuries. The mainstream position is not indifference; it is a theological judgment that what must be imitated is Jesus's character, while his ceremonial practices belonged to the Law he fulfilled. Christians read passages such as Mark 7, Acts 10, and the letters of Paul as lifting the old food laws and rituals. On its own terms that position is coherent.

Misconception two: "Muslims copied these practices from Jesus." The Islamic claim is not that Islam was reconstructed from the Gospels, but that one God sent all the prophets with one way of worship — the Hebrew Bible itself describes prophets prostrating, fasting, and giving alms — and that Islam is that way continued. The resemblance is not borrowing; it is family likeness.

Misconception three: "This comparison settles which religion is true." It does not. Practice is one strand of evidence; the deeper questions — who God is, who Jesus is, what he taught about worship — are treated in the related articles.

Misconception four: "The disagreement is about whether Jesus kept these practices." Virtually everyone agrees he kept them. The real disagreement is whether they were meant to end with him. Christianity says the law reached its goal in Christ and its ceremonies lapsed. Islam answers that the way of the prophets was never abolished by God, and notes that Jesus himself is quoted saying he came not to destroy the law but to fulfill it (Matthew 5:17). That is the genuine crux.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Return to Jesus's own criterion, quoted above from two Gospels (Matthew 7:21; Luke 6:46): the Kingdom belongs not to everyone who says "Lord, Lord," but to "he who does the will of my Father." Whatever else following Jesus means, by his own words it cannot mean less than doing.

Islam's case is then simple to state. The man the Gospels describe prayed with his face to the ground, fasted, taught secret charity, greeted with peace, kept the food laws of Moses, and submitted his will entirely to God — "not what I desire, but what you desire" (Matthew 26:39). The community whose ordinary members still do all of these things every day, as obedience to the God Jesus worshipped, is the Muslim community. The Quran presents this as no accident: it calls his disciples muslimun, those who submit, and calls believers to answer as they answered (Quran 61:14).

None of this requires doubting the sincerity of Christian love for Jesus. It suggests something gentler and, for a seeker, more interesting: the way of life Jesus actually lived did not vanish from the earth. A reader who admires the prayer of Gethsemane can find it today — practiced, not only read about — among the people who say of Jesus what his own disciples say in the Quran: "We have believed in Allah and testify that we are Muslims [submitting to Him]" (Quran 3:52).

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does this article claim that Christians do not really follow Jesus?
A: No. Christians follow Jesus according to their own understanding: faith in his person and imitation of his character. The article compares recorded daily practices and lets readers weigh what the resemblance means.

Q: If Muslims do not worship Jesus, why do they pray the way he prayed?
A: Because Muslims worship the God Jesus prayed to. Islam teaches that all prophets practiced one way of devotion to the one God, which Jesus's recorded practice confirms.

Q: Did Jesus eat pork?
A: The Gospels never record it, and as an observant Jew he kept the Torah's food laws, which forbid pork. Christians who eat pork rely on later passages such as Mark 7 and Acts 10, understood as ending the food laws. Muslims continue the abstention Jesus himself practiced.

Q: Is saying 'God willing' really in the Bible?
A: Yes. James 4:15 reads: "For you ought to say, 'If the Lord wills, we will both live, and do this or that.'" The Quran gives the same command in 18:23-24, and Muslims practice it daily as insha'Allah.

Q: Does this comparison prove Islam is true?
A: By itself, no — and the article says so. It is one strand of evidence; questions about God, the Trinity, and worship are examined in the related articles.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following books span Muslim, Christian, and academic perspectives; listing them is not an endorsement of every argument they contain.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature.

Geza Vermes — Jesus the Jew: A Historian's Reading of the Gospels.

E. P. Sanders — The Historical Figure of Jesus.

Thomas a Kempis — The Imitation of Christ.

C. S. Lewis — Mere Christianity.

title: Jesus as the Messiah
subtitle: One shared title, two understandings — what al-Masih means in Islam and what “Christ” means in Christianity
summary: Islam and Christianity both call Jesus “the Messiah” — a title the Quran itself gives him through the angels before his birth. The word means “anointed one” and originally described human kings and priests chosen by God, which is how Jewish tradition has always understood the awaited Messiah. Christianity came to fill the title with belief in Jesus’s divinity and atoning death, while Islam holds that it means what it originally meant: God’s anointed prophet and servant sent to Israel. This article traces the title’s history and compares the two understandings verse by verse.

sections.title: In Brief: A Title Both Faiths Share
sections.body:
Muslims and Christians share something remarkable: both call Jesus “the Messiah.” The Quran itself gives Jesus this title — al-Masih — announcing it through the angels before his birth. But the two faiths fill the shared title with different content. Christians understand “Christ” to mean the divine Son of God who died for humanity’s sins and rose again; Islam understands al-Masih as the anointed prophet and servant of God, sent to the Children of Israel with the Gospel.

This article explains where the title came from, what each tradition means by it, and why Islam sees its own understanding as the original one — reading the key verses of the Quran and the Bible in full along the way.

sections.title: Introduction: Christ Is a Title, Not a Name
sections.body:
Few words carry as much weight between Islam and Christianity as “Messiah.” In English Bibles it usually appears in its Greek-derived form, “Christ” — so often that many readers assume Christ is part of Jesus’s name. It is not a name but a title, from the Hebrew mashiach, meaning “anointed one,” rendered in Greek as christos and in Arabic as al-Masih.

Remarkably, the Quran itself uses this title for Jesus — freely, repeatedly, and with honor, bestowing it through an angelic announcement before he is even born. This is genuine common ground, and this library tries to be honest about common ground wherever it exists.

But shared words can carry different meanings. When a Christian confesses “Jesus is the Christ” and a Muslim says “Isa al-Masih,” the same title is being understood in importantly different ways. This article walks through what the title meant in its original setting, what Christians came to mean by it, and what the Quran means by it.

sections.title: The Main Question: Same Title, Same Meaning?
sections.body:
The question is easy to state: when the Quran calls Jesus al-Masih and the New Testament calls him the Christ, are the two faiths saying the same thing?

Three positions need distinguishing. Jewish tradition, which produced the term, expected the Messiah to be a human being — a divinely chosen king and deliverer, never God Himself. Christianity confesses Jesus as the Messiah and, over time, bound that confession to belief in his divinity, so that “Christ” came to mean the divine Son who died for sins and rose again. Islam affirms Jesus as the Messiah in a sense much closer to the original: the anointed human servant of God, a prophet sent to the Children of Israel.

So the real question is not whether Jesus is the Messiah — Muslims and Christians agree he is — but what being the Messiah means.

sections.title: Historical Context: Anointing in Ancient Israel
sections.body:
In ancient Israel, oil was poured over the head of a person set apart for sacred office. Kings were anointed this way, and so were high priests. The Hebrew Bible repeatedly calls the king of Israel God’s “anointed” — the mashiach. The word thus began its life as a title for human beings chosen and commissioned by God. The Hebrew Bible even applies it to a foreign ruler: in the book of Isaiah, the Persian king Cyrus, who allowed the Jewish exiles to return home, is described as God’s anointed. Whatever else the word meant, it meant a human being appointed by the divine — not a divine being.

After the line of David’s kings fell and the Jewish people came under foreign empires, a hope grew that God would one day raise up a final anointed king from David’s descendants — a righteous ruler who would restore justice and gather the scattered people. By the time of Jesus, under Roman rule, this hope was very much alive and took different forms — a royal deliverer, a priestly figure, a prophet like Moses — but it consistently centered on a human agent of God.

When Jesus’s contemporaries asked whether he was the Messiah, this is what they were asking. And Jewish tradition, from that day to this, has continued to expect a human servant of God — not God become man.

sections.title: The Quranic Perspective: Al-Masih, Servant of God
sections.body:
The Quran’s use of the title begins before Jesus’s birth. When the angels bring Mary the news of the son she will bear, the title Messiah is part of the announcement itself:

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ يَٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍۢ مِّنْهُ ٱسْمُهُ ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ وَجِيهًۭا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْءَاخِرَةِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ

“[And mention] when the angels said, "O Mary, indeed Allah gives you good tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary - distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near [to Allah].” (Quran 3:45)

Notice what this verse does. It gives Jesus the title al-Masih as an honor conferred by God, calls him “a word from Him” — brought into being by God’s creative command — and declares him distinguished in this world and the Hereafter. No other prophet in the Quran bears this title. In Arabic, al-Masih comes from the root m-s-h, to wipe or anoint — the same Semitic root family as the Hebrew mashiach. Muslim commentators have traditionally explained it along these lines: Jesus was anointed — blessed, purified, and set apart by God — and some connected it to the way he passed his hand over the sick, who were healed by God’s permission.

Yet the Quran is equally careful to state what the title does not mean. Its most direct passage on the subject addresses Christians — the “People of the Scripture” — by name:
