A: The Quran answers this with the example of Adam (Quran 3:59): Adam had neither father nor mother, yet he was a created human being. A miraculous origin shows God's power to create as He wills; it does not make the created one divine. Notably, classical Christian theology also holds that Jesus's divine sonship is eternal rather than caused by the virgin birth, so both traditions agree the miracle alone does not establish divinity.

Q: Who was the spirit or angel that appeared to Mary?
A: Luke names the angel Gabriel. The Quran says God sent 'Our Spirit,' who appeared to Mary in the form of a man (Quran 19:17), and Muslim commentators have overwhelmingly identified this Spirit as Gabriel. The two accounts converge on the same messenger.

Q: Do the Quran and the Bible describe the same annunciation?
A: The parallels are striking: an angel announces a son to a virgin, she asks how this can be when no man has touched her, and the answer appeals to God's power. Both accounts also call Jesus the Messiah and exalt Mary among women. The two faiths differ mainly in the theological conclusion they draw, not in the event they describe.

Q: Does the Quran call Jesus the Messiah?
A: Yes. In Quran 3:45 the angels announce him as 'the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary,' distinguished in this world and the Hereafter. Islam affirms Jesus as the Messiah sent to Israel while understanding that title as a mark of his mission, not of divinity.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The books below treat the virgin birth and the wider place of Jesus and Mary in Islam and Christianity. They range from accessible introductions to detailed scholarship, and they include Christian as well as Muslim authors so that each tradition can be heard in its own voice.

Geoffrey Parrinder — Jesus in the Qur'an. A respectful, widely used survey by a Christian scholar of religion covering the Quranic passages about Jesus and Mary, including the annunciation narratives.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature. A collection showing how deeply Jesus is loved and quoted in the Islamic tradition beyond the Quran itself.

Mustafa Akyol — The Islamic Jesus. An accessible modern discussion of what the Quran says about Jesus and Mary and how it relates to Jewish and Christian views.

Raymond E. Brown — The Birth of the Messiah. A classic, detailed Christian scholarly commentary on the infancy narratives of Matthew and Luke; useful for seeing how Christian scholarship itself reads these chapters.

Gabriel Said Reynolds — The Qur'an and the Bible: Text and Commentary. A verse-by-verse academic resource comparing Quranic passages with their biblical parallels, including the Mary narratives.

relatedArticles.title: The Miracles of Jesus
relatedArticles.subtitle: What the Quran and the Gospels report — and what the miracles were meant to prove
relatedArticles.summary: Islam and Christianity both affirm that Jesus (peace be upon him) performed real miracles: healing the blind and lepers, raising the dead, and feeding the faithful. The Quran adds distinctive wonders such as Jesus speaking as an infant in the cradle and giving life to a bird formed from clay. Strikingly, the Quran says each miracle happened 'by permission of Allah', while the New Testament itself describes them as works 'which God did by him'. This article catalogs the miracles in both scriptures and asks whether miracles prove Jesus's divinity — or his prophethood.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Few things surprise new readers of the Quran more than how much it honors Jesus (peace be upon him) — and how vividly it affirms his miracles. The Quran states that Jesus healed the blind and the leper, raised the dead, and formed a bird from clay that came alive when he breathed into it. It adds wonders the Bible does not record, such as Jesus speaking from the cradle as a newborn to defend his mother's honor.

The Gospels report their own catalog: healings, the feeding of thousands from a few loaves, and the raising of Lazarus from the tomb. So both traditions agree that Jesus was a true worker of miracles.

Where they differ is on what the miracles mean. The Quran repeats, again and again, that Jesus did these things 'by permission of Allah'. The New Testament itself describes Jesus as a man through whom God worked wonders, and records Jesus saying he can do nothing by himself. This article lays out the evidence from both scriptures and examines the central question: do miracles prove that Jesus is God, or that God sent him?

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
This article belongs to a series on Jesus as the Quran and the Bible present him. Our method throughout is simple: let each scripture speak in its own words first, quote it exactly, and only then compare. Every verse below was retrieved directly from the source texts — the Quran in the Uthmani Arabic script with the Saheeh International translation, and the Bible in the World English Bible translation.

Miracles are a natural meeting point between the two faiths. A Christian reading the Quran for the first time discovers that Islam does not reduce Jesus to a mere teacher of ethics: the Quran presents him as one of the mightiest messengers God ever sent, marked out by signs no ordinary man could produce. A Muslim reading the Gospels finds accounts of healing and mercy that feel deeply familiar in spirit.

Precisely because both traditions affirm the miracles, they make an excellent test case for the deeper question that separates the two faiths: who was Jesus? A prophet empowered by God, as Islam teaches — or God incarnate, as the later Christian creeds defined him? The miracles themselves, as we will see, carry an answer within the very way both scriptures describe them.

sections.title: The Question: Do Miracles Prove Divinity?
sections.body:
The argument is often put like this: 'Only God can raise the dead. Jesus raised the dead. Therefore Jesus is God.' It sounds compelling. But it stands or falls on one point: did Jesus perform miracles by his own inherent power, or did God perform them through him?

Both scriptures address this directly. The Quran answers with a repeated, deliberate phrase — bi-idhnillah, 'by permission of Allah' — attached to each miracle. The New Testament, in the very first public sermon of Christianity recorded in the book of Acts, describes the miracles as works 'which God did by him'. And the Hebrew Bible describes earlier prophets — Moses, Elijah, Elisha — performing wonders of the same kind, including raising the dead, without anyone concluding that those prophets were divine.

So the real question is not whether the miracles happened; both faiths say they did. The question is what they were for: proof of Jesus's own deity, or God's public endorsement of His messenger.

sections.title: Historical Context
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century, in Galilee and Judea, when the region was under Roman rule. Historians of early Christianity — whatever their personal beliefs — broadly agree that Jesus was known among his contemporaries as a healer, and his reputation for extraordinary deeds is among the most widely accepted features of his public life. The written Gospels, composed in Greek in the decades after his ministry, preserve that reputation in narrative form.

In the Israelite tradition Jesus's audience knew, miracles had a well-understood job: they were credentials. Moses came to Pharaoh with signs. Elijah and Elisha healed the sick and even raised the dead. A prophet's wonders were read as God's endorsement of the man's message, not as evidence that the man himself was divine. Jesus's miracles landed in exactly this context, and the earliest recorded Christian preaching, as we will see below, describes them in exactly these terms.

The Quran was revealed in the seventh century in Arabia, and it addresses Jews and Christians directly and by name. On the subject of Jesus's miracles it does something remarkable: rather than disputing them, it affirms them emphatically — including wonders not found in the Bible — and then frames every one of them with a phrase that settles the question of whose power was at work.

sections.title: The Miracles in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's fullest single catalog of Jesus's miracles appears in Surah Aal-i-Imraan (the third chapter), where Jesus announces his mission to the Children of Israel. Quran 3:49 reads:

وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنِّى قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍۢ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ أَنِّىٓ أَخْلُقُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ فَأَنفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ وَأُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةًۭ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

'And [make him] a messenger to the Children of Israel, [who will say], 'Indeed I have come to you with a sign from your Lord in that I design for you from clay [that which is] like the form of a bird, then I breathe into it and it becomes a bird by permission of Allah. And I cure the blind and the leper, and I give life to the dead - by permission of Allah. And I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses. Indeed in that is a sign for you, if you are believers.' (Quran 3:49, Saheeh International)

One verse gathers four wonders: giving life to a bird of clay, curing the blind and the leper, raising the dead, and announcing hidden knowledge. Twice in a single breath comes the refrain bi-idhnillah — 'by permission of Allah'. And notice what the verse says the miracles are: 'a sign for you, if you are believers.' A sign points beyond itself, to the One who sent it.

The fifth chapter returns to the same catalog, but this time the speaker is God Himself, addressing Jesus. Quran 5:110 reads:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَتِكَ إِذْ أَيَّدتُّكَ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ تُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًۭا ۖ وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ ۖ وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِى فَتَنفُخُ فِيهَا فَتَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَتُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَنكَ إِذْ جِئْتَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ فَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِنْ هَٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌۭ مُّبِينٌۭ

'[The Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, remember My favor upon you and upon your mother when I supported you with the Pure Spirit and you spoke to the people in the cradle and in maturity; and [remember] when I taught you writing and wisdom and the Torah and the Gospel; and when you designed from clay [what was] like the form of a bird with My permission, then you breathed into it, and it became a bird with My permission; and you healed the blind and the leper with My permission; and when you brought forth the dead with My permission; and when I restrained the Children of Israel from [killing] you when you came to them with clear proofs and those who disbelieved among them said, "This is not but obvious magic."' (Quran 5:110, Saheeh International)

Four times in this one verse: 'with My permission.' The repetition is not decoration; it is doctrine. Every miracle is affirmed in the same movement that attributes its power entirely to God.

The verse also mentions a miracle unique to the Quran: Jesus 'spoke to the people in the cradle'. Surah Maryam narrates the scene. When Mary, unmarried and accused, brought her newborn to her people, she simply pointed to the child. Quran 19:29 reads:

فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّۭا

'So she pointed to him. They said, "How can we speak to one who is in the cradle a child?"' (Quran 19:29, Saheeh International)

Then the infant spoke — and his first recorded words in the Quran define his whole identity. Quran 19:30 reads:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

'[Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet.' (Quran 19:30, Saheeh International)

The miracle that saves Mary's honor is, at the same moment, a declaration: servant of Allah, prophet.

The Quran also records a table miracle. The fifth chapter is named Al-Maaida, 'The Table Spread', after it. The disciples ask for a meal sent down from heaven, and the exchange is telling. Quran 5:112 reads:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَن يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةًۭ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ قَالَ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

'[And remember] when the disciples said, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, can your Lord send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven? [Jesus] said," Fear Allah, if you should be believers."' (Quran 5:112, Saheeh International)

The disciples explain their motive in Quran 5:113:

قَالُوا۟ نُرِيدُ أَن نَّأْكُلَ مِنْهَا وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنَا وَنَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ صَدَقْتَنَا وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّٰهِدِينَ

'They said, "We wish to eat from it and let our hearts be reassured and know that you have been truthful to us and be among its witnesses."' (Quran 5:113, Saheeh International)

Notice what Jesus does next. He does not produce the meal himself; he prays. Quran 5:114 reads:

قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ٱللَّهُمَّ رَبَّنَآ أَنزِلْ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةًۭ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ تَكُونُ لَنَا عِيدًۭا لِّأَوَّلِنَا وَءَاخِرِنَا وَءَايَةًۭ مِّنكَ ۖ وَٱرْزُقْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ

'Said Jesus, the son of Mary, "O Allah, our Lord, send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven to be for us a festival for the first of us and the last of us and a sign from You. And provide for us, and You are the best of providers."' (Quran 5:114, Saheeh International)

And God answers in Quran 5:115:

قَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مُنَزِّلُهَا عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنكُمْ فَإِنِّىٓ أُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًۭا لَّآ أُعَذِّبُهُۥٓ أَحَدًۭا مِّنَ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ

'Allah said, "Indeed, I will sent it down to you, but whoever disbelieves afterwards from among you - then indeed will I punish him with a punishment by which I have not punished anyone among the worlds."' (Quran 5:115, Saheeh International)

Many readers hear in this passage an echo of the Gospel table scenes — the blessed meals, the feeding miracles — retold with the lines of authority drawn sharply: the disciples ask, Jesus prays, God sends.

For the Quran, this is not a rule about Jesus alone but about every messenger. Quran 13:38 states the principle:

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًۭا مِّن قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ أَزْوَٰجًۭا وَذُرِّيَّةًۭ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍۢ كِتَابٌۭ

'And We have already sent messengers before you and assigned to them wives and descendants. And it was not for a messenger to come with a sign except by permission of Allah. For every term is a decree.' (Quran 13:38, Saheeh International)

And the Quran draws the conclusion explicitly, in a verse that names the miracles' limit. Quran 5:75 reads:

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

'The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded.' (Quran 5:75, Saheeh International)

A man who eats depends on food; the One who sustains all things depends on nothing. The miracle worker was mighty — but he was a messenger, in a long line of messengers.

sections.title: The Miracles in the Bible
sections.body:
The Gospels present Jesus healing the blind and lepers, feeding great crowds, and raising the dead. The question for our purposes is how the New Testament itself frames these works — and its earliest summary is strikingly clear. In the book of Acts, Peter stands up at Pentecost and delivers the first public sermon of the new community. Acts 2:22 reads:

'Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know,' (Acts 2:22, World English Bible)

Three phrases carry the theology: Jesus is 'a man', he is 'approved by God', and the wonders are works 'which God did by him'. The miracles authenticate the messenger; the power belongs to God.

Jesus says the same of himself in the Gospel of John. John 5:30 reads:

'I can of myself do nothing. As I hear, I judge, and my judgment is righteous; because I don't seek my own will, but the will of my Father who sent me.' (John 5:30, World English Bible)

The most dramatic miracle in John is the raising of Lazarus, and the narrative detail matters: before the dead man walks out, Jesus prays — aloud, deliberately, and he explains why. John 11:41-44 reads:

'So they took away the stone from the place where the dead man was lying. Jesus lifted up his eyes, and said, "Father, I thank you that you listened to me. I know that you always listen to me, but because of the multitude standing around I said this, that they may believe that you sent me." When he had said this, he cried with a loud voice, "Lazarus, come out!" He who was dead came out, bound hand and foot with wrappings, and his face was wrapped around with a cloth. Jesus said to them, "Free him, and let him go."' (John 11:41-44, World English Bible)

Jesus states the miracle's purpose in his own prayer: 'that they may believe that you sent me.' Not 'that I am God' — 'that you sent me'.

The same posture appears in the feeding of the five thousand. Matthew 14:19-20 reads:

'He commanded the multitudes to sit down on the grass; and he took the five loaves and the two fish, and looking up to heaven, he blessed, broke and gave the loaves to the disciples, and the disciples gave to the multitudes. They all ate, and were filled. They took up twelve baskets full of that which remained left over from the broken pieces.' (Matthew 14:19-20, World English Bible)

Before the bread multiplies, Jesus looks 'up to heaven' and blesses — the gesture of a servant receiving, not a source producing.

The Hebrew Bible supplies the precedent that Jesus's first audience already knew. Centuries earlier, the prophet Elisha raised a dead child. 2 Kings 4:32-35 reads:

'When Elisha had come into the house, behold, the child was dead, and lying on his bed. He went in therefore, and shut the door on them both, and prayed to Yahweh. He went up, and lay on the child, and put his mouth on his mouth, and his eyes on his eyes, and his hands on his hands. He stretched himself on him; and the child's flesh grew warm. Then he returned, and walked in the house once back and forth; and went up, and stretched himself out on him. Then the child sneezed seven times, and the child opened his eyes.' (2 Kings 4:32-35, World English Bible)

Elisha prayed, and God raised the dead through him. No one, in either faith, concludes that Elisha was divine.

Jesus even extends the miracles beyond himself. John 14:12 reads:

'Most certainly I tell you, he who believes in me, the works that I do, he will do also; and he will do greater works than these, because I am going to my Father.' (John 14:12, World English Bible)

In fairness, Christian scholarship reads these same texts within a different frame. The Gospel of John calls the miracles 'signs' that reveal Jesus's glory and his unique relationship to the Father, and classical Christian theology understands statements like 'I can of myself do nothing' as the voluntary submission of the divine Son — an expression of his humanity or of his eternal relation to the Father, not a denial of his deity. Christians do not typically rest the case for Jesus's divinity on the miracles alone; they rest it on the resurrection and on the way John's Gospel speaks of Jesus's identity. That is the mainstream Christian position, and any honest comparison must engage it rather than a caricature.

sections.title: Reading the Two Accounts Side by Side
sections.body:
Set the two scriptures beside each other and a remarkable convergence appears. The Quran says Jesus gave life to the clay bird, cured the blind and the leper, and raised the dead 'by permission of Allah' (Quran 3:49) — 'with My permission', God says four times over (Quran 5:110). The New Testament's first sermon calls the miracles 'wonders and signs which God did by him' (Acts 2:22). Jesus himself says, 'I can of myself do nothing' (John 5:30), and before raising Lazarus he thanks the Father 'that you listened to me' (John 11:41). On the mechanics of the miracles — whose power was at work — the Quran and the earliest Christian preaching speak with one voice: the power was God's.

The two scriptures even agree on the purpose. The Quran calls the wonders 'a sign for you, if you are believers' (Quran 3:49). Jesus, in John, prays aloud 'that they may believe that you sent me' (John 11:42). In both texts the miracle is an argument for sent-ness — for prophethood — addressed to an audience deciding whether to trust the man who stands before them.

The precedent of earlier prophets seals the logical point. Elisha prayed and a dead child revived (2 Kings 4:32-35). Moses came to Pharaoh with signs. If raising the dead proved divinity, the proof would prove far too much: it would deify Elisha and Elijah, and — by Jesus's own words that believers 'will do greater works than these' (John 14:12) — his followers too. No Christian accepts those conclusions, which shows that the inference from miracle to divinity is not one the Bible itself teaches.

Where the traditions genuinely part ways is elsewhere. Christian theology reads Jesus's dependence on the Father as the internal life of the Trinity: the Son eternally receives from the Father, so dependence is compatible with deity. Islam replies that this reading arrives after the fact: taken plainly, a man who prays, receives permission, and credits God is showing us what a servant of God looks like — which is exactly what the infant Jesus of the Quran announces: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah' (Quran 19:30). It is also worth saying honestly that the Christian case for Jesus's divinity does not mainly rest on the miracles; it rests on the resurrection and on the exalted language of John's Gospel. Those questions deserve their own careful treatment — see our articles on the crucifixion and on whether Jesus claimed to be God. But on the ground this article covers, the miracles themselves, the two scriptures point the same way.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A first misconception: 'Muslims deny the miracles of Jesus.' The truth is the reverse. The Quran affirms every major category of miracle the Gospels report — healing the blind and the leper, raising the dead, wondrous provision of food — and adds wonders of its own: the newborn Jesus speaking in the cradle (Quran 19:29-30) and the clay bird brought to life (Quran 3:49). Within Islam these are real events, affirmed by God's own word, not legends to be explained away.

A second misconception: 'If Jesus raised the dead, he must be God.' As we have seen, both scriptures block this inference. The Quran attaches 'by permission of Allah' to the miracles; Acts says God did them 'by him'; Jesus says he can do nothing of himself; and Elisha raised the dead centuries earlier without anyone worshiping him. A miracle demonstrates that God is acting; it does not identify the man through whom God acts as God Himself.

A third misconception, raised from the other direction: 'The Quran borrowed the clay-bird story from a Christian legend.' It is true, and should be said plainly, that a similar story of the child Jesus bringing clay birds to life appears in the Infancy Gospel of Thomas, an early non-canonical Christian writing that circulated long before the Quran, and secular scholars often describe the Quran as engaging stories its first audience already knew. The Islamic response is twofold. First, the Quran presents itself as confirming and correcting earlier tradition; if the event truly happened, its survival in Christian memory — even outside the canonical Gospels — is precisely what one would expect, and retelling a true event is not borrowing a fiction. Second, the Quran's framing is pointedly its own: where a legend might display a boy wielding wondrous power, the Quran twice inserts 'by permission of Allah' into the telling (Quran 3:49), stripping the story of any suggestion of independent divine power. The Quran does not copy; it corrects.

A fourth misconception: 'Christians claim the miracles alone prove Jesus is God.' Thoughtful Christian theologians generally do not argue this — they know their own Bible describes prophets and apostles working wonders by God's power. The serious Christian arguments lie elsewhere, in the resurrection and in John's portrait of Jesus. Recognizing this keeps the conversation honest: the miracles are common ground between the two faiths, not a battleground.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
In Islamic theology a prophet's miracle is called a mu'jizah — literally, something that disables imitation — and its function is fixed: it is God's public endorsement of His messenger. By that measure the Quran gives Jesus extraordinary honor. He is one of the greatest of the resolute messengers, whose signs began at birth and ran through his whole mission: he spoke in the cradle, gave life to clay, opened blind eyes, cleansed lepers, raised the dead, and was answered from heaven with a table spread before his disciples. Islam does not diminish the miracles of Jesus. It insists on them.

But Islam also insists on what the miracles meant, and here it appeals to the pattern both scriptures share: permission, prayer, dependence. 'It was not for a messenger to come with a sign except by permission of Allah' (Quran 13:38). 'Which God did by him' (Acts 2:22). The consistent witness of the texts is tawhid — the absolute oneness of God — with Jesus as God's servant and messenger, exactly as his first recorded words in the Quran declare: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah' (Quran 19:30).

For the sincere seeker, this is good news rather than a demotion. It means the wonder you feel reading about Lazarus walking out of the tomb, or the crowds fed from five loaves, needs no revision — only redirection. The miracles were always signposts. Islam's invitation is to follow them where they point: past the blessed hands of the messenger, to the God who healed and raised and provided through him — the God Jesus prayed to, and the God he called his Lord.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does the Quran really affirm the miracles of Jesus?
A: Yes, emphatically. The Quran states that Jesus healed the blind and the leper, raised the dead, brought a clay bird to life, announced hidden knowledge, spoke as an infant in the cradle, and was answered with a table sent down from heaven (Quran 3:49, 5:110, 5:112-115, 19:29-30). Muslims accept all of these as real events.

Q: Do miracles prove that Jesus is God?
A: Not according to either scripture. The Quran says each miracle happened 'by permission of Allah', and the New Testament describes them as wonders 'which God did by him' (Acts 2:22), while Jesus says, 'I can of myself do nothing' (John 5:30). Earlier prophets such as Elisha also raised the dead (2 Kings 4:32-35), and no one concludes they were divine.

Q: Which miracles appear in the Quran but not in the Bible?
A: Two stand out: Jesus speaking from the cradle as a newborn to defend his mother (Quran 19:29-30), and the clay bird that came alive when he breathed into it (Quran 3:49). The table sent down from heaven (Quran 5:112-115) has no exact biblical parallel, though many readers hear echoes of the Gospel feeding narratives and the Last Supper.

Q: Why does the Quran keep repeating 'by permission of Allah'?
A: Because the phrase settles the very question this article examines. In one verse God says 'with My permission' four times (Quran 5:110). The repetition affirms the miracles fully while attributing their power entirely to God — honoring Jesus as a mighty messenger without deifying him.

Q: Didn't the Quran take the clay-bird story from a Christian legend?
A: A similar story appears in the Infancy Gospel of Thomas, a non-canonical Christian text older than the Quran, and scholars note the parallel. Muslims respond that the Quran confirms and corrects earlier tradition: if the event was real, its memory surviving among Christians is expected, and the Quran retells it with its own theological frame — 'by permission of Allah' — which the legend lacks.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below come from Muslim, Christian, and secular academic authors, so a reader can test this article's claims against several perspectives. Khalidi gathers the rich Islamic literary tradition about Jesus; Reynolds sets the Quran beside the Bible passage by passage; Akyol writes an accessible Muslim account of Jesus; Ehrman and Vermes represent critical historical scholarship on how Jesus was remembered and how claims about him developed.

As always on this site, we encourage readers to open the primary sources themselves: read Surah Aal-i-Imraan and Surah Al-Maaida alongside the Gospel accounts, and weigh what each text actually says about where the power behind the miracles came from.

relatedArticles.title: The Crucifixion Question
relatedArticles.subtitle: What happened to Jesus at the end? The Christian account, the historians' verdict, and the Quran's answer
relatedArticles.summary: Did Jesus die on the cross? This is the sharpest single disagreement between Christianity and Islam. Christianity places the crucifixion and resurrection at the very center of its faith, and mainstream historians widely accept that the crucifixion happened. The Quran declares that Jesus was neither killed nor crucified, but that it was made to appear so, and that God raised him to Himself. This article presents all three positions fairly and explains what is really at stake: whether God's forgiveness requires a blood sacrifice, or whether He forgives directly out of His mercy.

sections.title: The Short Answer
sections.body:
Did Jesus die on the cross? On almost no other question do Christianity and Islam disagree so sharply. Christianity teaches that Jesus was crucified, died for humanity's sins, and rose from the dead — and makes this event the center of the faith. The Quran teaches that Jesus was neither killed nor crucified, but that it was made to appear so, and that God raised him to Himself.

Between the two stands a third voice: historians without religious commitments generally accept that the crucifixion happened. An honest comparison cannot ignore this, and this article does not.

What follows presents each position as its own scholars state it, then explains why Muslims hold theirs with confidence — and what is at stake: whether forgiveness requires the blood of an innocent, or flows directly from God's mercy.

sections.title: Introduction: Where Two Faiths Part Ways
sections.body:
Islam and Christianity agree about Jesus to a surprising degree. Both affirm his virgin birth, his miracles, his title of Messiah, and his return before the end of history. A Muslim cannot be a Muslim without honoring Jesus.

Yet the two faiths tell different stories about how his earthly mission ended. For Christians the crucifixion is the heart of everything: it stands at the center of worship and of the doctrine of salvation. For Muslims, the Quran's declaration that Jesus was not killed and not crucified is equally non-negotiable, because it comes as the direct speech of God.

The subject deserves gentleness. For Christians the cross carries their deepest experience of grace; for Muslims, God's rescue of His messenger expresses His justice and power. This article will not mock either conviction. It states each position from its own sources, admits honestly where historical scholarship stands, and then makes the Islamic case openly rather than pretending the disagreement is smaller than it is.

sections.title: What Exactly Is Being Asked?
sections.body:
The crucifixion question is really three questions folded into one.

First, a textual question: what do the scriptures claim? The New Testament: Jesus was crucified, died, was buried, and was raised. The Quran: his enemies neither killed nor crucified him, it was made to appear otherwise, and God raised him to Himself. Neither text is vague about its own claim.

Second, a historical question: what can historians, using ordinary methods of evidence and probability, conclude about what happened outside Jerusalem in the first century? That question has a mainstream answer, stated plainly below.

Third — and here lie the deepest stakes — a theological question: how are human beings forgiven? Christianity's answer runs through the cross, an atoning sacrifice. Islam's answer runs directly from the sinner to God: repentance met by mercy, no blood required. The crucifixion question is finally a question about the character of salvation.

sections.title: The Historical Setting and the Historians' Verdict
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century in Galilee and Judea, then governed as part of the Roman Empire. Crucifixion was a Roman method of public execution — slow, deliberately degrading, used especially against slaves and those accused of rebellion. Its purpose was not only to kill but to warn.

The Gospels devote long narratives to Jesus' arrest, his condemnation under the Roman governor Pontius Pilate, and his crucifixion. The letters of Paul, generally regarded as the earliest surviving Christian documents, already preach the crucifixion and resurrection as settled proclamation, and references to Jesus' execution appear in later non-Christian sources as well.

Here honesty requires a plain statement. In mainstream historical-critical scholarship — including scholars who are neither Christian nor Muslim — the crucifixion is regarded as among the most widely accepted facts about Jesus' life. Historians reason that early Christians were unlikely to invent a shameful execution for the man they proclaimed Messiah, and that multiple early sources report it.

The same honesty requires a second statement. That method treats the resurrection not as an establishable fact but as a faith claim, because the historian's tools weigh probabilities of ordinary events and cannot certify miracles. What the method affirms, and what it cannot see, will both matter when we weigh the Quranic account.

sections.title: What the Quran Says
sections.body:
The Quran addresses the crucifixion directly in Surah An-Nisaa, rebuking those who boasted of having killed Jesus. Quran 4:157 reads:

وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا ٱلْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ لَفِى شَكٍّۢ مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا ٱتِّبَاعَ ٱلظَّنِّ ۚ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًۢا

"And [for] their saying, "Indeed, we have killed the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, the messenger of Allah." And they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them. And indeed, those who differ over it are in doubt about it. They have no knowledge of it except the following of assumption. And they did not kill him, for certain." (Quran 4:157, Saheeh International)

Notice what the verse does and does not deny. It does not deny that an event took place or that onlookers believed they had crucified Jesus. It denies that they killed him and that they crucified him, explaining the gap in one phrase: shubbiha lahum — it was made to appear so to them. It even asserts that those involved were confused: those who differ over it, it says, are in doubt about it.

The next verse states what actually happened. Quran 4:158:

بَل رَّفَعَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًۭا

"Rather, Allah raised him to Himself. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise." (Quran 4:158, Saheeh International)

How was it made to appear so? The Quran does not say, and beginners should know that everything beyond this point is interpretation, not scripture. Many classical commentators understood shubbiha lahum as substitution: another person, made to resemble Jesus, was crucified in his place. Others placed the weight on appearance itself: the crowd, the chaos, and God's intervention left observers certain of something that had not happened. The identities proposed for a substitute come from commentary literature of varying reliability, not from the Quran, and no version is required belief.

A related verse, Quran 3:55, records God's promise to Jesus:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

"[Mention] when Allah said, "O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve and make those who follow you [in submission to Allah alone] superior to those who disbelieve until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ." (Quran 3:55, Saheeh International)

The phrase rendered "I will take you" (mutawaffika) has been read in more than one way: the classical majority held that God took Jesus up alive, his death deferred until his return, while a minority read it as a peaceful death before the raising. On either reading, the decisive point stands — Jesus did not die on a cross at his enemies' hands.

Finally, Quran 4:159 points forward:

وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِۦ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا

"And there is none from the People of the Scripture but that he will surely believe in Jesus before his death. And on the Day of Resurrection he will be against them a witness." (Quran 4:159, Saheeh International)

Most commentators connect this to Jesus' return at the end of history, and narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim describe Jesus, son of Mary, descending near the end of time — a hope Muslims share, in their own form, with Christians.

sections.title: What the New Testament Says
sections.body:
Fairness requires letting Christianity speak in its strongest voice, and that voice is Paul's. Writing to the church in Corinth, he summarizes the message he says he received:

"For I delivered to you first of all that which I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures," (1 Corinthians 15:3-4, World English Bible)

This compact formula — died for our sins, buried, raised — is widely regarded by Christian scholars as one of the earliest summaries of Christian preaching. In the same chapter Paul argues that if Christ was not raised, the faith itself is emptied. The crucifixion and resurrection are not one doctrine among many; they are the load-bearing wall of the whole structure.

The Gospels narrate the event with stark brevity:

"It was the third hour, and they crucified him." (Mark 15:25, World English Bible)

Readers have long noticed that John's Gospel appears to place the final sentencing at a later hour, a detail Christian interpreters and critical scholars discuss in different ways; but all four Gospels treat the crucifixion as the climax of the story.

Two sayings from these narratives deserve special attention. The first is the cry from the cross:

"About the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice, saying, “Eli, Eli, lima sabachthani?” That is, “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”" (Matthew 27:46, World English Bible)

Christian interpreters note that these words open Psalm 22 and read the cry as Jesus bearing the weight of human sin while trusting the psalm's arc toward vindication. Muslim readers ask a different question: how could one who is himself God cry out that God has forsaken him? To Muslims the cry fits a beloved prophet in extremity far more naturally than an incarnate deity.

The second comes from Gethsemane, the night before:

"He said, “Abba, Father, all things are possible to you. Please remove this cup from me. However, not what I desire, but what you desire.”" (Mark 14:36, World English Bible)

Christian theology reads this as the human will of Jesus submitting perfectly to the divine plan. Muslims read it as something simpler: a righteous prophet, in anguish, begging his Lord — for whom all things are possible — to be spared. And the Quran's answer in 4:158, Muslims note, is that this prayer was heard: God raised him to Himself.

sections.title: Three Positions, Weighed Honestly
sections.body:
Set the three positions side by side. Christianity: Jesus was crucified, died for sins, and rose — the center of the faith. Historical scholarship: the crucifixion is the most securely attested event of Jesus' life, but the resurrection lies beyond what the method can affirm. Islam: Jesus was neither killed nor crucified; it was made to appear so; God raised him alive.

Each position rests on a different authority: apostolic testimony, probabilistic reasoning over sources, and the Quran received as God's direct speech. That is why the debate cannot be settled simply by announcing the historians' verdict.

Consider what historical method can see. Its conclusion follows from weighing sources under a working rule that excludes miracles from explanation. But the Quranic claim is precisely a claim of divine intervention in what observers perceived. A miracle of appearance is, by definition, invisible to a method that assumes appearances were normal — and every chain of testimony runs back through the very eyewitnesses the Quran says were deceived and confused. A Muslim can grant everything the method genuinely shows — an execution event, communities convinced Jesus had died — while denying the one link it cannot secure: that the man on the cross was Jesus.

Honesty requires admitting the argument cuts both ways. The Muslim accepts a miracle on the authority of revelation, which the secular historian will not. But the Christian does the same at the decisive point, since the resurrection — the actual center of the faith — is likewise held on revelation and testimony, not neutral history. At the moment that matters most, both faiths ask for trust in a revelation; the real question is which revelation deserves it, which is why the transmission of the Quran and of the Bible are examined elsewhere in this library. And beneath the historical question lies the theological one: if Jesus did not die for sins, forgiveness must come — as Islam says it does — from God's own direct mercy.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
First: the Quran does not deny that Jesus existed or that a dramatic public event took place. It denies that Jesus was the one killed and crucified, and states that observers were made to perceive otherwise. The position is not that nothing happened, but that what happened is not what they thought.

Second: it is often said that Muslims believe Judas was crucified instead. No name appears in the Quran. Proposed identities for a substitute come from later commentary of uneven reliability, and none is an article of faith. The Quran leaves the mechanism with God.

Third: the denial is not hostility toward Christianity. The Quran affirms Jesus' virgin birth, miracles, messiahship, and return. Muslims understand the denial of the crucifixion as God's vindication of Jesus — a rescue, not a rejection.

Fourth, a misconception in the other direction: that Christians believe God simply died on the cross. Mainstream Christian theology is more careful: it holds that the divine nature cannot die, and that Christ died in his human nature. Muslims find the two-natures framework untenable — that critique belongs to the article on the Trinity — but fairness requires stating the doctrine as Christians hold it.

Fifth: some academic writers compare Quran 4:157 to early sects called docetists, who taught that Christ only seemed to suffer because he was too divine to have a real body. The comparison fails at the crucial point: the Quran insists Jesus was fully human — born of a mother, eating food, praying. Its claim is not that Jesus had no real body, but that God protected His human messenger from that death. Muslims receive the verse as revelation correcting the record, not as an echo of anyone's speculation.

sections.title: The Islamic Case: Rescue, Not Sacrifice
sections.body:
The Islamic position is not an evasion of history; it is a claim about God, resting on three convictions.

The first is God's justice. Guilt is not transferable: no innocent can justly be punished so that the guilty go free. The Quran states the principle in Surah Faatir:

وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَإِن تَدْعُ مُثْقَلَةٌ إِلَىٰ حِمْلِهَا لَا يُحْمَلْ مِنْهُ شَىْءٌۭ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰٓ ۗ إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ وَمَن تَزَكَّىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَتَزَكَّىٰ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

"And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And if a heavily laden soul calls [another] to [carry some of] its load, nothing of it will be carried, even if he should be a close relative. You can only warn those who fear their Lord unseen and have established prayer. And whoever purifies himself only purifies himself for [the benefit of] his soul. And to Allah is the [final] destination." (Quran 35:18, Saheeh International)

If no soul bears another's burden, salvation cannot run through the execution of an innocent man, even a willing one. It must run through each soul's own turning to God.

The second is God's mercy. Christianity reasons that sin against an infinite God demands an infinite payment that only the cross could supply. Islam replies that forgiveness is not a debt to be settled but a gift to be granted: the Lord who commands His servants to forgive freely does not need blood before doing the same. The sinner repents, and God forgives directly — nothing stands between the servant and the Master. This is explored fully in the article on forgiveness in Islam and Christianity.

The third is God's faithfulness to His messengers. Across the Quran, God rescues His prophets as their enemies close in; read in that light, the ending of Jesus' story is the pattern's most glorious case. The prophet who prayed "all things are possible to you. Please remove this cup from me" was answered by the God for whom all things are possible: "Rather, Allah raised him to Himself. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise." (Quran 4:158). Where the cross would say God's beloved was forsaken, the Quran says he was honored and taken up — and, per Quran 4:159, his story is not finished.

A Muslim therefore faces this question without anxiety. The historical debate is real, and this article has stated it honestly. But where every party must finally trust a testimony, the Muslim trusts the speech of God, and finds in it a conclusion worthy of both God's justice and His mercy: no scapegoat, no forsaken prophet, no debt collected in blood — a rescue, a raising, and a door of forgiveness open to every soul that knocks.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus was crucified?
A: No. The Quran states that Jesus was neither killed nor crucified but that it was made to appear so (4:157), and that God raised him to Himself (4:158). Muslims receive this as God's direct testimony.

Q: If it was not Jesus on the cross, who was it?
A: The Quran does not say. Many classical commentators spoke of a substitute made to resemble Jesus, but no identification is part of the Quranic text or required belief. Muslims are content to leave the mechanism with God.

Q: Don't historians agree the crucifixion happened?
A: Mainstream scholarship does regard it as among the best-attested facts of Jesus' life, and Muslims should acknowledge that honestly. But historical method excludes divine intervention by design, while the Quran's claim is precisely that God intervened in what observers perceived. The same method also declines to affirm the resurrection — so both faiths ultimately rest on revelation at the decisive point.

Q: Without the cross, how are sins forgiven in Islam?
A: Directly. The sinner repents sincerely and God forgives out of His mercy — no intermediary, no sacrifice, no transferred guilt, for no soul bears the burden of another (Quran 35:18).

Q: Is Jesus alive now, according to Islam?
A: The majority view is that God raised him alive and that he will return before the Day of Resurrection — a hope described in narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim, and connected by most commentators to Quran 4:159.

Q: Does denying the crucifixion dishonor Jesus?
A: The opposite. In the Islamic account the denial is God's rescue and vindication of a beloved messenger, whom Muslims may never mention except with reverence.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below cover the ground from several angles: a scholarly history of Muslim interpretation of Quran 4:157; comparative studies of Jesus in the Quran and in Christian doctrine; the image of Jesus in the wider Islamic tradition; and a reconstruction of Jesus' life by a prominent non-Christian historian, useful for seeing how the secular case is actually argued.

As with every article in this library, the list exists so you can check the claims for yourself. Read the strongest version of each position, and weigh them with the seriousness the question deserves.

relatedArticles.title: The Second Coming of Jesus
relatedArticles.subtitle: Both faiths await his return — what each expects him to do reveals everything
relatedArticles.summary: Islam and Christianity both teach that Jesus is alive with God and will return before the end of the world — one of the deepest agreements between the two faiths. The Quran ties Jesus to “the Hour” and Muslim narrations describe his descent as a just ruler, while the New Testament promises that he will come back the way he ascended. Both traditions insist that no one knows when. The decisive difference is what he returns to do: Christianity awaits him as divine judge, Islam as God’s human servant and Messiah — and that difference distills the whole question of who Jesus is.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Islam and Christianity disagree about many things concerning Jesus, but on one striking point they agree: both teach that he is alive with God and will return before the end of the world. The Quran calls Jesus a matter of “knowledge of the Hour,” which classical commentators overwhelmingly understood to mean that his return will signal that the end is near, and well-known Muslim narrations describe him descending to rule with justice.

The New Testament promises that “this Jesus” will come back the same way he was seen going, and historic Christian creeds affirm that he will come again to judge the living and the dead. Both traditions also insist, in their own scriptures, that no one knows when.

This article sets out each tradition’s expectation from its own sources, shows how much they share, and then asks what the one great difference — whether he returns as God’s servant or as God — reveals about the deeper question of who Jesus is.

sections.title: The Agreement Nobody Expects
sections.body:
Ask a devout Christian what she is waiting for, and somewhere near the top of the list will be the return of Jesus. Ask a devout Muslim the same question, and you may be surprised to hear the same answer.

The disagreement between Islam and Christianity about Jesus is real and serious, and this library does not soften it. But it sits alongside an agreement that startles many people on both sides: the world’s two largest religions both teach that Jesus, the son of Mary, is alive with God and will return before the end of history. No other figure is awaited by so much of humanity.

This article lays out what each tradition actually teaches about that return, from its own sources. Following this library’s method, scripture is quoted exactly as published translations render it, classical interpretation is labelled as interpretation, and well-known Muslim narrations are described rather than quoted. The aim is that a Christian reader should recognize her own belief in the description of it, and a Muslim reader his — and that both should see clearly where, and why, the two expectations part ways.

sections.title: The Question Behind the Question
sections.body:
The question this article asks is not “will Jesus return?” On that, mainstream Islam and mainstream Christianity answer yes together.

The real questions lie one layer down. What does the Quran itself say, and what comes from hadith — the recorded narrations of the Prophet Muhammad’s teachings — and from classical commentary? What did Jesus’ own disciples expect, according to the New Testament? And where do the two pictures overlap? As we will see, they overlap far more than most people on either side assume.

Then comes the decisive question: when he returns, what will he come to do? Will he come as the servant and prophet of God, or as God coming to judge the world? The answer each tradition gives is a compressed summary of everything it believes about who Jesus is. That is why a chapter about the end of the story turns out to be a chapter about the heart of it.

sections.title: Two Communities, One Long Wait
sections.body:
The expectation of Jesus’ return is as old as Christianity itself. The letters of Paul, commonly dated to around the middle of the first century and among the earliest Christian documents we possess, already comfort believers with the promise that the Lord will come. The Gospels record Jesus speaking at length about the coming of “the Son of Man,” and the book of Acts opens with the disciples being promised that he will come back. By the fourth century the mainstream churches had fixed the expectation into their creeds: Christ will come again to judge the living and the dead. Christians have disagreed among themselves about the surrounding details — whether there will be a visible thousand-year reign, whether believers will be taken up before a final tribulation — but the core conviction of a real, personal return has been constant across Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant Christianity.

The Muslim expectation is nearly as old as Islam. The Quran, delivered in the early seventh century, ties Jesus to “the Hour” — its name for the end of the world — and Muslim scholars from the earliest generations transmitted narrations describing his descent in the last days. Belief in the return of Jesus became a standard article of mainstream Sunni faith; classical creedal texts, such as the widely memorized statement of faith by the Egyptian scholar al-Tahawi, include the descent of Jesus among the signs of the Hour. Shia Islam likewise awaits his return alongside the Mahdi, a righteous leader expected at the end of time.

One further historical parallel is worth noticing. Both communities have repeatedly produced confident date-setters, and both communities’ scholars have repeatedly rebuked them — because both scriptures, as we will see, place the timing of the end beyond human knowledge.

sections.title: What the Quran Says
sections.body:
The Quran speaks of Jesus’ connection to the end of the world in a handful of brief, weighty verses. In Surah Az-Zukhruf, at the close of a passage about him, God says:

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَعِلْمٌۭ لِّلسَّاعَةِ فَلَا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِهَا وَٱتَّبِعُونِ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

“And indeed, Jesus will be [a sign for] knowledge of the Hour, so be not in doubt of it, and follow Me. This is a straight path.” (Quran 43:61)

A word about translation is owed here. The Arabic says literally “and indeed he” — or, on another reading of the pronoun, “it” — “is knowledge of the Hour.” The bracketed words “Jesus” and “a sign for” are the translators’ clarification, following the reading that dominated classical Quran commentary: the pronoun refers to Jesus, and his return to earth will be one of the great signs by which people will know the Hour has drawn near. A minority of commentators took the pronoun to refer to the Quran itself. This library flags such matters honestly: the connection of this verse to Jesus’ return is early, dominant, and mainstream — and it is interpretation.

A second verse appears in Surah An-Nisaa, immediately after the Quran’s famous denial that Jesus was killed on the cross and its statement that God raised him to Himself:

وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِۦ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا

“And there is none from the People of the Scripture but that he will surely believe in Jesus before his death. And on the Day of Resurrection he will be against them a witness.” (Quran 4:159)

Here too the translators have made an interpretive choice visible: the Arabic says “believe in him,” and “Jesus” is the translators’ identification of the pronoun. Classical commentary preserves two main readings of “before his death.” On one, it means the death of each Person of the Book: before dying, every such soul glimpses the truth about Jesus, too late to act on it. On the other reading — the one tied most naturally to the raising mentioned in the verses just before — it means the death of Jesus himself: he did not die on the cross, he will return, and before his eventual death the People of the Scripture will come to believe in him as he truly is. Both readings are old; the second became an anchor for the doctrine of his return. Either way, the verse’s ending is unambiguous: on the Day of Resurrection, Jesus will stand as a witness concerning those who received scripture before.

The foundation beneath both verses is what God says to Jesus in Surah Aal-i-Imraan:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

“[Mention] when Allah said, "O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve and make those who follow you [in submission to Allah alone] superior to those who disbelieve until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ.” (Quran 3:55)

Jesus, in the Quran, was not killed. God took him and raised him to Himself. A man who is alive with God can return — and, on the mainstream Muslim understanding, will.

What the Quran states briefly, the hadith literature fills out. Narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim — the two most rigorously authenticated Sunni collections — describe the son of Mary descending among the Muslims in the last days, ruling with justice, and breaking the cross: a vivid image classically understood as his personal correction of beliefs that grew up around him after he was raised. Other well-known narrations describe him confronting and defeating the false messiah (al-Masih ad-Dajjal, a great deceiver expected before the end), an era of peace and plenty following his victory, and, in some reports, his living out his remaining years on earth and dying as every human being dies. Following this library’s rules, these narrations are described here rather than quoted; readers will find them in the standard collections in the chapters on the tribulations and the signs of the Hour. Their standing should not be understated: many classical hadith specialists judged the reports of Jesus’ descent to be so numerously transmitted that the expectation belongs to settled mainstream doctrine, not folklore.

sections.title: What the New Testament Says
sections.body:
The New Testament’s expectation begins at the moment Jesus leaves. The book of Acts describes the disciples watching him ascend, and two men in white addressing them:

“who also said, “You men of Galilee, why do you stand looking into the sky? This Jesus, who was received up from you into the sky, will come back in the same way as you saw him going into the sky.”” (Acts 1:11, World English Bible)

The promise is concrete: the same Jesus, returning the same way — personally and visibly.

The Gospels place the fullest teaching about that return on Jesus’ own lips, in the discourse he gave on the Mount of Olives. Matthew records:

“and then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in the sky. Then all the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of the sky with power and great glory.” (Matthew 24:30)

The phrase “Son of Man” — Jesus’ habitual way of referring to himself — echoes a vision in the book of Daniel of one like a son of man brought on the clouds before God. Christians read Matthew 24 as Jesus’ promise that he himself will appear in glory at the end of the age, and the historic creeds state the purpose: he will come again to judge the living and the dead. For mainstream Christianity — Catholic, Orthodox, and most Protestant churches — this is a single, visible, bodily return. Some Protestant traditions add further structure around it, such as a rapture of believers or a literal thousand-year reign, and Christians debate these details among themselves; the return itself is common ground.

Then comes a verse that has echoed through this discussion for two thousand years. Asked when these things would come to pass, Jesus answers — in Mark’s Gospel:

“But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.” (Mark 13:32)

Matthew’s Gospel carries the same teaching:

“But no one knows of that day and hour, not even the angels of heaven, but my Father only.” (Matthew 24:36)

Attentive readers will notice that the translation of Matthew quoted here lacks the words “nor the Son” found in Mark. Ancient manuscripts of Matthew differ over whether the phrase appears, which is why translations vary; its presence in Mark is not in dispute.

Two things stand out in these verses. First, the hour is unknowable, and Jesus shuts the door on the calculating spirit — a prohibition his followers have honored unevenly. Second, and remarkably, the Son himself is placed among those who do not know. Christians and Muslims read that second point very differently, as the next section explains.

sections.title: Shared Waiting, Different Roles
sections.body:
Set side by side, the two expectations agree on far more than either community usually realizes.

Both await a real, personal return — not a metaphor, not a movement, but the man himself. Acts promises that “this Jesus” will “come back in the same way” he was seen going; Muslim narrations describe a bodily descent. Both tie his return to the end of history: on the dominant classical reading the Quran makes him “knowledge of the Hour,” and Matthew places his coming at the climax of the age. Both declare the timing sealed — “of that day or that hour no one knows,” says Jesus in Mark, and the Quran consistently places knowledge of the Hour with God alone. And both therefore command the same posture: not calculation but readiness. Live now as you would wish to be found living then.

The parting of ways is over the role of the one who returns. In Christian teaching, the returning Jesus comes “with power and great glory” as divine judge; the creeds say he will judge the living and the dead, which makes his return the return of God incarnate to close the books of history. In Muslim teaching, the returning Jesus comes as what Islam holds he always was: the Messiah, a human servant and messenger of God. He will defeat deception, establish justice, restore the pure worship of the One God — and then he will die, as every human being dies, to be raised on the Last Day with everyone else. Judgment on that Day belongs to God alone.

The Quran’s own wording draws this line finely. In 3:55 God tells Jesus, “Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ” — God is the judge, and the centuries-long dispute about Jesus is among the things He will judge. And in 4:159 Jesus appears on the Day of Resurrection not as the court but as “a witness.” Testimony is given before a judge, not by one.

This is where Mark 13:32 becomes more than a footnote. If the Son does not know the day or the hour, Muslims ask, in what sense is he the all-knowing God? Christian theology has a considered answer — that Christ has two natures, divine and human, and spoke here from the human nature, whose knowledge was limited — and fairness requires saying that this answer is ancient and carefully worked out. But fairness also permits the Muslim reply: the two-natures reading is required by the doctrine, not by the sentence. Taken on its own terms, the verse describes exactly what Islam says Jesus is — an honored creature whose knowledge, like his life, is granted by God and bounded by God.

And that is what the difference finally reveals. Eschatology — the doctrine of last things — is theology in miniature. Tell someone what you expect Jesus to do when he returns, and you have told them who you believe he is. Christianity awaits its God; Islam awaits God’s servant. Everything else the two traditions dispute about Jesus is folded inside that sentence.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
“The second coming is a Christian idea that Muslims borrowed.” The Muslim expectation rests on the Quran’s own verses and on narrations transmitted from Islam’s earliest generations, and it entered the classical creeds long before modern polemics between the two communities. A Muslim would also invert the premise: on the Islamic account both expectations descend from the same source — authentic prophetic teaching — so agreement is not borrowing but shared inheritance.

“The Quran describes the second coming in vivid detail.” It does not, and honesty about this strengthens rather than weakens the Muslim case. The Quran’s references — 43:61 and 4:159 above — are brief, and their application to the return is classical interpretation: dominant, early, but interpretation. The vivid details — the descent, the false messiah, the just rule — come from hadith. This article labels each layer as what it is.

“If Jesus returns, he must bring a new revelation, contradicting Islam’s belief that Muhammad is the final prophet.” Mainstream Muslim scholarship holds the opposite. Jesus returns not to found anything but to complete his own interrupted human span and to rule by the final revelation already given. He comes back as a prophet God already sent, not as a new one, so his return leaves the finality of Muhammad’s prophethood intact.

“Muslims who await Jesus are half-worshipping him.” Islam gives Jesus titles it gives no one else — the Messiah, a word from God, born of a virgin — and awaits his return, yet directs every act of worship to God alone. Reverence and worship are different categories; collapsing the two is precisely the error Islam believes later generations made about Jesus.

“The signs are all here; the date can be worked out.” Both traditions bar that door with their own scripture. “But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father” (Mark 13:32); and in the Quran, knowledge of the Hour rests with God — Jesus is its sign, not its schedule.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Seen from inside Islam, the return of Jesus is God’s public vindication of a wronged prophet.

He was not abandoned to a criminal’s death; God said to him, “indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself” (Quran 3:55). He was neither the failure his enemies proclaimed nor the deity his later admirers proclaimed; in the Muslim expectation he will return to correct both errors in person — the narrations’ image of him breaking the cross is classically read as his own hand dismantling the theology built in his absence. And on its classical reading, 4:159 holds out an extraordinary promise: before his death, the People of the Scripture will at last believe in him as he truly is — one community gathered around one prophet, worshipping the One God whom he worshipped.

Then he will die. That quiet ending may be the most Islamic sentence in the whole expectation. Jesus’ story finishes not on a throne of universal judgment but in the earth, like Abraham, like Moses, like Muhammad — a complete human life, resumed and completed, with judgment left where the Quran leaves it: “Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ” (Quran 3:55).

To the Christian reader who loves Jesus and awaits him: that love and that waiting are not what Islam disputes. Islam’s invitation is to await him as — in the Muslim account — he would ask to be awaited: as the servant and Messiah of the One God, whose own recorded words in the Gospels place the knowledge of the hour with the Father alone. The Quran’s verse about him ends by addressing all of us: “so be not in doubt of it, and follow Me. This is a straight path” (Quran 43:61).

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims really believe Jesus will return?
A: Yes. It is mainstream doctrine in both Sunni and Shia Islam, grounded in Quranic verses such as 43:61 and 4:159 as classically interpreted, and in numerous narrations in the most authenticated hadith collections.

Q: Does the Quran state the second coming explicitly?
A: The Quran’s references are brief. It calls Jesus “knowledge of the Hour” (43:61) and speaks of the People of the Scripture believing in him “before his death” (4:159); reading these as his return is the dominant classical interpretation, and the detailed picture comes from hadith. This article labels each layer honestly.

Q: Will Jesus bring a new religion or a new scripture when he returns?
A: No. Mainstream Muslim teaching holds that he returns as a prophet God already sent, ruling by the final revelation rather than bringing a new one — which is why his return does not conflict with Muhammad being the final prophet.

Q: What is the Dajjal?
A: Al-Masih ad-Dajjal, often rendered “the false messiah,” is a great deceiver whom Muslim narrations expect before the end and whom Jesus is expected to defeat. Christians will recognize a broad parallel in their own tradition’s expectation of a final deceiver often called the antichrist.

Q: Do all Christians expect the same sequence of end-time events?
A: No. The core conviction — a real, visible return of Christ as judge — is shared across Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant Christianity, but details such as a rapture or a literal millennium are debated among Christians themselves.

Q: Can anyone calculate when Jesus will return?
A: Both traditions say no. Jesus in the Gospels says no one knows that day or hour, and the Quran places knowledge of the Hour with God alone. Both traditions call for readiness, not arithmetic.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The scripture quoted in this article comes from the Saheeh International translation of the Quran (via api.alquran.cloud, with the Uthmani Arabic text) and the World English Bible (via bible-api.com). Muslim narrations were described, not quoted, in line with this library’s sourcing rules; readers can locate them in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim in the chapters on tribulations and the signs of the Hour.

For readers who want to go deeper, the works listed below approach Jesus in Islam from several angles: a Christian scholar’s survey of the Quranic material, an anthology of how Muslim civilization remembered Jesus, a Muslim argument about his status, and a secular historian’s reconstruction of the earliest Christian expectation of the end. None of them is required to accept this article’s conclusions; each helps test them.

relatedArticles.title: What Is Tawhid?
relatedArticles.subtitle: The Oneness of God: Islam's Foundational Doctrine and the Message of Every Prophet
relatedArticles.summary: Tawhid is the Islamic doctrine of the oneness of God: He alone is Lord, He alone deserves worship, and nothing is like Him. Muslims believe it was the message of every prophet, from Noah and Abraham to Moses and Jesus. This article defines Tawhid and its opposite, shirk, in plain language for beginners. It then quotes the Quran and the Bible side by side, including the Shema of Moses and the words of Jesus in the Gospel of Mark.

sections.title: In Plain Words
sections.body:
Tawhid (pronounced taw-HEED) is the Arabic word for the oneness of God, and it is the single most important teaching in Islam. It means that there is only one God, that He alone has the right to be worshipped, that He alone is the Lord and Sustainer of everything, and that nothing in creation is like Him.

Muslims believe Tawhid was not invented in the seventh century. They believe it was the one message carried by every prophet God ever sent, from Noah and Abraham to Moses, Jesus, and finally Muhammad, peace be upon them all.

This article explains what Tawhid means in plain language, introduces its opposite, called shirk, and then lets the texts speak for themselves: first the Quran, and then the Bible, where the same theme of God's exclusive oneness appears in the words of Moses, Isaiah, and Jesus himself.

sections.title: Introduction: The Center of Islam
sections.body:
Every religion has a center of gravity. In Islam, that center is not a ritual, a place, or even a prophet. It is a statement about God: la ilaha illa Allah, "there is no deity worthy of worship except God." The doctrine behind that statement is called Tawhid, from an Arabic root meaning "to make one" or "to affirm as one."

Muslim scholars have traditionally unpacked Tawhid into three connected aspects, and learning them makes everything else in Islam easier to understand. The first is the oneness of God's lordship (in Arabic, tawhid al-rububiyyah): God alone creates, sustains, gives life, and governs the universe, with no helper, rival, or committee. The second is the oneness of worship (tawhid al-uluhiyyah): because God alone is Lord, He alone deserves prayer, devotion, sacrifice, and ultimate love. Worship, in other words, is God's exclusive right. The third is the oneness of His names and attributes (tawhid al-asma wa-l-sifat): God is described by the most beautiful names, such as the Most Merciful and the All-Knowing, and His perfection is His alone; nothing in creation resembles Him.

These three aspects are teaching categories, not three separate doctrines. In the Quran they are woven together into a single, seamless picture of one God who made you, who sustains you, and who asks for your heart undivided.

sections.title: The Question: What Does "One God" Really Mean?
sections.body:
The question this article answers is simple to ask and profound to think through: what exactly does Islam mean when it says God is one, and was that oneness the original message of all the prophets, including Moses and Jesus?

"One" here is not merely a number, as if the only issue were counting. Tawhid means God is one in an exclusive and absolute sense: no partners who share His power, no intermediaries who receive a portion of worship, no offspring who inherit His nature, and no image or likeness that captures Him. It is oneness of being, oneness of authority, and oneness of the right to be worshipped, all at once.

Tawhid also has an opposite, and the Quran treats it with the utmost seriousness. It is called shirk, which means associating partners with God: directing any act of worship to something other than Him, believing that anything shares His lordship, or giving His unique attributes to a created being. Shirk can be as obvious as bowing to a statue or as subtle as making wealth, power, or one's own desires the true object of devotion. In Islam it is the gravest of all sins, because it gives away the one thing that belongs to God alone.

sections.title: One Message, Many Prophets
sections.body:
The Quran does not present Islam as a new religion founded in seventh-century Arabia. It presents Islam as the restoration of the original faith of humanity: surrender to the one God. In the Quran's telling, history is a long chain of prophets, each sent to his own people, each carrying the same core message of Tawhid, and each followed, in time, by communities that drifted from it.

The Hebrew Bible preserves that same core message in some of its most famous lines. The declaration known as the Shema, from the Book of Deuteronomy, is the closest thing Judaism has to a creed; observant Jews have recited it in daily prayer for many centuries, and it declares in a single breath that God is one. The Ten Commandments open not with a rule about honesty or family, but with God's exclusive claim to worship. The prophet Isaiah repeats the theme with striking force: there is no god besides the one God.

The Gospels then record something remarkable for this discussion. When Jesus was asked which commandment is the greatest of all, he did not announce a new doctrine of God. He recited the Shema of Moses. Centuries after Jesus, church councils formulated the doctrine of the Trinity, the teaching that the one God exists as three persons; Christians regard this as the correct interpretation of their scriptures. Islam, arriving after those debates, called people back to what it presents as the older, simpler creed: the unqualified oneness proclaimed by every prophet. The sections below quote the texts on which that claim rests.

sections.title: Tawhid in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's most concentrated statement of Tawhid is its 112th chapter, Surah al-Ikhlas, "Purity of Faith." It is only four verses long, and Muslim tradition holds that it summarizes the Quran's entire teaching about who God is. Its first verse reads (the Arabic below opens with the basmala, "In the name of God, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful," the phrase with which the chapter begins):

بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ

"Say, "He is Allah, [who is] One," (Quran 112:1)

ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ

"Allah, the Eternal Refuge." (Quran 112:2)

The word translated "Eternal Refuge" is al-Samad: the One on whom all creation depends, while He depends on nothing and no one. Then come two verses that draw the boundary lines of Tawhid with complete precision:

لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ

"He neither begets nor is born," (Quran 112:3)

وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ

"Nor is there to Him any equivalent."" (Quran 112:4)

God has no parents and no offspring, no origin and no heirs, and nothing anywhere is comparable to Him. In four short verses, the chapter rules out idolatry, polytheism, and any doctrine that gives God a son or an equal.
