A: Al-Masih (the Messiah) is a title of honor the Quran gives uniquely to Jesus, marking him as the anointed one promised to the Children of Israel. Unlike in Christian theology, the title does not imply divinity; the Quran pairs it with 'son of Mary' and 'messenger of Allah' (Quran 4:171, 5:75).

Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus was crucified?
A: Mainstream Islamic teaching, based on the Quran, holds that Jesus was not killed by crucifixion but that God saved him and raised him to Himself, and that he will return before the end of time. That subject deserves its own careful treatment and is covered in related articles in this library.

Q: If Christians and Muslims both love Jesus, why does the difference matter?
A: Because both faiths agree that who God is — and who may be worshiped — is the most important question a human being faces. If Jesus is God, refusing him worship is a grave error; if he is God's human messenger, directing worship to him is precisely the excess both the Quran and, Muslims argue, Jesus' own recorded words warn against.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works are offered as general further reading for those who want to go deeper — from Muslim, Christian, and academic perspectives. They are listed as starting points, not as proof of any specific claim made above; every scriptural quotation in this article was drawn directly from the source texts cited in each verse's attribution, and the historical dates concerning the early councils were checked against the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's treatment of the history of Trinitarian doctrine.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature. Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God. Dale Tuggy — 'Trinity,' Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy. C.S. Lewis — Mere Christianity. W. Montgomery Watt — Muslim-Christian Encounters: Perceptions and Misperceptions.

relatedArticles.title: Did Jesus Claim to Be God?
relatedArticles.subtitle: What the Gospels record, what the Quran declares, and how to weigh Jesus’s own words
relatedArticles.summary: Whether Jesus himself ever claimed to be God is the pivotal question between Islam and Christianity. This article presents the Gospel passages Christians cite for his divinity, with their mainstream Christian interpretation, alongside the many sayings in which Jesus distinguishes himself from God. It notes honestly that no Gospel records Jesus saying “I am God” or “worship me,” and that the most exalted language appears in the latest Gospel. The Quran’s verdict is then given in Jesus’s own voice: worship God, “my Lord and your Lord.”

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body: Did Jesus ever say, in his own words, “I am God — worship me”? This is perhaps the single most important question separating Islam and Christianity, because so much else — the Trinity, the meaning of the crucifixion, the nature of salvation — depends on the answer. This article examines the Gospel passages Christians cite as claims to divinity, the passages in which Jesus plainly distinguishes himself from God, and the Quran’s account of what Jesus actually taught. The Islamic position is that Jesus, peace be upon him, was the Messiah and a mighty messenger who called people to worship the one God alone — and that his own recorded words, read as a whole, support exactly that.

sections.title: Two Faiths, One Question
sections.body:
Muslims and Christians agree on a remarkable amount about Jesus of Nazareth. Both honour him as the Messiah, born miraculously of the Virgin Mary, a worker of wonders by God’s permission, and a teacher of profound righteousness. The Quran names Jesus in dozens of verses and devotes an entire chapter to his mother, Maryam (Mary). No one can be a Muslim without loving and believing in Jesus.

The disagreement begins at one precise point: is Jesus God? Christianity, as defined by its historic creeds, answers yes — Jesus is God the Son, the second person of the Trinity, fully divine and fully human. Islam answers no — Jesus is a beloved prophet and messenger, honoured above almost all of humanity, but a servant of God like every other created being.

Because the stakes could not be higher, the question deserves a careful answer from the sources themselves — beginning with the words of Jesus, as each tradition records them.

sections.title: What Exactly Is Being Asked?
sections.body:
It is important to be precise about what is being asked. The question is not whether the New Testament contains exalted language about Jesus; it does. Nor is it whether later church councils declared him divine; they did. The question is narrower and more decisive: did Jesus himself, in his own recorded words, ever clearly claim to be God and instruct people to worship him?

Here a simple textual fact should be stated plainly, because careful readers on both sides acknowledge it: nowhere in the four Gospels does Jesus utter the sentence “I am God,” and nowhere does he command “worship me.” Mainstream Christian scholarship does not generally dispute this. Instead, it argues that Jesus claimed divinity indirectly — through titles he accepted, actions such as forgiving sins, and statements whose implications his hearers understood. Muslims respond that for a doctrine on which, in Christian teaching, eternal salvation depends, God would not leave humanity to rely on inference — and that the indirect statements read very differently beside everything else Jesus said.

Both readings are laid out below, each in its strongest form.

sections.title: How and When the Gospels Were Written
sections.body:
To weigh the Gospel evidence fairly, a few background facts accepted by mainstream historical scholarship are needed.

Jesus taught in Aramaic in first-century Palestine, but the four Gospels were written in Greek, decades after his ministry, by authors drawing on earlier oral and written tradition. Scholars broadly agree on their relative order: Mark is generally regarded as the earliest Gospel, commonly dated to around 70–75 CE; Matthew and Luke followed over roughly the next two decades; and John is usually dated to around 95 CE or slightly later, making it the latest of the four.

That order matters, because the Gospels do not all speak about Jesus in the same register. In Mark, the earliest account, Jesus prays to God, says that no one knows the Hour but the Father, and deflects even the compliment “good.” The most elevated divine-sounding sayings — “before Abraham came into existence, I AM,” “I and the Father are one,” “he who has seen me has seen the Father” — appear only in John, the latest Gospel. Critical scholars of the New Testament widely note this trajectory: the highest christology (the understanding of Christ’s status) appears in the latest source. Christian scholars respond that John preserves genuine early tradition, and some argue devotion to Jesus arose very early; that debate is ongoing, but where the strongest sayings sit in the timeline is not in dispute.

Finally, the formal doctrine that Jesus is fully God was defined at the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE, roughly three centuries after Jesus — amid serious disagreement among Christians themselves, since the priest Arius and his many followers held that the Son was a created being.

sections.title: The Quran: Jesus in His Own Words
sections.body:
The Quran does not treat the message of Jesus as a mystery to be reconstructed. It states that message directly, and it places it on Jesus’s own lips. Announcing his mission to the Children of Israel, Jesus says:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۗ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

“Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path."” (Quran 3:51)

The same declaration is recorded in Surah Az-Zukhruf, where the Quran summarises both his credentials and his call:

وَلَمَّا جَآءَ عِيسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

“And when Jesus brought clear proofs, he said, "I have come to you with wisdom and to make clear to you some of that over which you differ, so fear Allah and obey me.” (Quran 43:63)

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

“Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is a straight path."” (Quran 43:64)

Notice what the Quranic Jesus asks for: obedience to himself as God’s messenger, and worship directed to God alone. The two are never confused. On that basis the Quran issues one of its most solemn warnings — grounded, again, in Jesus’s own words:

لَقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ يَٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍۢ

“They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the Messiah, the son of Mary" while the Messiah has said, "O Children of Israel, worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord." Indeed, he who associates others with Allah - Allah has forbidden him Paradise, and his refuge is the Fire. And there are not for the wrongdoers any helpers.” (Quran 5:72)

The Quran then looks forward to the Day of Judgment, when God will question Jesus before humanity about what was done in his name:

وَإِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ءَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ ٱتَّخِذُونِى وَأُمِّىَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ سُبْحَٰنَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِحَقٍّ ۚ إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُۥ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُۥ ۚ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِى وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِكَ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ

“And [beware the Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'" He will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it. You know what is within myself, and I do not know what is within Yourself. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen.” (Quran 5:116)

And Jesus’s answer, in the Quran, is a complete summary of his ministry:

مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ شَهِيدٌ

“I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness.” (Quran 5:117)

In the Quran’s telling, then, Jesus never claimed divinity, never sought worship, and will himself disown any such claim before God. His message was the message of every prophet: worship the one God, “my Lord and your Lord.”

sections.title: The Gospels: Both Sets of Sayings
sections.body:
Christians who affirm the divinity of Jesus point above all to the Gospel of John, and three passages carry most of the weight.

The first is John 8:58: “Jesus said to them, “Most certainly, I tell you, before Abraham came into existence, I AM.”” In mainstream Christian interpretation, “I AM” deliberately echoes God’s self-identification to Moses at the burning bush in the book of Exodus, so Jesus is claiming the divine name — and the crowd’s attempt to stone him shows they understood it that way.

The second is John 10:30–33: “I and the Father are one.” Therefore Jews took up stones again to stone him. Jesus answered them, “I have shown you many good works from my Father. For which of those works do you stone me?” The Jews answered him, “We don’t stone you for a good work, but for blasphemy: because you, being a man, make yourself God.”” Here, Christians note, the audience itself accuses Jesus of making himself God — and he does not simply say, “you have misunderstood me.”

The third is John 14:9: “Jesus said to him, “Have I been with you such a long time, and do you not know me, Philip? He who has seen me has seen the Father. How do you say, ‘Show us the Father?’” On the Christian reading, Jesus is the perfect visible revelation of the invisible Father.

These interpretations deserve to be stated fairly, and they have been. But alongside them stand other sayings of Jesus — spread across the Gospels, including John itself — that point in a different direction. In Mark 10:18, when a man addresses him as “good teacher,” Jesus replies: “Jesus said to him, “Why do you call me good? No one is good except one—God.” In John 14:28 he tells his disciples: “You heard how I told you, ‘I go away, and I come to you.’ If you loved me, you would have rejoiced, because I said ‘I am going to my Father;’ for the Father is greater than I.” In Mark 13:32, speaking of the Last Day, he says: “But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.” — a plain statement that the Son’s knowledge is limited. After the events of Easter morning, in John 20:17, he says: “Jesus said to her, “Don’t hold me, for I haven’t yet ascended to my Father; but go to my brothers, and tell them, ‘I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God.’”” And in John 17:3, praying to the Father, he defines salvation itself: “This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ.”

Christian theology harmonises these sayings through later doctrinal categories: the Father is “greater” in role but not in nature; the Son’s ignorance of the Hour belongs to his human nature. These harmonisations are coherent once the doctrine of the two natures is assumed. The Muslim observation is that the doctrine must be assumed first — the plain sense of the words describes a servant of God who prays, obeys, depends, and worships.

sections.title: Weighing the Totality of Jesus’s Words
sections.body:
Set the two portraits side by side and something striking emerges: the Jesus of the Quran and the Jesus of the earliest Gospel material sound remarkably alike.

In Mark, Jesus deflects the word “good” toward God, confesses that only the Father knows the Hour, and prays to God in his hardest moments. In the Quran, Jesus says, “Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him.” The registers match. The explicit claims of divinity that Christians cite come overwhelmingly from John, written around six decades after Jesus’s ministry — and even John preserves “the Father is greater than I,” “the only true God,” and “my God and your God.”

That last phrase deserves a pause. In John 20:17 Jesus speaks of “my Father and your Father… my God and your God.” In Quran 3:51 and 43:64 he speaks of “my Lord and your Lord.” In these passages the two scriptures attribute to Jesus the very same self-understanding: he stands with humanity on the worshipping side of the line between Creator and creature.

The Islamic argument, then, is not built on ignoring the Bible. It is built on the totality of Jesus’s recorded words: a man who prayed to God, called God “my God,” disclaimed unlimited knowledge and goodness, and defined eternal life as knowing “the only true God” — and the one whom that God sent.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few recurring misunderstandings deserve direct answers.

First: “Muslims deny Jesus.” They do not. Belief in Jesus as the Messiah and one of the greatest of all messengers is an article of faith in Islam. What Muslims deny is that he is God — which, Muslims argue, is also what he himself denied.

Second: “‘I and the Father are one’ can only mean identity of essence.” Yet in the same Gospel, Jesus prays for his disciples “that they may all be one; even as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be one in us; that the world may believe that you sent me. The glory which you have given me, I have given to them; that they may be one, even as we are one;” (John 17:21–22). Whatever oneness Jesus shares with the Father in John’s language, he asks the same oneness for his followers — which fits unity of purpose and will far more naturally than unity of divine essence.

Third: “the Quran misunderstands the Trinity, because in 5:116 it speaks of Jesus and Mary.” The verse does not claim to define the church’s creed. It is a question God puts to Jesus about what people actually did — taking him and his mother as objects of devotion “besides Allah.” The Quran addresses worship as practised, and the exaltation of Mary in popular devotion was and is a real phenomenon; the verse condemns directing worship to anyone other than God, however it is theologised.

Fourth: “Jesus is called the Son of God, so he must be divine.” In biblical idiom, “son of God” was an honorific applied to kings, to the righteous, and to Israel collectively; Jewish hearers did not take it as a claim to be the Creator. The Quran rejects the title precisely because, in later usage, it came to mean something no prophet taught.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Islam’s verdict on this question is not an attack on Jesus; it is a defence of him. The Quran insists that Jesus said nothing more and nothing less than what God sent him to say: “I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord” (Quran 5:117).

On the Islamic reading, the evidence lines up. No Gospel records Jesus saying “I am God” or “worship me.” The earliest Gospel presents a praying, dependent servant of God. The loftiest language appears in the latest Gospel, and even there Jesus calls the Father “the only true God” and “my God.” The formal doctrine of his deity was settled by councils centuries later. The Quran cuts through that development and restores the original voice: “Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is a straight path.” (Quran 43:64)

For the sincere seeker, the invitation of Islam is simple and, Muslims would say, deeply loyal to Jesus: worship the God Jesus worshipped, pray to the God Jesus prayed to, and follow him as he asked to be followed — as the Messiah and messenger of the one God. That, in the Quran’s words, is the straight path.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe in Jesus?
A: Yes. Belief in Jesus as the Messiah and one of God’s greatest messengers is required of every Muslim. The Quran affirms his virgin birth and his miracles by God’s permission.

Q: Does Jesus ever say “I am God” anywhere in the Bible?
A: No. The sentence appears nowhere in the four Gospels, and Jesus never commands anyone to worship him. Christian scholars argue his divinity is claimed indirectly; Muslims argue the indirect passages are outweighed by his plain statements of dependence on God.

Q: What about “before Abraham came into existence, I AM” (John 8:58)?
A: Christians read it as invoking the divine name from Exodus. Muslims note it is an ambiguous phrase found only in the latest Gospel, which also records Jesus calling the Father “the only true God” (John 17:3) and “my God” (John 20:17).

Q: Why does Jesus sound so exalted in the Gospel of John?
A: Mainstream critical scholarship dates John latest of the four Gospels, around 95 CE or after, and observes that the most elevated language about Jesus appears there rather than in the earlier accounts. Muslims see in this the gradual exaltation of a prophet by later generations.

Q: What did Jesus ask people to do, according to the Quran?
A: To worship God alone: “Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path.” (Quran 3:51)

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works, from Christian, critical-historical, and Muslim perspectives, allow the reader to go deeper into how the understanding of Jesus developed and what his own words show.

Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God traces, from a critical-historical standpoint, how a Jewish preacher came to be regarded as divine, including the rising christology across the Gospels. Larry W. Hurtado — Lord Jesus Christ: Devotion to Jesus in Earliest Christianity presents the strongest scholarly case that devotion to Jesus arose early, and is included here for balance. Raymond E. Brown — An Introduction to New Testament Christology is a careful treatment by a leading Catholic scholar, candid about what the texts do and do not say. Geza Vermes — Jesus the Jew reads Jesus within his first-century Jewish context. James D. G. Dunn — Christology in the Making examines how the doctrine of the incarnation developed within the New Testament period. Muhammad ‘Ata ur-Rahim — Jesus: Prophet of Islam presents the Muslim understanding of Jesus’s mission and of how it was transformed after him.

relatedArticles.title: The Second Coming of Jesus
relatedArticles.subtitle: Both faiths await his return — what each expects him to do reveals everything
relatedArticles.summary: Islam and Christianity both teach that Jesus is alive with God and will return before the end of the world — one of the deepest agreements between the two faiths. The Quran ties Jesus to “the Hour” and Muslim narrations describe his descent as a just ruler, while the New Testament promises that he will come back the way he ascended. Both traditions insist that no one knows when. The decisive difference is what he returns to do: Christianity awaits him as divine judge, Islam as God’s human servant and Messiah — and that difference distills the whole question of who Jesus is.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Islam and Christianity disagree about many things concerning Jesus, but on one striking point they agree: both teach that he is alive with God and will return before the end of the world. The Quran calls Jesus a matter of “knowledge of the Hour,” which classical commentators overwhelmingly understood to mean that his return will signal that the end is near, and well-known Muslim narrations describe him descending to rule with justice.

The New Testament promises that “this Jesus” will come back the same way he was seen going, and historic Christian creeds affirm that he will come again to judge the living and the dead. Both traditions also insist, in their own scriptures, that no one knows when.

This article sets out each tradition’s expectation from its own sources, shows how much they share, and then asks what the one great difference — whether he returns as God’s servant or as God — reveals about the deeper question of who Jesus is.

sections.title: The Agreement Nobody Expects
sections.body:
Ask a devout Christian what she is waiting for, and somewhere near the top of the list will be the return of Jesus. Ask a devout Muslim the same question, and you may be surprised to hear the same answer.

The disagreement between Islam and Christianity about Jesus is real and serious, and this library does not soften it. But it sits alongside an agreement that startles many people on both sides: the world’s two largest religions both teach that Jesus, the son of Mary, is alive with God and will return before the end of history. No other figure is awaited by so much of humanity.

This article lays out what each tradition actually teaches about that return, from its own sources. Following this library’s method, scripture is quoted exactly as published translations render it, classical interpretation is labelled as interpretation, and well-known Muslim narrations are described rather than quoted. The aim is that a Christian reader should recognize her own belief in the description of it, and a Muslim reader his — and that both should see clearly where, and why, the two expectations part ways.

sections.title: The Question Behind the Question
sections.body:
The question this article asks is not “will Jesus return?” On that, mainstream Islam and mainstream Christianity answer yes together.

The real questions lie one layer down. What does the Quran itself say, and what comes from hadith — the recorded narrations of the Prophet Muhammad’s teachings — and from classical commentary? What did Jesus’ own disciples expect, according to the New Testament? And where do the two pictures overlap? As we will see, they overlap far more than most people on either side assume.

Then comes the decisive question: when he returns, what will he come to do? Will he come as the servant and prophet of God, or as God coming to judge the world? The answer each tradition gives is a compressed summary of everything it believes about who Jesus is. That is why a chapter about the end of the story turns out to be a chapter about the heart of it.

sections.title: Two Communities, One Long Wait
sections.body:
The expectation of Jesus’ return is as old as Christianity itself. The letters of Paul, commonly dated to around the middle of the first century and among the earliest Christian documents we possess, already comfort believers with the promise that the Lord will come. The Gospels record Jesus speaking at length about the coming of “the Son of Man,” and the book of Acts opens with the disciples being promised that he will come back. By the fourth century the mainstream churches had fixed the expectation into their creeds: Christ will come again to judge the living and the dead. Christians have disagreed among themselves about the surrounding details — whether there will be a visible thousand-year reign, whether believers will be taken up before a final tribulation — but the core conviction of a real, personal return has been constant across Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant Christianity.

The Muslim expectation is nearly as old as Islam. The Quran, delivered in the early seventh century, ties Jesus to “the Hour” — its name for the end of the world — and Muslim scholars from the earliest generations transmitted narrations describing his descent in the last days. Belief in the return of Jesus became a standard article of mainstream Sunni faith; classical creedal texts, such as the widely memorized statement of faith by the Egyptian scholar al-Tahawi, include the descent of Jesus among the signs of the Hour. Shia Islam likewise awaits his return alongside the Mahdi, a righteous leader expected at the end of time.

One further historical parallel is worth noticing. Both communities have repeatedly produced confident date-setters, and both communities’ scholars have repeatedly rebuked them — because both scriptures, as we will see, place the timing of the end beyond human knowledge.

sections.title: What the Quran Says
sections.body:
The Quran speaks of Jesus’ connection to the end of the world in a handful of brief, weighty verses. In Surah Az-Zukhruf, at the close of a passage about him, God says:

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَعِلْمٌۭ لِّلسَّاعَةِ فَلَا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِهَا وَٱتَّبِعُونِ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

“And indeed, Jesus will be [a sign for] knowledge of the Hour, so be not in doubt of it, and follow Me. This is a straight path.” (Quran 43:61)

A word about translation is owed here. The Arabic says literally “and indeed he” — or, on another reading of the pronoun, “it” — “is knowledge of the Hour.” The bracketed words “Jesus” and “a sign for” are the translators’ clarification, following the reading that dominated classical Quran commentary: the pronoun refers to Jesus, and his return to earth will be one of the great signs by which people will know the Hour has drawn near. A minority of commentators took the pronoun to refer to the Quran itself. This library flags such matters honestly: the connection of this verse to Jesus’ return is early, dominant, and mainstream — and it is interpretation.

A second verse appears in Surah An-Nisaa, immediately after the Quran’s famous denial that Jesus was killed on the cross and its statement that God raised him to Himself:

وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِۦ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا

“And there is none from the People of the Scripture but that he will surely believe in Jesus before his death. And on the Day of Resurrection he will be against them a witness.” (Quran 4:159)

Here too the translators have made an interpretive choice visible: the Arabic says “believe in him,” and “Jesus” is the translators’ identification of the pronoun. Classical commentary preserves two main readings of “before his death.” On one, it means the death of each Person of the Book: before dying, every such soul glimpses the truth about Jesus, too late to act on it. On the other reading — the one tied most naturally to the raising mentioned in the verses just before — it means the death of Jesus himself: he did not die on the cross, he will return, and before his eventual death the People of the Scripture will come to believe in him as he truly is. Both readings are old; the second became an anchor for the doctrine of his return. Either way, the verse’s ending is unambiguous: on the Day of Resurrection, Jesus will stand as a witness concerning those who received scripture before.

The foundation beneath both verses is what God says to Jesus in Surah Aal-i-Imraan:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

“[Mention] when Allah said, "O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve and make those who follow you [in submission to Allah alone] superior to those who disbelieve until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ.” (Quran 3:55)

Jesus, in the Quran, was not killed. God took him and raised him to Himself. A man who is alive with God can return — and, on the mainstream Muslim understanding, will.

What the Quran states briefly, the hadith literature fills out. Narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim — the two most rigorously authenticated Sunni collections — describe the son of Mary descending among the Muslims in the last days, ruling with justice, and breaking the cross: a vivid image classically understood as his personal correction of beliefs that grew up around him after he was raised. Other well-known narrations describe him confronting and defeating the false messiah (al-Masih ad-Dajjal, a great deceiver expected before the end), an era of peace and plenty following his victory, and, in some reports, his living out his remaining years on earth and dying as every human being dies. Following this library’s rules, these narrations are described here rather than quoted; readers will find them in the standard collections in the chapters on the tribulations and the signs of the Hour. Their standing should not be understated: many classical hadith specialists judged the reports of Jesus’ descent to be so numerously transmitted that the expectation belongs to settled mainstream doctrine, not folklore.

sections.title: What the New Testament Says
sections.body:
The New Testament’s expectation begins at the moment Jesus leaves. The book of Acts describes the disciples watching him ascend, and two men in white addressing them:

“who also said, “You men of Galilee, why do you stand looking into the sky? This Jesus, who was received up from you into the sky, will come back in the same way as you saw him going into the sky.”” (Acts 1:11, World English Bible)

The promise is concrete: the same Jesus, returning the same way — personally and visibly.

The Gospels place the fullest teaching about that return on Jesus’ own lips, in the discourse he gave on the Mount of Olives. Matthew records:

“and then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in the sky. Then all the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of the sky with power and great glory.” (Matthew 24:30)

The phrase “Son of Man” — Jesus’ habitual way of referring to himself — echoes a vision in the book of Daniel of one like a son of man brought on the clouds before God. Christians read Matthew 24 as Jesus’ promise that he himself will appear in glory at the end of the age, and the historic creeds state the purpose: he will come again to judge the living and the dead. For mainstream Christianity — Catholic, Orthodox, and most Protestant churches — this is a single, visible, bodily return. Some Protestant traditions add further structure around it, such as a rapture of believers or a literal thousand-year reign, and Christians debate these details among themselves; the return itself is common ground.

Then comes a verse that has echoed through this discussion for two thousand years. Asked when these things would come to pass, Jesus answers — in Mark’s Gospel:

“But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.” (Mark 13:32)

Matthew’s Gospel carries the same teaching:

“But no one knows of that day and hour, not even the angels of heaven, but my Father only.” (Matthew 24:36)

Attentive readers will notice that the translation of Matthew quoted here lacks the words “nor the Son” found in Mark. Ancient manuscripts of Matthew differ over whether the phrase appears, which is why translations vary; its presence in Mark is not in dispute.

Two things stand out in these verses. First, the hour is unknowable, and Jesus shuts the door on the calculating spirit — a prohibition his followers have honored unevenly. Second, and remarkably, the Son himself is placed among those who do not know. Christians and Muslims read that second point very differently, as the next section explains.

sections.title: Shared Waiting, Different Roles
sections.body:
Set side by side, the two expectations agree on far more than either community usually realizes.

Both await a real, personal return — not a metaphor, not a movement, but the man himself. Acts promises that “this Jesus” will “come back in the same way” he was seen going; Muslim narrations describe a bodily descent. Both tie his return to the end of history: on the dominant classical reading the Quran makes him “knowledge of the Hour,” and Matthew places his coming at the climax of the age. Both declare the timing sealed — “of that day or that hour no one knows,” says Jesus in Mark, and the Quran consistently places knowledge of the Hour with God alone. And both therefore command the same posture: not calculation but readiness. Live now as you would wish to be found living then.

The parting of ways is over the role of the one who returns. In Christian teaching, the returning Jesus comes “with power and great glory” as divine judge; the creeds say he will judge the living and the dead, which makes his return the return of God incarnate to close the books of history. In Muslim teaching, the returning Jesus comes as what Islam holds he always was: the Messiah, a human servant and messenger of God. He will defeat deception, establish justice, restore the pure worship of the One God — and then he will die, as every human being dies, to be raised on the Last Day with everyone else. Judgment on that Day belongs to God alone.

The Quran’s own wording draws this line finely. In 3:55 God tells Jesus, “Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ” — God is the judge, and the centuries-long dispute about Jesus is among the things He will judge. And in 4:159 Jesus appears on the Day of Resurrection not as the court but as “a witness.” Testimony is given before a judge, not by one.

This is where Mark 13:32 becomes more than a footnote. If the Son does not know the day or the hour, Muslims ask, in what sense is he the all-knowing God? Christian theology has a considered answer — that Christ has two natures, divine and human, and spoke here from the human nature, whose knowledge was limited — and fairness requires saying that this answer is ancient and carefully worked out. But fairness also permits the Muslim reply: the two-natures reading is required by the doctrine, not by the sentence. Taken on its own terms, the verse describes exactly what Islam says Jesus is — an honored creature whose knowledge, like his life, is granted by God and bounded by God.

And that is what the difference finally reveals. Eschatology — the doctrine of last things — is theology in miniature. Tell someone what you expect Jesus to do when he returns, and you have told them who you believe he is. Christianity awaits its God; Islam awaits God’s servant. Everything else the two traditions dispute about Jesus is folded inside that sentence.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
“The second coming is a Christian idea that Muslims borrowed.” The Muslim expectation rests on the Quran’s own verses and on narrations transmitted from Islam’s earliest generations, and it entered the classical creeds long before modern polemics between the two communities. A Muslim would also invert the premise: on the Islamic account both expectations descend from the same source — authentic prophetic teaching — so agreement is not borrowing but shared inheritance.

“The Quran describes the second coming in vivid detail.” It does not, and honesty about this strengthens rather than weakens the Muslim case. The Quran’s references — 43:61 and 4:159 above — are brief, and their application to the return is classical interpretation: dominant, early, but interpretation. The vivid details — the descent, the false messiah, the just rule — come from hadith. This article labels each layer as what it is.

“If Jesus returns, he must bring a new revelation, contradicting Islam’s belief that Muhammad is the final prophet.” Mainstream Muslim scholarship holds the opposite. Jesus returns not to found anything but to complete his own interrupted human span and to rule by the final revelation already given. He comes back as a prophet God already sent, not as a new one, so his return leaves the finality of Muhammad’s prophethood intact.

“Muslims who await Jesus are half-worshipping him.” Islam gives Jesus titles it gives no one else — the Messiah, a word from God, born of a virgin — and awaits his return, yet directs every act of worship to God alone. Reverence and worship are different categories; collapsing the two is precisely the error Islam believes later generations made about Jesus.

“The signs are all here; the date can be worked out.” Both traditions bar that door with their own scripture. “But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father” (Mark 13:32); and in the Quran, knowledge of the Hour rests with God — Jesus is its sign, not its schedule.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Seen from inside Islam, the return of Jesus is God’s public vindication of a wronged prophet.

He was not abandoned to a criminal’s death; God said to him, “indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself” (Quran 3:55). He was neither the failure his enemies proclaimed nor the deity his later admirers proclaimed; in the Muslim expectation he will return to correct both errors in person — the narrations’ image of him breaking the cross is classically read as his own hand dismantling the theology built in his absence. And on its classical reading, 4:159 holds out an extraordinary promise: before his death, the People of the Scripture will at last believe in him as he truly is — one community gathered around one prophet, worshipping the One God whom he worshipped.

Then he will die. That quiet ending may be the most Islamic sentence in the whole expectation. Jesus’ story finishes not on a throne of universal judgment but in the earth, like Abraham, like Moses, like Muhammad — a complete human life, resumed and completed, with judgment left where the Quran leaves it: “Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ” (Quran 3:55).

To the Christian reader who loves Jesus and awaits him: that love and that waiting are not what Islam disputes. Islam’s invitation is to await him as — in the Muslim account — he would ask to be awaited: as the servant and Messiah of the One God, whose own recorded words in the Gospels place the knowledge of the hour with the Father alone. The Quran’s verse about him ends by addressing all of us: “so be not in doubt of it, and follow Me. This is a straight path” (Quran 43:61).

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims really believe Jesus will return?
A: Yes. It is mainstream doctrine in both Sunni and Shia Islam, grounded in Quranic verses such as 43:61 and 4:159 as classically interpreted, and in numerous narrations in the most authenticated hadith collections.

Q: Does the Quran state the second coming explicitly?
A: The Quran’s references are brief. It calls Jesus “knowledge of the Hour” (43:61) and speaks of the People of the Scripture believing in him “before his death” (4:159); reading these as his return is the dominant classical interpretation, and the detailed picture comes from hadith. This article labels each layer honestly.

Q: Will Jesus bring a new religion or a new scripture when he returns?
A: No. Mainstream Muslim teaching holds that he returns as a prophet God already sent, ruling by the final revelation rather than bringing a new one — which is why his return does not conflict with Muhammad being the final prophet.

Q: What is the Dajjal?
A: Al-Masih ad-Dajjal, often rendered “the false messiah,” is a great deceiver whom Muslim narrations expect before the end and whom Jesus is expected to defeat. Christians will recognize a broad parallel in their own tradition’s expectation of a final deceiver often called the antichrist.

Q: Do all Christians expect the same sequence of end-time events?
A: No. The core conviction — a real, visible return of Christ as judge — is shared across Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant Christianity, but details such as a rapture or a literal millennium are debated among Christians themselves.

Q: Can anyone calculate when Jesus will return?
A: Both traditions say no. Jesus in the Gospels says no one knows that day or hour, and the Quran places knowledge of the Hour with God alone. Both traditions call for readiness, not arithmetic.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The scripture quoted in this article comes from the Saheeh International translation of the Quran (via api.alquran.cloud, with the Uthmani Arabic text) and the World English Bible (via bible-api.com). Muslim narrations were described, not quoted, in line with this library’s sourcing rules; readers can locate them in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim in the chapters on tribulations and the signs of the Hour.

For readers who want to go deeper, the works listed below approach Jesus in Islam from several angles: a Christian scholar’s survey of the Quranic material, an anthology of how Muslim civilization remembered Jesus, a Muslim argument about his status, and a secular historian’s reconstruction of the earliest Christian expectation of the end. None of them is required to accept this article’s conclusions; each helps test them.

relatedArticles.title: Jesus as the Messiah
relatedArticles.subtitle: One shared title, two understandings — what al-Masih means in Islam and what “Christ” means in Christianity
relatedArticles.summary: Islam and Christianity both call Jesus “the Messiah” — a title the Quran itself gives him through the angels before his birth. The word means “anointed one” and originally described human kings and priests chosen by God, which is how Jewish tradition has always understood the awaited Messiah. Christianity came to fill the title with belief in Jesus’s divinity and atoning death, while Islam holds that it means what it originally meant: God’s anointed prophet and servant sent to Israel. This article traces the title’s history and compares the two understandings verse by verse.

sections.title: In Brief: A Title Both Faiths Share
sections.body:
Muslims and Christians share something remarkable: both call Jesus “the Messiah.” The Quran itself gives Jesus this title — al-Masih — announcing it through the angels before his birth. But the two faiths fill the shared title with different content. Christians understand “Christ” to mean the divine Son of God who died for humanity’s sins and rose again; Islam understands al-Masih as the anointed prophet and servant of God, sent to the Children of Israel with the Gospel.

This article explains where the title came from, what each tradition means by it, and why Islam sees its own understanding as the original one — reading the key verses of the Quran and the Bible in full along the way.

sections.title: Introduction: Christ Is a Title, Not a Name
sections.body:
Few words carry as much weight between Islam and Christianity as “Messiah.” In English Bibles it usually appears in its Greek-derived form, “Christ” — so often that many readers assume Christ is part of Jesus’s name. It is not a name but a title, from the Hebrew mashiach, meaning “anointed one,” rendered in Greek as christos and in Arabic as al-Masih.

Remarkably, the Quran itself uses this title for Jesus — freely, repeatedly, and with honor, bestowing it through an angelic announcement before he is even born. This is genuine common ground, and this library tries to be honest about common ground wherever it exists.

But shared words can carry different meanings. When a Christian confesses “Jesus is the Christ” and a Muslim says “Isa al-Masih,” the same title is being understood in importantly different ways. This article walks through what the title meant in its original setting, what Christians came to mean by it, and what the Quran means by it.

sections.title: The Main Question: Same Title, Same Meaning?
sections.body:
The question is easy to state: when the Quran calls Jesus al-Masih and the New Testament calls him the Christ, are the two faiths saying the same thing?

Three positions need distinguishing. Jewish tradition, which produced the term, expected the Messiah to be a human being — a divinely chosen king and deliverer, never God Himself. Christianity confesses Jesus as the Messiah and, over time, bound that confession to belief in his divinity, so that “Christ” came to mean the divine Son who died for sins and rose again. Islam affirms Jesus as the Messiah in a sense much closer to the original: the anointed human servant of God, a prophet sent to the Children of Israel.

So the real question is not whether Jesus is the Messiah — Muslims and Christians agree he is — but what being the Messiah means.

sections.title: Historical Context: Anointing in Ancient Israel
sections.body:
In ancient Israel, oil was poured over the head of a person set apart for sacred office. Kings were anointed this way, and so were high priests. The Hebrew Bible repeatedly calls the king of Israel God’s “anointed” — the mashiach. The word thus began its life as a title for human beings chosen and commissioned by God. The Hebrew Bible even applies it to a foreign ruler: in the book of Isaiah, the Persian king Cyrus, who allowed the Jewish exiles to return home, is described as God’s anointed. Whatever else the word meant, it meant a human being appointed by the divine — not a divine being.

After the line of David’s kings fell and the Jewish people came under foreign empires, a hope grew that God would one day raise up a final anointed king from David’s descendants — a righteous ruler who would restore justice and gather the scattered people. By the time of Jesus, under Roman rule, this hope was very much alive and took different forms — a royal deliverer, a priestly figure, a prophet like Moses — but it consistently centered on a human agent of God.

When Jesus’s contemporaries asked whether he was the Messiah, this is what they were asking. And Jewish tradition, from that day to this, has continued to expect a human servant of God — not God become man.

sections.title: The Quranic Perspective: Al-Masih, Servant of God
sections.body:
The Quran’s use of the title begins before Jesus’s birth. When the angels bring Mary the news of the son she will bear, the title Messiah is part of the announcement itself:

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ يَٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍۢ مِّنْهُ ٱسْمُهُ ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ وَجِيهًۭا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْءَاخِرَةِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ

“[And mention] when the angels said, "O Mary, indeed Allah gives you good tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary - distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near [to Allah].” (Quran 3:45)

Notice what this verse does. It gives Jesus the title al-Masih as an honor conferred by God, calls him “a word from Him” — brought into being by God’s creative command — and declares him distinguished in this world and the Hereafter. No other prophet in the Quran bears this title. In Arabic, al-Masih comes from the root m-s-h, to wipe or anoint — the same Semitic root family as the Hebrew mashiach. Muslim commentators have traditionally explained it along these lines: Jesus was anointed — blessed, purified, and set apart by God — and some connected it to the way he passed his hand over the sick, who were healed by God’s permission.

Yet the Quran is equally careful to state what the title does not mean. Its most direct passage on the subject addresses Christians — the “People of the Scripture” — by name:

يَٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌۭ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًۭا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًۭا

“O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, "Three"; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs.” (Quran 4:171)

Here the Quran affirms the exalted titles — the Messiah, a messenger of God, His word, a soul from Him — and in the same breath draws the boundary: do not commit excess in religion; do not say “Three”; God is one, exalted above having a son. The very next verse explains why servanthood is no insult to the Messiah:

لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًۭا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًۭا

“Never would the Messiah disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels near [to Him]. And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant - He will gather them to Himself all together.” (Quran 4:172)

To be a servant of God is, in the Quran’s vision, the highest station a creature can hold. The Messiah does not disdain it — he embodies it.

Finally, the Quran points to something disarmingly simple about Jesus and his mother:

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

“The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded.” (Quran 5:75)

The Messiah and his truthful mother “both used to eat food.” Whoever eats depends on nourishment, and whoever depends on anything is a creature, not the self-sufficient Creator. For the Quran, Jesus’s messiahship and his full humanity sit together without tension.

sections.title: The Biblical Perspective: The Christ in the Gospels
sections.body:
The New Testament was written in Greek, and it translates the Hebrew title mashiach with the Greek word christos. One of its own verses shows the equivalence explicitly. When Andrew meets Jesus and runs to tell his brother, the Gospel of John pauses to explain the Hebrew title for its Greek-speaking readers:

“He first found his own brother, Simon, and said to him, “We have found the Messiah!” (which is, being interpreted, Christ).” (John 1:41, World English Bible)

“Messiah” and “Christ” are one word in two languages. The most famous confession of the title comes from Peter. When Jesus asks his disciples who they say he is, Peter answers:

“Simon Peter answered, “You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.”” (Matthew 16:16)

Christians have historically read “the Son of the living God” as a confession of Jesus’s divinity, joining the two halves of Peter’s sentence into one: the Messiah is the divine Son. Readers should know, however, that scholars of early Judaism note that “son of God” in a first-century Jewish setting was also a familiar way of speaking about the anointed king — a title of appointment rather than of shared divine essence. Which reading is correct is taken up in this library’s article on whether Jesus claimed to be God.

Jesus himself, in the Gospels, accepts the title of Messiah plainly. To a Samaritan woman who says she knows the Messiah is coming, he replies directly:

“The woman said to him, “I know that Messiah comes, he who is called Christ. When he has come, he will declare to us all things.” Jesus said to her, “I am he, the one who speaks to you.”” (John 4:25-26)

And in the synagogue at Nazareth, the Gospel of Luke records Jesus defining his own anointing by reading aloud from the scroll of the prophet Isaiah:

““The Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to preach good news to the poor. He has sent me to heal the broken hearted, to proclaim release to the captives, recovering of sight to the blind, to deliver those who are crushed, and to proclaim the acceptable year of the Lord.” He closed the book, gave it back to the attendant, and sat down. The eyes of all in the synagogue were fastened on him. He began to tell them, “Today, this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing.”” (Luke 4:18-21)

This passage repays attention. Jesus says the Spirit of the Lord is on him because God “has anointed” him — to preach good news, to heal, to proclaim release. The anointing is something done to Jesus by God, for a mission of preaching and mercy. For Christians, this scene opens a messiahship that culminates in the cross and resurrection: mainstream Christian teaching holds that Jesus fulfilled and transformed the messianic hope by dying for humanity’s sins and rising again, and “Christ” carries all of that within it.

sections.title: Comparing the Two Understandings
sections.body:
Set side by side, the agreement is real — and so is the difference.

Both faiths agree that Jesus is the Messiah, the anointed one promised to Israel; that the title was announced from God’s side, not seized as human ambition; and that it sets Jesus apart with unique honor. A Muslim can read Peter’s “You are the Christ” and Andrew’s “We have found the Messiah!” and affirm those confessions as far as the words themselves go.

The difference lies in what each tradition pours into the title. In Christian understanding, “Christ” came to mean the eternal Son of God made flesh, whose anointed mission was to die an atoning death and rise again. In Islamic understanding, al-Masih means what the word itself says: one anointed by God — chosen, purified, and commissioned as His servant and messenger to the Children of Israel.

Islam’s case is straightforward. The very grammar of the title implies servanthood: an anointed one is, by definition, anointed by someone above him. That is exactly what the synagogue scene in Luke shows — “The Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to preach good news to the poor.” The Anointer is God; the anointed is His servant. The Quran’s declaration that “Never would the Messiah disdain to be a servant of Allah” (Quran 4:172) is, on this reading, not a demotion of Jesus but a return to the title’s plain sense.

Honesty requires naming where the disagreement runs deepest. Christian scholarship holds that Jesus redefined the messianic hope around his death and resurrection, and historians of antiquity — religious and secular alike — overwhelmingly accept that Jesus was crucified. The Quran denies that the crucifixion befell Jesus, and Muslims accept this on the authority of revelation rather than by claiming the historical sources say otherwise; that question is examined honestly in this library’s article on the crucifixion. But Islam’s position on the meaning of “Messiah” does not depend on that dispute: the title, in its origin and on Jesus’s own lips at Nazareth, describes a human being anointed by God — precisely what the Quran affirms.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A first misconception: “Muslims deny that Jesus is the Messiah.” The opposite is true. The Quran names him al-Masih again and again, conferring the title through the angels before his birth (Quran 3:45). Denying it would contradict the Quran itself.

A second: “Messiah means a divine being, so calling Jesus the Messiah is calling him God.” Linguistically and historically, that is not what the word means. “Anointed one” was a title of Israel’s human kings and priests, and the Hebrew Bible applies it even to the Persian king Cyrus. Divinity entered the meaning of “Christ” through later Christian doctrine about Jesus — not through the word itself.

A third: “The Quran uses al-Masih as an empty borrowed name.” In fact the Quran deploys the title deliberately: it bestows it at the annunciation (3:45), joins it to Jesus’s role as God’s messenger and word (4:171), and reaches for it precisely where servanthood is affirmed (4:172) — as if to say that the anointed one, of all people, is God’s servant.

A fourth: “Accepting Jesus as the Messiah logically requires accepting the Trinity.” The title functioned for centuries in a strictly monotheistic Jewish setting where the anointed one was expected to be human. The Quran confesses Jesus as Messiah, messenger, and word of God, and immediately adds, “Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son” (Quran 4:171).

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Islam’s conclusion begins with agreement. Yes — Jesus, son of Mary, is the Messiah. The Quran says so in the voice of the angels. He is God’s word cast to Mary, honored in this world and the next, strengthened with miracles, sent to the Children of Israel with the Gospel. A Muslim who did not honor the Messiah would not be a Muslim.

But Islam invites Christians and seekers alike to ask what the title meant before later councils defined it. An anointed one is anointed by Another. The kings of Israel were anointed servants of God; the awaited deliverer was expected to be the same; and Jesus at Nazareth read aloud the words “he has anointed me to preach good news to the poor” — a mission received, not a deity disclosed. The Quran’s teaching that “Never would the Messiah disdain to be a servant of Allah” (Quran 4:172) asks to be heard not as a denial of Jesus’s greatness but as its truest description.

To the sincere seeker, Islam offers the shared title with what it holds to be its original content: Jesus the Messiah — the anointed prophet sent to Israel, born of a virgin as a sign to the worlds, who ate food, prayed, and worshipped the One who anointed him. On this understanding, honoring the Messiah and worshipping God alone are the same act of faithfulness that Jesus himself modeled.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus is the Messiah?
A: Yes. The Quran itself names him al-Masih — the Messiah — and no other figure in the Quran bears this title. Affirming it is part of mainstream Islamic belief.

Q: Does the word “Messiah” itself mean “God”?
A: No. It means “anointed one” and was used in the Hebrew Bible for human kings and priests set apart by God. The belief that the Messiah is divine is a later theological claim about Jesus, not the meaning of the word.

Q: Why does the Quran call Jesus “a word from Him”?
A: Muslims understand this as pointing to Jesus’s miraculous origin — created by God’s direct command, without a father. The same verse insists that God is one (Quran 4:171), so “word” is an honor, not a claim that Jesus is an eternal divine person.

Q: Is the “false messiah” in Islamic teaching related to this title?
A: Linguistically, yes. Narrations in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim describe an end-times deceiver called al-Masih ad-Dajjal, “the false messiah,” whom Jesus will confront after his return. That Arabic can attach masih even to a deceiver shows the word is a title, not a statement of divinity.

Q: Do Muslims believe the Messiah will return?
A: Yes. Mainstream Islamic teaching, drawing on the Quran’s allusions and well-known narrations in the major hadith collections, holds that Jesus will return before the end of history. See this library’s article on the second coming of Jesus.

Q: Can someone accept Jesus as the Messiah without accepting the Trinity?
A: Islam answers yes — that is precisely the Muslim position, and it mirrors the original Jewish expectation of a human, divinely appointed Messiah.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
For readers who want to go deeper, the following books are widely available. Tarif Khalidi’s The Muslim Jesus gathers the sayings and stories about Jesus preserved in classical Islamic literature and shows the reverence Muslim civilization has held for him. Mustafa Akyol’s The Islamic Jesus is an accessible modern account of how Jesus is understood in Islam. Geza Vermes’s Jesus the Jew is a classic historical study that places Jesus and his titles — including “Messiah” and “son of God” — within first-century Judaism. The Study Quran, edited by Seyyed Hossein Nasr, provides a full translation with commentary on the verses discussed in this article. For a concise statement of how Christian scholarship reads Jesus, Richard Bauckham’s Jesus: A Very Short Introduction is a helpful starting point.

As always in this library, we encourage readers to read both sides carefully and to weigh the evidence for themselves.

relatedArticles.title: Original Sin vs Personal Responsibility
relatedArticles.subtitle: Are we born guilty, or born pure? What the Bible and the Quran each say about Adam's sin and ours
relatedArticles.summary: Western Christianity teaches that every human being is born affected by Adam's first sin — a condition called original sin — and that this is why everyone needs the saving work of Christ. Islam teaches a different starting point: every child is born pure, in a natural state called the fitrah; Adam sinned, repented, and was completely forgiven; and no soul carries the guilt of another. This article presents each view from its own scriptures — Romans 5 and Psalm 51 on the Christian side, the Quran's verses on Adam and personal responsibility on the Islamic side — and then compares them, including the Bible's own words in Ezekiel 18 and Deuteronomy 24.

sections.title: In One Minute
sections.body:
Original sin is the Western Christian teaching that the sin of Adam, the first human, left every one of his descendants born into a damaged condition — and, in the classic formulation, sharing in his guilt. On this view, no one starts life with a clean record, which is why humanity needs a rescuer: Christ. Islam begins somewhere else entirely. The Quran teaches that every child is born upon the fitrah, a pure natural disposition inclined toward God; that Adam sinned, repented, and was fully forgiven on the spot; and that no soul ever bears the sin of another.

This article lets each tradition make its case from its own scripture. You will read the two Bible passages most often cited for original sin, Romans 5:12 and Psalm 51:5, exactly as they appear in the Bible, and the Quranic verses on Adam and personal accountability exactly as they appear in the Quran. Then the two frameworks are compared — including through the Bible's own striking statement in Ezekiel 18 that a child does not bear the sin of a parent.

sections.title: Why This Question Changes Everything
sections.body:
Few doctrinal questions carry as much weight as this one. What a religion believes about a newborn baby quietly determines its whole map of salvation. If a baby arrives already implicated in an ancient sin, then salvation must mean rescue from a condition no human effort can undo — and in Christianity, that rescue is the death and resurrection of Jesus. If a baby arrives pure, then salvation means something different: staying close to God, and returning to Him in repentance whenever we fail.

Two terms will appear throughout, so let us define them now. Original sin, in Western Christian usage, is not a sin a person commits; it is a state or condition inherited from Adam — a wounded human nature, and in the influential formulation of Augustine of Hippo, a share in Adam's guilt itself. The fitrah, in Islamic usage, is the opposite claim: the innate, uncorrupted disposition toward God and moral truth upon which every human being is created.

Both traditions tell the story of Adam and Eve, the first human couple, and their disobedience in the Garden. The stories begin alike. They end very differently — and the ending is where the two theologies part ways.

sections.title: The Question on the Table
sections.body:
The question is not whether human beings sin. Both Christianity and Islam agree, emphatically, that we do — constantly. The question is what we are born with before we have done anything at all. Did Adam's sin leave his descendants born guilty, or born spiritually damaged, in a way that requires a divine remedy beyond their own repentance? Or does every soul begin with a clean record, accountable only for what it will itself choose?

That single question drags several others behind it. Is it just for guilt to be inherited by people who did not commit the act? What happens to infants who die? Why does everyone die, if death entered the world as a punishment? And what, exactly, did Jesus come to do? Each tradition's answers hang together as a system, which is why this comparison is best done slowly and from the primary texts.

sections.title: How the Doctrine Took Shape
sections.body:
The raw material for the doctrine of original sin is the account in Genesis 3, where Adam and Eve eat from the forbidden tree and are expelled from the Garden. Genesis itself, however, never uses the phrase 'original sin,' and Jewish tradition — which reveres the same account — never developed such a doctrine. The doctrine's classic form was worked out centuries after Jesus, above all by Augustine of Hippo (354–430), the North African bishop whose thought shaped Western Christianity more than any other early figure.

Augustine developed and defended the doctrine during his long controversy with Pelagius, a British ascetic whose followers denied that humans inherit sin from Adam's fall. Augustine argued that humanity sinned 'in Adam' and inherits both a corrupted nature and a real share in guilt. His side prevailed: Pelagianism was condemned as a heresy in 418, and Augustine's position was codified by church councils at Carthage (418) and Orange (529). From there it passed into the mainstream of both Catholic and Protestant theology. The sixteenth-century Protestant Reformers retained the doctrine and, in some traditions, described the fallen human condition in even starker terms.

One important nuance: Eastern Orthodox Christianity never fully adopted Augustine's framework. Orthodox theology speaks of 'ancestral sin' — humanity inherits the consequences of Adam's fall, above all mortality and a weakened nature, but not Adam's personal guilt. So 'original sin' as inherited guilt is specifically a Western Christian development, not a belief of all Christians everywhere.

The Quran, proclaimed in the seventh century, retells the story of Adam with a strikingly different ending — repentance sought, forgiveness granted — and lays down a general principle of personal responsibility that leaves no room for inherited sin. The historical details above (Augustine's dates, the condemnation of 418, and the councils of Carthage and Orange) are drawn from the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's entries on Augustine and on sin in Christian thought.

sections.title: What the Quran Says
sections.body:
The Quran's starting point for human nature is a word that appears in Surah Ar-Room: the fitrah, the pure natural disposition upon which God creates every person.

فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًۭا ۚ فِطْرَتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى فَطَرَ ٱلنَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

'So direct your face toward the religion, inclining to truth. [Adhere to] the fitrah of Allah upon which He has created [all] people. No change should there be in the creation of Allah. That is the correct religion, but most of the people do not know.' (Quran 30:30)

A well-known saying of the Prophet Muhammad, recorded in the hadith collections of al-Bukhari and Muslim, teaches that every child is born upon this fitrah. A newborn, in Islam, is not a guilty creature awaiting cleansing; it is a clean slate already inclined toward its Creator.

What about Adam? The Quran does not minimize his sin. It states it bluntly:

فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ وَعَصَىٰٓ ءَادَمُ رَبَّهُۥ فَغَوَىٰ

'And Adam and his wife ate of it, and their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred.' (Quran 20:121)

But the very next verse transforms how the story is remembered:

ثُمَّ ٱجْتَبَٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ

'Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him].' (Quran 20:122)

Surah Al-Baqara tells the same ending and names the divine attributes behind it:

فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ كَلِمَٰتٍۢ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

'Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful.' (Quran 2:37)

In the Quran, Adam's story closes with forgiveness, not with a wound passed down to his children. And the Quran states the general principle in the plainest possible terms:

مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًۭا

'Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul. And whoever errs only errs against it. And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And never would We punish until We sent a messenger.' (Quran 17:15)

The same principle is repeated in Surah Al-An'aam:

قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِى رَبًّۭا وَهُوَ رَبُّ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ ۚ وَلَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُم مَّرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

'Say, "Is it other than Allah I should desire as a lord while He is the Lord of all things? And every soul earns not [blame] except against itself, and no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. Then to your Lord is your return, and He will inform you concerning that over which you used to differ."' (Quran 6:164)

Finally, the closing verse of Surah Al-Baqara ties responsibility to capacity — no one is held to account for what lies beyond their power, let alone for an act committed before they existed:

لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا ٱكْتَسَبَتْ ۗ رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَآ إِن نَّسِينَآ أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنَآ إِصْرًۭا كَمَا حَمَلْتَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِۦ ۖ وَٱعْفُ عَنَّا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَآ ۚ أَنتَ مَوْلَىٰنَا فَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَٰفِرِينَ

'Allah does not charge a soul except [with that within] its capacity. It will have [the consequence of] what [good] it has gained, and it will bear [the consequence of] what [evil] it has earned. "Our Lord, do not impose blame upon us if we have forgotten or erred. Our Lord, and lay not upon us a burden like that which You laid upon those before us. Our Lord, and burden us not with that which we have no ability to bear. And pardon us; and forgive us; and have mercy upon us. You are our protector, so give us victory over the disbelieving people."' (Quran 2:286)

Taken together, these verses give Islam's answer in full: born pure, judged individually, forgiven directly. The fall of Adam is a lesson in human weakness and divine mercy — not a hereditary wound.

sections.title: What the Bible Says
sections.body:
Fairness requires hearing the Christian case at its strongest. The doctrine of original sin rests chiefly on the apostle Paul's letter to the Romans:

'Therefore as sin entered into the world through one man, and death through sin; and so death passed to all men, because all sinned.' (Romans 5:12)

In mainstream Western Christian reading, Adam here acts as the representative head of the human race: his one act let sin and death into the world, and the surrounding passage goes on to draw a deliberate parallel between Adam, through whom came condemnation, and Christ, through whom comes justification. Historians of doctrine often note that Augustine read this verse in Latin, where the final phrase was rendered 'in whom all sinned' — that is, all humanity sinned in Adam — whereas modern translations of the Greek, including the one quoted above, read 'because all sinned.' Christian scholars continue to discuss how much weight that difference carries; the doctrine itself does not rest on this verse alone.

The second key text is David's psalm of repentance:

'Behold, I was born in iniquity. In sin my mother conceived me.' (Psalm 51:5)

The classic Christian reading takes this as a confession that sinfulness reaches back to conception itself. Others, including many modern commentators, read it as the poetry of a broken man expressing how completely sin pervades his life — a cry of anguish rather than a doctrinal statement about all newborns. Both readings exist within Christian scholarship.

Yet the Bible also contains passages that speak with remarkable directness against the transfer of sin from one person to another. The prophet Ezekiel devotes an entire chapter to rejecting a proverb which claimed that children suffer for their fathers' sins:

'The soul who sins, he shall die. The son shall not bear the iniquity of the father, neither shall the father bear the iniquity of the son. The righteousness of the righteous shall be on him, and the wickedness of the wicked shall be on him.' (Ezekiel 18:20)

The Law of Moses states the same principle as a rule of justice:

'The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers. Every man shall be put to death for his own sin.' (Deuteronomy 24:16)

Christian interpreters generally respond that these passages concern individual judgment and human courts within Israel's covenant, while Romans 5 speaks of humanity's standing before God on a different level. That response deserves to be stated fairly — and weighed against the plain wording of the texts.

Finally, there are the words of Jesus himself about children:

'Most certainly I tell you, unless you turn, and become as little children, you will in no way enter into the Kingdom of Heaven.' (Matthew 18:3)

Christian commentators usually read this as a lesson in humility and trust. Muslims notice something further: Jesus holds up the child — not the baptized adult — as the model of one fit for the Kingdom, which sits naturally with the Islamic teaching that children are born pure.

sections.title: Reading the Texts Side by Side
sections.body:
Set the quoted texts next to each other and the pattern is hard to miss. Ezekiel 18:20 says the son 'shall not bear the iniquity of the father.' Deuteronomy 24:16 says every man answers 'for his own sin.' The Quran says 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (17:15, 6:164) and 'every soul earns not [blame] except against itself' (6:164). On the principle of individual responsibility, the Hebrew prophets and the Quran speak with one voice.

The tension appears when Romans 5:12 is added. Even there, the verse as translated from the Greek ends with personal agency — death passed to all 'because all sinned.' The strong Augustinian reading, in which all humanity shares Adam's very guilt, leaned on a Latin rendering that modern Christian translations have set aside. What remains debated within Christianity is how much of the doctrine survives that correction: most Western churches still teach an inherited fallen condition, while Eastern Orthodoxy confines the inheritance to mortality and weakness, not guilt.

The deepest difference, though, is not a single verse but the ending of Adam's story. In the Augustinian telling, the Garden closes with an unhealed wound that only the cross, centuries later, can treat. In the Quran's telling, the wound is healed before Adam leaves the story: 'Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance' (Quran 2:37); 'Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him]' (Quran 20:122). Where one tradition sees a debt accumulating across millennia, the other sees a door — repentance — that has stood open since the first human sin, for Adam and for every child of Adam after him.

sections.title: Common Misunderstandings on Both Sides
sections.body:
It is worth clearing away several frequent confusions, because both traditions are often misstated in debate.

First: Islam does not teach that human beings are sinless or naturally good in a naive sense. The Quran describes Adam's disobedience without excuse — 'And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred' (Quran 20:121) — and expects every person to sin and to repent throughout life. The disagreement with Christianity is not about whether we sin; it is about whether we inherit sin.

Second: original sin does not mean Christians believe babies have personally done something wrong. Catholic teaching is explicit that original sin is a state contracted by inheritance, not an act committed by the child. Fair comparison requires stating the doctrine as its adherents state it.

Third: not all Christians hold the Augustinian doctrine. Eastern Orthodox Christianity speaks of ancestral sin — inherited mortality and a weakened nature, but not inherited guilt. A Christian reader from that tradition will already agree with part of the Islamic critique of the Western formulation.
