Fourth: the Quran's Adam is not a trivialized figure whose sin 'did not matter.' His sin mattered enough that the Quran records it candidly; what differs is the remedy — immediate, personal repentance met by immediate, personal forgiveness.

Fifth: pointing to Ezekiel 18 is not a claim that the Bible contradicts itself beyond repair. Christian theologians have harmonizations, some of them careful and serious. The comparative question a seeker should ask is more modest: which overall framework requires the fewer and less strained harmonizations to hold its scriptures together?

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
From the texts quoted above, the Islamic position assembles itself into a simple, coherent whole. God is just: 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (Quran 17:15), and He 'does not charge a soul except [with that within] its capacity' (Quran 2:286) — so no one can be born owing a debt for an act committed before their birth. God is merciful: when the first sin happened, He 'accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful' (Quran 2:37) — so no unpaid debt was left to accumulate. And God created us sound: every child is born upon 'the fitrah of Allah upon which He has created [all] people' (Quran 30:30).

On this view, salvation needs no inherited crisis and no third party to absorb guilt. Each person stands before God directly, falls directly, and returns directly, as Adam did. Muslims find this not only just and merciful but also in deep harmony with the Bible's own words in Ezekiel 18:20 and Deuteronomy 24:16, and with the innocence Jesus pointed to when he told his followers to 'become as little children' (Matthew 18:3). The invitation of this article is simply to read all of these texts slowly, side by side, and ask which picture of God — and of the newborn child — they most naturally describe.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does Islam deny that Adam and Eve sinned?
A: No. The Quran says plainly that 'Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred' (Quran 20:121). What Islam denies is that this sin, once repented of and forgiven (Quran 20:122, 2:37), was transmitted to Adam's descendants as guilt or as a corrupted nature requiring redemption.

Q: If there is no original sin, why does everyone still die?
A: In Islam, death is not a punishment inherited from Adam; it is part of how God designed this earthly life as a test, with the return to Him at its end (Quran 6:164 speaks of that return). Mortality is our created condition, not our sentence.

Q: What exactly is the fitrah?
A: The fitrah is the innate disposition upon which God creates every human being (Quran 30:30) — an inborn orientation toward its Creator and toward moral truth. Upbringing and environment can cover it over, but it is the factory setting, so to speak, of every soul.

Q: Do all Christians believe in original sin the same way?
A: No. The doctrine of inherited guilt is a Western development associated especially with Augustine. Eastern Orthodox Christianity teaches 'ancestral sin' instead: humanity inherits the consequences of the fall — mortality and weakness — but not Adam's guilt.

Q: What does Islam say about babies who die?
A: Since children are born pure and are not accountable before maturity, Islam has no concept of an unbaptized infant at risk. The mainstream position among Muslim scholars is that children who die rest in God's mercy.

sections.title: Sources and Further Reading
sections.body:
Every Quran verse in this article was retrieved from api.alquran.cloud (Tanzil Uthmani Arabic text with the Saheeh International translation) and every Bible verse from bible-api.com (World English Bible, a public-domain translation), and each is quoted verbatim as returned. The historical claims about Augustine of Hippo (354–430), the condemnation of Pelagianism in 418, and the councils of Carthage (418) and Orange (529) were checked against the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entries on Augustine and on sin in Christian thought.

For readers who want to go deeper, the further-reading list below includes classic Christian sources, so you can encounter the doctrine of original sin as its greatest exponents taught it; a standard academic survey of Christian theology; an Eastern Orthodox account of ancestral sin; and accessible treatments of the Islamic understanding of human nature, sin, and repentance. Reading both sides in their own words is the fairest path — and this library exists to make that path easier.

relatedArticles.title: Did Jesus Worship God?
relatedArticles.subtitle: What the Gospel accounts and the Quran both record about the prayer life of Jesus — and what it means
relatedArticles.summary: Both the Bible and the Quran record Jesus praying, fasting, and prostrating to God. The Gospels show him praying before dawn, all night on a mountainside, and face-down on the ground in Gethsemane, while the Quran quotes him saying 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.' Mainstream Christianity understands this prayer as the incarnate Son communing with the Father within the Trinity; Islam understands it as the devotion of God's honored prophet and servant. This article quotes the key texts from both scriptures, presents both readings fairly, and then makes the Islamic case from the texts themselves.

sections.title: In a Nutshell
sections.body:
The four Gospels repeatedly describe Jesus praying: alone before dawn, all night on a mountainside, and face-down on the ground in the garden of Gethsemane on the night of his arrest. He fasted, he prostrated, and he taught that God alone deserves worship. The Quran describes the same man in one sentence: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.'

This article gathers the actual texts — five passages from the Quran and seven from the Bible, each quoted word for word — and lets them speak. It then explains, fairly, how Christians and Muslims each understand a praying Jesus, and why Islam sees in his prayer life the simplest answer to the question of who he really was: not God receiving worship, but God's beloved prophet offering it.

sections.title: A Question Both Faiths Can Answer Together
sections.body:
Ask a Muslim and a Christian who Jesus is, and you will get two very different answers. Ask them whether Jesus prayed, and you will get the same answer from both. The Gospel writers mention his prayer life so often, and so casually, that it reads less like a claim being argued and more like a habit everyone around him knew: he slipped away before sunrise to pray, he spent whole nights in prayer, and in his final hours he fell on his face and pleaded with God.

The Quran describes the same pattern. In it, Jesus — called Isa in Arabic — introduces himself with the words 'I am the servant of Allah' and says that God 'has enjoined upon me prayer' for as long as he lives.

That agreement makes this question unusually valuable for a sincere seeker. Many debates about Jesus turn on disputed verses, Greek grammar, or complicated manuscript history. This one does not. The evidence sits on the surface of both scriptures, in plain narrative sentences. Our task is simply to gather those sentences and then ask, with fairness to both faiths, what a praying, fasting, prostrating Jesus tells us about who he was.

sections.title: What Exactly Are We Asking?
sections.body:
First, a definition. In both the Bible and the Quran, worship means devotion directed to God: prayer, fasting, prostration (bowing with the face to the ground), thanksgiving, and obedience offered to the Creator. The Arabic word is ibadah, and it comes from the same root as abd, meaning 'servant.' A worshiper is, by definition, a servant of the One being worshiped.

So the question 'Did Jesus worship God?' really contains two questions. The first is factual: do the records show Jesus performing acts of worship, and if so, toward whom did he direct them? The second is interpretive: if Jesus did worship God, what does that tell us about his identity? Worship flows in one direction — from the dependent to the One depended upon, from creature to Creator. A person who prays is asking; a person who fasts is submitting; a person who prostrates places himself low before another.

As we will see, Muslims and Christians largely agree on the factual answer. Where they part ways is on the second question — and that difference goes to the heart of what separates the two faiths.

sections.title: A Praying Jew in First-Century Palestine
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century in Roman-ruled Palestine, and he lived as a Jew. The everyday piety of a devout Jew in that world included regular prayer, fasting, worship at the synagogue and the Jerusalem Temple, and the declaration that God is one. The Gospels — Christianity's own foundational documents — show Jesus fully inside this world: he attends synagogue, keeps the festivals, fasts, and constantly quotes the Hebrew scriptures, including their commands to worship God alone.

It is also historically important that the formal doctrines about Jesus' divine nature were articulated after his lifetime, as Christianity spread through the Greek and Roman world. The Council of Nicaea, which met in 325 CE, declared the Son to be of the same substance as the Father, and a later council in the mid-fifth century, at Chalcedon, defined Jesus as one person with two complete natures, fully divine and fully human. Christians regard these councils as clarifying what the scriptures already taught. Muslims regard them as adding a layer of philosophy on top of a simpler original message.

Either way, one point matters for this article: the reports of Jesus praying are older than the councils that explained why he prayed. The prayer came first; the theology about the prayer came later. That order is worth keeping in mind as we turn to the texts themselves.

sections.title: Jesus in the Quran: The Servant Who Prays
sections.body:
The Quran mentions Jesus in dozens of verses and consistently presents him through one lens: he is God's honored Messiah, messenger, and servant, and his message was that God alone deserves worship. His very first words in the Quran — spoken miraculously as an infant in the cradle to defend his mother Mary — are a declaration of servanthood:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

'[Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet.' (Quran 19:30, Saheeh International)

The Arabic phrase abd Allah means 'servant of Allah.' In Islam this is not a lowly title but the highest one a human being can hold. The infant Jesus continues:

وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّۭا

'And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive' (Quran 19:31, Saheeh International)

Notice what Jesus says God commanded him to do: salah (formal prayer) and zakah (obligatory charity) — the same core duties every Muslim carries. In the Quran's portrait, Jesus is not the object of worship; he is a worshiper, under God's command, for as long as he lives.

His message to others matched his own practice. Speaking to the Children of Israel, he says:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۗ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

'Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path."' (Quran 3:51, Saheeh International)

The Quran repeats this sentence, in almost identical words, as the summary of his teaching:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

'Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is a straight path."' (Quran 43:64, Saheeh International)

Twice, the same claim: God is 'my Lord and your Lord.' Jesus places himself on the same side of the Creator-creature line as his listeners. Finally, the Quran gives Jesus a speech on the Day of Judgment, when God asks him whether he ever told people to take him and his mother as gods besides Allah. Jesus answers:

مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ شَهِيدٌ

'I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness.' (Quran 5:117, Saheeh International)

For the Quran, then, the answer to our question is not a side detail. Jesus worshiping God, and calling everyone else to do the same, is the whole point of who Jesus is.

sections.title: Jesus in the Gospels: A Life Full of Prayer
sections.body:
Now the Bible, quoted here from the World English Bible translation. What is striking is how strongly the Gospel narrative agrees with the Quranic portrait on the factual question. Mark, widely regarded by scholars as the earliest Gospel, describes Jesus' habit this way:

Early in the morning, while it was still dark, he rose up and went out, and departed into a deserted place, and prayed there. (Mark 1:35)

Luke reports the same pattern as routine:

But he withdrew himself into the desert, and prayed. (Luke 5:16)

These were not public performances. The texts stress that he prayed alone, in deserted places, before anyone was awake. Before choosing his twelve closest disciples, Luke tells us:

In these days, he went out to the mountain to pray, and he continued all night in prayer to God. (Luke 6:12)

Note Luke's own wording: all night in prayer 'to God.' The most intense scene comes on the night of his arrest, in the garden of Gethsemane:

He went forward a little, fell on his face, and prayed, saying, “My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass away from me; nevertheless, not what I desire, but what you desire.” (Matthew 26:39)

Here is prostration — falling on the face — the very posture Muslims take in every salah. And here is surrender: my desire yields to Yours. The Arabic word for that surrender of the will to God is islam.

The Letter to the Hebrews, reflecting back on Jesus' earthly life, sums it up in one remarkable sentence:

He, in the days of his flesh, having offered up prayers and petitions with strong crying and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and having been heard for his godly fear, (Hebrews 5:7)

Jesus offers prayers and petitions; God is the one 'able to save him'; and he is heard because of his reverence toward God.

In John's Gospel — the Gospel with the loftiest language about Jesus — Jesus prays aloud before his disciples:

Jesus said these things, and lifting up his eyes to heaven, he said, “Father, the time has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may also glorify you; even as you gave him authority over all flesh, so he will give eternal life to all whom you have given him. This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ. (John 17:1-3)

In his own prayer, Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and describes himself as the one 'whom you sent.' Finally, when the devil tempts him, Jesus states the rule that governs all worship:

Then Jesus said to him, “Get behind me, Satan! For it is written, ‘You shall worship the Lord your God, and you shall serve him only.’” (Matthew 4:10)

He is quoting the Torah, the scripture of his people: worship belongs to God alone.

sections.title: Two Readings of One Record
sections.body:
Islam holds that Jesus worshiped God because he was exactly what he said he was: God's servant and messenger. His prayer, fasting, and prostration were the natural acts of a created being before his Creator, and his instruction 'so worship Him' places his followers on the same path he walked. On this reading, nothing in his prayer life needs explaining; it is simply what a prophet does.

Mainstream Christianity holds — and fairness requires stating this properly — that Jesus is the eternal Son of God who took on a complete human nature. Christians do not believe in three gods; the doctrine of the Trinity teaches one God in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. When Jesus prays, Christian theology says, the incarnate Son prays in and through his human nature, and his prayer expresses the eternal loving communion between the Son and the Father. On this reading, Jesus praying to the Father is not one god petitioning another, and not God talking to himself, but a real relationship between distinct persons within the one God.

The texts say: Jesus rose before dawn to pray, withdrew habitually to pray, prayed all night 'to God,' fell on his face and yielded his will to the Father's, offered petitions 'with strong crying and tears to him who was able to save him from death,' was 'heard for his godly fear,' addressed the Father as 'the only true God,' and taught that God alone is to be worshiped and served. The Quran says he called himself the servant of Allah, was commanded to pray and give charity for as long as he lived, and told everyone that God is 'my Lord and your Lord.'

The Islamic argument at this point is an argument from simplicity. Both readings are internally coherent. But one requires interpretive machinery the texts themselves never state — two natures in one person, prayer offered by one divine person to another — while the other requires nothing beyond the plain sentences. Read at face value, the man these passages describe is a worshiper of God. That is the entire Islamic claim, and it is the Quran's claim in Jesus' own voice.

sections.title: Clearing Up Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
Misconception one: 'Christians believe Jesus prayed to himself.' This is not what Christian theology teaches, and repeating it attacks a position no informed Christian holds. Trinitarian doctrine distinguishes the persons of the Father and the Son, so in Christian terms the Son prays to the Father, not to himself. The serious Islamic critique is not that the doctrine is self-evidently absurd, but that it is a framework added onto texts that read naturally without it.

Misconception two: 'Muslims deny or diminish Jesus.' The opposite is true. The Quran calls Jesus the Messiah, a word from God, strengthened by the Holy Spirit, born of a virgin, and a worker of miracles by God's permission. A Muslim's faith is not valid without believing in him. What Islam denies is one thing only: that he is God or the literal son of God.

Misconception three: 'Jesus prayed only as an example for others, not because he needed to.' The texts resist this. Mark 1:35 and Luke 5:16 describe private prayer, away from every observer, before dawn and in deserted places — the opposite of a teaching demonstration. And Hebrews 5:7 describes petitions offered 'with strong crying and tears' to the One able to save him. That is real need, not theater.

Misconception four: 'Calling Jesus a servant of God is an insult.' In the Bible's own vocabulary, 'servant of God' is a title of honor carried by the prophets, and the Quran uses the equivalent word, abd, for its most honored figures, including the Prophet Muhammad himself. When the Quran's infant Jesus says 'I am the servant of Allah,' he is claiming a rank, not accepting a demotion.

sections.title: The Straight Path of the Praying Messiah
sections.body:
Stand back from the details and look at the shape of the life the sources describe. Here is a man who rises in the dark to pray while his companions sleep. He fasts. He withdraws to deserts and mountains to be alone with God. He spends entire nights in prayer. On the worst night of his life he does not proclaim his own power; he falls on his face in the dust and says, in effect: Your will, not mine. He calls the Father 'the only true God.' He answers the devil with scripture: worship God, serve him only.

Islam asks one simple question: whom does that life describe — the One who is worshiped, or one who worships?

The Quran's answer is that this is the life of a beloved servant and mighty messenger of God: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah.' Nothing in the Gospel record of his devotion has to be reinterpreted or qualified for that answer to work. And Jesus' own recorded instruction — worship the Lord your God and serve him only; Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him — is precisely what a Muslim does five times a day, with the same prostration Jesus made in Gethsemane.

To honor Jesus, on the Islamic view, is not to worship him but to worship as he worshiped and whom he worshiped. That, the Quran says twice in his own voice, is 'a straight path' — the sirat al-mustaqim from which this library takes its name. The invitation of Islam is simply this: follow the praying Messiah to the God he prayed to.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: If Jesus is God, why did he pray?
A: Christians answer that the eternal Son took on a real human nature, and in that nature he genuinely prayed, depended, and submitted; his prayer also expresses the eternal relationship between the Son and the Father. Muslims answer that a far simpler explanation is the one Jesus gives in the Quran and lives out in the Gospels: he prayed because God was his God and Lord.

Q: Do Christians believe Jesus prayed to himself?
A: No. Trinitarian teaching distinguishes the persons of the Father and the Son within the one God, so the Son prays to the Father. Muslims find this framework unnecessary, but it should be described accurately.

Q: Did Jesus pray the way Muslims pray?
A: There are striking overlaps: Matthew 26:39 shows him prostrate with his face to the ground, he fasted, and he prayed through the night and before dawn. Islam does not claim his ritual was identical to today's salah, only that his posture before God — servant before Lord — is the same.

Q: Doesn't John 17 support the Trinity, since Jesus asks the Father to glorify the Son?
A: Christians do read it that way, and that reading should be engaged rather than dismissed. Muslims reply that in the very same prayer Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and defines himself as the one God 'sent' — the vocabulary of a messenger, which is exactly the Quran's category for him.

Q: Is 'servant of God' a demotion of Jesus?
A: Not in either scripture's vocabulary. The Bible gives the title to its greatest prophets, and in the Quran servanthood to God is the highest human station, applied to Muhammad as well. Jesus claiming it from the cradle is presented as an honor, not an insult.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below are offered for general further reading only. They are not cited as proof of any specific claim in this article; the argument above rests on the scripture passages quoted, which were retrieved word for word from the Quran (Saheeh International translation) and the World English Bible.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature. A scholarly collection showing how deeply Jesus is loved and honored in the Islamic tradition.

Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God. A historical study by a non-Muslim scholar of how beliefs about Jesus' divinity developed in early Christianity.

C. S. Lewis — Mere Christianity. A classic, accessible presentation of mainstream Christian belief, useful for understanding the Christian position in its own voice.

Muhammad 'Ata ur-Rahim — Jesus, Prophet of Islam. An Islamic perspective on the history of beliefs about Jesus.

relatedArticles.title: How Was the Bible Transmitted?
relatedArticles.subtitle: From spoken word to Greek manuscripts to modern translations — the journey of the Christian scriptures, told honestly
relatedArticles.summary: The Bible reached today's readers through a long historical journey: oral teaching, composition in Greek, hand copying for many centuries, gradual agreement on the canon, and chains of translation. This article presents that history as mainstream Christian scholarship itself describes it, using Luke's own prologue as a window into how the Gospels were composed. It then offers the Islamic perspective: the Quran affirms the original Gospel of Jesus as guidance and light, while warning that scripture can be altered by human hands. The aim is an honest, respectful account that helps seekers compare the two transmission stories fairly.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
The Bible did not arrive as a single finished book. It grew over centuries: Jesus taught orally, authors later wrote accounts in Greek, scribes copied those writings by hand for well over a thousand years, and church leaders gradually agreed on which books belonged.

Almost everything in this article comes from Christian scholarship itself — the discipline called textual criticism, built largely by devout Christians. Islam, for its part, honors the original Gospel given to Jesus as true revelation from God. Knowing how the Bible reached us helps Christians and Muslims discuss scripture honestly, without caricature on either side.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
Every scripture has a history — a real, human journey from the moment of revelation to the printed page in your hands. This article traces that journey for the Bible, especially the New Testament.

A word about method. Nothing here relies on polemics. The account below is the standard picture taught in Christian seminaries. The discipline that studies it, textual criticism, was developed overwhelmingly by Christian scholars who loved the Bible and wanted to recover its earliest wording. When modern Christian Bibles carry footnotes noting that some ancient manuscripts lack a verse, that honesty is the fruit of their work.

Islam enters this conversation with respect. The Quran speaks of the Injil — the Gospel given to Jesus — as genuine revelation, "guidance and light." The Islamic question is not whether God spoke through Jesus; Muslims affirm that He did. The question is what happened to that message afterward.

sections.title: The Question We Are Asking
sections.body:
What happened between Jesus teaching in Galilee and the Bible on a bookshelf today?

Break it into stages. First, an oral stage: Jesus wrote no book, and his teaching circulated by word of mouth. Second, a writing stage: letters and Gospel accounts were composed in Greek in the decades after his ministry. Third, a copying stage: before printing, every copy was made by hand. Fourth, a canon stage: communities gradually agreed on which writings counted as scripture. Fifth, a translation stage: the text passed through Greek and Latin into thousands of languages.

Each stage is described by Christian historians themselves.

sections.title: From Spoken Word to Printed Page
sections.body:
Begin with language. Historians broadly agree that Jesus taught in Aramaic, the everyday tongue of first-century Jewish Galilee, yet the New Testament was written in Greek. Apart from a few preserved Aramaic phrases, the recorded words of Jesus therefore already stand in translation — a point made routinely in Christian introductions to the New Testament.

The earliest Christian writings are generally held to be the letters of Paul; the four Gospels came later in the first century, according to mainstream dating, drawing on oral tradition and earlier written material. None of the original documents — the autographs — survives for any book of the Bible. What survives are copies of copies.

Hand copying introduces variation. The surviving Greek manuscripts of the New Testament number in the thousands, from small early papyrus fragments — the earliest commonly dated to the second century — to complete codices such as Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, generally dated to the fourth century. Comparing them reveals many textual variants. Mainstream textual critics, most of them Christians, describe the great majority as trivial: spelling, word order, slips of the pen. A smaller number are significant, and modern Christian Bibles mark them openly — well-known examples include the longer ending of Mark and the story of the woman caught in adultery in John, both flagged in standard translations as absent from the earliest manuscripts.

The canon — the official list of biblical books — took shape gradually. Early communities used differing collections, and the earliest surviving list matching exactly today's twenty-seven New Testament books appears in a letter of Athanasius, bishop of Alexandria, in the later fourth century. Even now the Old Testament canon differs among Christians: Catholic and Orthodox Bibles include books, called deuterocanonical, that Protestant Bibles omit.

Finally, translation. The Hebrew scriptures were rendered into Greek — the Septuagint — before Jesus's time, and New Testament authors often quote that version. Jerome's Latin Vulgate later served the Western church for roughly a thousand years. Modern translations work from critical editions: scholarly reconstructions assembled by comparing manuscripts. All of this is the mainstream account, told by Christian scholarship with admirable honesty.

sections.title: What the Quran Says About the Gospel
sections.body:
The Quran's starting point is reverence for what God gave Jesus. In Surah al-Ma'idah, God says:

وَقَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَٰرِهِم بِعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ مُصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَٰهُ ٱلْإِنجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدًۭى وَنُورٌۭ وَمُصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَهُدًۭى وَمَوْعِظَةًۭ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ

"And We sent, following in their footsteps, Jesus, the son of Mary, confirming that which came before him in the Torah; and We gave him the Gospel, in which was guidance and light and confirming that which preceded it of the Torah as guidance and instruction for the righteous." (Quran 5:46)

The Injil — the Gospel given to Jesus — is described as genuine revelation: guidance and light, confirming the Torah before it. Islam affirms the message of Jesus as coming from the same God who sent the Quran.

The very next verse addresses Christians directly:

وَلْيَحْكُمْ أَهْلُ ٱلْإِنجِيلِ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَٰسِقُونَ

"And let the People of the Gospel judge by what Allah has revealed therein. And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed - then it is those who are the defiantly disobedient." (Quran 5:47)

Christians are called the People of the Gospel and urged to judge by what God revealed in it — the Gospel's original content is treated as authoritative divine guidance.

At the same time, the Quran warns that revelation can be interfered with by human hands:

فَوَيْلٌۭ لِّلَّذِينَ يَكْتُبُونَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ يَقُولُونَ هَٰذَا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَشْتَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًۭا قَلِيلًۭا ۖ فَوَيْلٌۭ لَّهُم مِّمَّا كَتَبَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَوَيْلٌۭ لَّهُم مِّمَّا يَكْسِبُونَ

"So woe to those who write the "scripture" with their own hands, then say, "This is from Allah," in order to exchange it for a small price. Woe to them for what their hands have written and woe to them for what they earn." (Quran 2:79)

This is the concept Muslims call tahrif: distortion of scripture. Two nuances matter. First, classical Muslim scholars differed over its extent — some emphasized alteration of the text, others alteration of meaning while much true text remained. Second, the verse condemns those who wrote material and falsely attributed it to God; it is not a blanket charge against every scribe or every Christian.

So the Islamic frame is neither "the Bible is worthless" nor "the Bible is exactly the original revelation." God truly gave Jesus a Gospel of guidance and light, and today's texts reach us through a long human history — carrying truth, alongside the marks of human hands.

sections.title: What the Bible Says About Itself
sections.body:
The New Testament gives us candid windows into its own composition. The clearest is the opening of Luke's Gospel, where the author explains his method:

"Since many have undertaken to set in order a narrative concerning those matters which have been fulfilled among us, even as those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and servants of the word delivered them to us, it seemed good to me also, having traced the course of all things accurately from the first, to write to you in order, most excellent Theophilus; that you might know the certainty concerning the things in which you were instructed." (Luke 1:1-4)

Read that slowly. Luke says many others had already written accounts. He says the material was "delivered" — handed down — from eyewitnesses to people like himself, who was not one. He describes tracing everything "accurately from the first": the language of careful investigation. This is an author describing historical research in his own words; many Christians would say God's inspiration worked precisely through such human effort.

John's Gospel closes with a similar candor about selection:

"There are also many other things which Jesus did, which if they would all be written, I suppose that even the world itself wouldn’t have room for the books that would be written." (John 21:25)

The Gospels, by their own testimony, are selections from a much larger body of things Jesus did and said — chosen and arranged by authors writing for particular audiences.

The classic Christian statement about scripture's nature comes from a letter to Timothy:

"Every Scripture is God-breathed and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for instruction in righteousness," (2 Timothy 3:16)

This verse anchors the Christian doctrine of inspiration: scripture is "God-breathed." It is also fair to note, as many Christian commentators themselves do, that when this letter was written the "Scripture" its first readers knew was above all the Hebrew scriptures — the New Testament as a collection did not yet exist — and that the verse describes scripture's character without listing which books belong.

By the Bible's own account, then, its composition involved prior sources, handed-down testimony, human investigation, and selection. That is where any honest account of transmission must begin.

sections.title: Two Transmission Stories, Side by Side
sections.body:
Set the two transmission stories side by side — each as its own scholarship tells it — and structural differences emerge.

Language: the Quran is preserved and recited in its original Arabic, while the words of Jesus were spoken in Aramaic but preserved in Greek — with few exceptions, not available in his own tongue.

Timescale: Muslim tradition and the early manuscript record describe a complete text standardized within a generation of the Prophet Muhammad's death, while the New Testament canon took shape over centuries.

Mechanism: the Quran's transmission ran on two parallel tracks — mass memorization alongside written copies — each checking the other; the Bible's ran on hand copying alone, which is why variants accumulated and textual criticism became necessary.

Self-description: Luke's prologue describes research and compilation of handed-down reports; the Quran presents itself throughout as the direct speech of God to one messenger.

Two cautions keep this honest. None of these differences impugns anyone's sincerity: generations of Christian scribes labored devotedly, and Christian scholars' candor about variants is a mark of integrity. And the Quran's own history — standardization under the caliph Uthman, early manuscripts, modes of recitation — deserves the same scrutiny, and receives it elsewhere in this library. The narrow point here is that the two scriptures traveled very different roads to reach us.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few misunderstandings cloud this topic on both sides.

"Muslims believe the Bible is worthless." Not so. The Quran calls the original Torah and Gospel guidance and light and calls Christians the People of the Gospel. The Islamic claim concerns transmission, not a denial that God spoke through Jesus.

"Textual variants mean nobody knows what the Bible says." Also not so. Mainstream textual critics hold that the vast majority of variants are minor and the general shape of the text is well established. The serious questions concern specific passages and wording — significant, but not a free-for-all.

"The books of the Bible were chosen at the Council of Nicaea." This story circulates in novels and internet memes, but historians of Christianity describe canon formation as a gradual process reflected in usage, debate, and later lists and councils. Repeating the Nicaea legend weakens any argument built on it.

"Since 2 Timothy says scripture is God-breathed, the transmission question is settled." The verse states a doctrine of inspiration; it does not list the canon's contents, and it could not certify copies and translations made centuries later. Inspiration and transmission are two different questions, and this article is about the second.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion: Continuity and Criterion
sections.body:
Islam's answer to all this is continuity. God did not speak once and fall silent: He sent the Torah to Moses, the Gospel to Jesus, and finally — Muslims believe — the Quran. The Quran describes its own role in that sequence:

وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ ۖ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ عَمَّا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ لِكُلٍّۢ جَعَلْنَا مِنكُمْ شِرْعَةًۭ وَمِنْهَاجًۭا ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةًۭ وَٰحِدَةًۭ وَلَٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِى مَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۖ فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًۭا فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

"And We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], the Book in truth, confirming that which preceded it of the Scripture and as a criterion over it. So judge between them by what Allah has revealed and do not follow their inclinations away from what has come to you of the truth. To each of you We prescribed a law and a method. Had Allah willed, He would have made you one nation [united in religion], but [He intended] to test you in what He has given you; so race to [all that is] good. To Allah is your return all together, and He will [then] inform you concerning that over which you used to differ." (Quran 5:48)

The Quran calls itself a confirmation of earlier scripture and a criterion over it — in Arabic, muhaymin, a guardian and standard. Whatever survives of the earlier revelation is confirmed where it agrees with the Quran, and the Quran serves as the measure where the records differ.

Notice the posture this creates. A Muslim has no need to mock the Bible's history — it is simply what Christian scholarship reports — nor to deny the light that remains in it, which the Quran affirms by name. The invitation is gentler than polemic: if you love the message of Jesus, look at the book that confirms him, honors his mother, and preserves the heart of what he taught — the call to worship the one God alone.

For the Christian reader, this history need not be threatening; your own scholars uncovered it. The open question is where God's guidance is available today in a form whose transmission can be traced with confidence. Islam's answer is the Quran — a claim to be tested against evidence in the companion articles.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Did Jesus write any part of the Bible?
A: No. Mainstream Christian scholarship agrees that Jesus left no writings. The Gospels were written about him, in Greek, in the decades after his ministry — a process Luke describes in his prologue.

Q: Do Muslims believe the Bible is completely corrupted?
A: No. The Quran affirms the original Torah and Gospel as guidance and light, and classical scholars differed over whether the distortion it warns of affected mainly the text or its interpretation. Today's Bible is seen as containing truth alongside material shaped by long human transmission.

Q: What language did Jesus speak, and why does it matter?
A: Historians broadly agree Jesus taught in Aramaic, while the New Testament was composed in Greek. With few exceptions, his words reach us in translation — a fact acknowledged in standard Christian scholarship.

Q: Who decided which books belong in the Bible?
A: No single meeting decided it. Books circulated and were debated for generations; the earliest surviving exact list of today's twenty-seven New Testament books comes from Athanasius in the later fourth century, and Christian communions still differ over the Old Testament canon.

Q: Is textual criticism an attack on Christianity?
A: No. It is a discipline built largely by Christian scholars seeking the earliest recoverable wording of the Bible. The manuscript footnotes in modern Christian Bibles are the fruit of that work.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works are offered as further reading; listing them does not imply agreement with every conclusion.

Bruce M. Metzger and Bart D. Ehrman — The Text of the New Testament: Its Transmission, Corruption, and Restoration. The standard scholarly introduction to New Testament textual criticism.

Bruce M. Metzger — The Canon of the New Testament: Its Origin, Development, and Significance. A detailed history of how the twenty-seven-book New Testament canon took shape.

F. F. Bruce — The Canon of Scripture. An evangelical Christian scholar's account of canon formation for both Testaments.

Bart D. Ehrman — Misquoting Jesus: The Story Behind Who Changed the Bible and Why. A popular-level introduction to manuscripts and variants; its conclusions are debated among scholars.

Muhammad Mustafa al-Azami — The History of the Qur'anic Text from Revelation to Compilation: A Comparative Study with the Old and New Testaments. A Muslim scholar's comparative study of Quranic and biblical transmission.

A note on sources: Quran quotations were fetched from api.alquran.cloud (Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation) and Bible quotations from bible-api.com (World English Bible), reproduced verbatim. Historical statements are kept to widely established findings, phrased in general terms rather than precise dates.

relatedArticles.title: How Was the Quran Preserved?
relatedArticles.subtitle: Memorization, manuscripts, and mass transmission: how the recitation of seventh-century Arabia became the book on your shelf
relatedArticles.summary: The Quran was preserved through two simultaneous channels: mass memorization by the Prophet Muhammad's companions and written records made by his scribes during his lifetime. A verified complete collection was assembled under Abu Bakr within about two years of the Prophet's death, and the third caliph, Uthman, distributed standardized copies about two decades later. Since then, unbroken chains of memorizers and manuscripts have carried the text in numbers Muslim scholars call mutawatir — too many independent transmitters for coordinated error. This article walks through that history, the Quran's own preservation promise, and an honest explanation of the canonical reading traditions (qira'at).

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body: The Quran was preserved through two channels working at once: living memorization and writing. During the Prophet Muhammad's lifetime his companions memorized the revelation as it came down while his scribes wrote it out; within about two years of his death the written records were gathered into one verified collection, and roughly two decades later the third caliph, Uthman, sent standardized copies to the major Muslim cities. Every generation since has passed the text on through both memory and manuscript in numbers Muslim scholars call mutawatir — too many independent carriers for coordinated error. The Quran frames this as a divine promise: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." (Quran 15:9).

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
Ask an honest question of any scripture and you are really asking two questions at once. First: what did the original message say? Second: can I trust that the text in my hands today still says it? For the Quran, Muslims give an unusually confident answer to that second question, and this article explains why in plain language.

We will look at three things: the history of how the Quran was memorized, written, collected, and standardized; the Quran's own words about its preservation; and the qira'at — the canonical reading traditions — explained honestly, because a fair account should address them rather than hide them.

Two terms before we begin. A hafiz (plural: huffaz) is a person who has memorized the entire Quran word for word. Tawatur means mass transmission: a report carried independently by so many people in every generation that they could not all have agreed on a lie or made the same mistake.

sections.title: The Question We Are Answering
sections.body:
The precise question is this: by what mechanism did the words recited by the Prophet Muhammad in seventh-century Arabia reach the printed copy on a shelf today, and how strong is the evidence that nothing was lost or changed along the way?

Notice what the question is not. It is not whether the Quran is true or divine — those are separate questions with their own articles. It is a question about transmission: the pipeline between the original recitation and the modern page. The Quran's pipeline has an unusual structure: two independent channels — mass memorization and writing — running side by side from the first generation, each checking the other.

sections.title: From Recitation to Standard Copies: The History
sections.body:
The Quran was revealed piecemeal over about twenty-three years, from roughly 610 CE until the Prophet Muhammad's death in 632 CE. It did not arrive as a bound book but as a recitation — the word Quran itself means "recitation" — and from the beginning the community received it by hearing and repeating it.

Seventh-century Arabia had a powerful memorization culture, and the Quran entered it as its most treasured text. The Prophet's companions memorized the revelation as it came, and because Muslims recite the Quran aloud in daily congregational prayers, the text was continuously performed in public — a slip before listeners who know it by heart is corrected on the spot. Hadith collections such as Sahih al-Bukhari also relate that the Prophet reviewed the revelation with the angel Gabriel every Ramadan, and twice in his final year.

Memory was not the only channel. The Prophet appointed scribes — Zayd ibn Thabit is the best known — who wrote each passage down as it was revealed, on parchment, palm stalks, flat stones, and shoulder bones, with the Prophet directing where each passage belonged within its surah (chapter). So by 632 CE the entire Quran existed twice over: in the memories of the huffaz and in written records.

The first gathering into a single collection came almost immediately. According to the account preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari, after the Battle of Yamama killed a number of memorizers, Umar ibn al-Khattab urged the first caliph, Abu Bakr, to have the written records collected. Abu Bakr commissioned Zayd ibn Thabit, who verified each verse against both the written records and the testimony of those who had learned it directly from the Prophet. The collection passed from Abu Bakr to Umar and then to Umar's daughter Hafsa, a widow of the Prophet.

The final step came under the third caliph, Uthman ibn Affan, around two decades after the Prophet's death. As Islam spread beyond Arabia, disputes arose over styles of recitation. Uthman had authoritative copies made from the collection kept with Hafsa, sent one to each major city, and ordered divergent private copies destroyed — a public policy to unify recitation, reported openly in Muslim sources, which also record that the companions broadly supported it. The standardized text is called the Uthmanic mushaf (a mushaf is a physical copy of the Quran), and its consonantal skeleton underlies every Quran printed today.

Physical evidence from that first century survives. Among the earliest is a set of parchment folios in the Mingana Collection at the University of Birmingham, containing parts of surahs 18, 19, and 20 in the early Arabic script known as Hijazi. According to the university, radiocarbon testing at Oxford dated the parchment to between 568 and 645 CE with 95.4 percent probability — a range overlapping the Prophet's own lifetime. Anyone can set those passages beside a modern copy and compare.

sections.title: The Quran's Own Promise of Preservation
sections.body:
Muslims read this history through a promise the Quran makes about itself. In Surah al-Hijr, God declares:

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَٰفِظُونَ

"Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." (Quran 15:9)

The claim is striking: the Quran does not ask its community to preserve it as a duty they might fail; it announces that God Himself has taken responsibility for guarding it. Muslims understand the whole history above — memorizers, scribes, early collection, standardized copies — as the means by which that promise was carried out.

The same theme appears at the very moment of revelation. Surah al-Qiyamah addresses the Prophet as he received the words, moving his tongue hurriedly for fear of losing them:

إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُۥ وَقُرْءَانَهُۥ

"Indeed, upon Us is its collection [in your heart] and [to make possible] its recitation." (Quran 75:17)

فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَٰهُ فَٱتَّبِعْ قُرْءَانَهُۥ

"So when We have recited it [through Gabriel], then follow its recitation." (Quran 75:18)

From the first, then, retention was declared God's responsibility rather than a human hope.

Two further passages describe the Quran's guarded status. In Surah al-Waqi'ah:

إِنَّهُۥ لَقُرْءَانٌۭ كَرِيمٌۭ

"Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an" (Quran 56:77)

فِى كِتَٰبٍۢ مَّكْنُونٍۢ

"In a Register well-protected;" (Quran 56:78)

And in Surah al-Buruj:

بَلْ هُوَ قُرْءَانٌۭ مَّجِيدٌۭ

"But this is an honored Qur'an" (Quran 85:21)

فِى لَوْحٍۢ مَّحْفُوظٍۭ

"[Inscribed] in a Preserved Slate." (Quran 85:22)

Muslim commentators have generally understood the "Register well-protected" and the "Preserved Slate" (in Arabic, al-lawh al-mahfuz) as the heavenly original kept with God: the Quran's source is guarded above, and its earthly text is guarded below.

A scripture's claim about itself cannot, by itself, prove that claim — a skeptic may fairly say so. That is why this article began with history rather than theology. The Muslim case is that the promise and the record fit together: the Quran announced it would be guarded, and the transmission history is what that guarding looked like in practice.

sections.title: What Are the Qira'at? The Reading Traditions Explained Honestly
sections.body:
Anyone who researches the Quran online will quickly meet the word qira'at, sometimes presented as a hidden problem. It is not hidden, and understanding it honestly is part of understanding preservation. (Because no Bible passages are required for this topic, this section stands in place of the usual biblical-perspective section.)

A qira'ah (plural: qira'at) is a canonical reading tradition — a complete, documented way of reciting the text, transmitted from the Prophet through his companions and the named master reciters of the early generations. Ten readings are recognized as canonical, each named after a master reciter and carried by his students. The most widespread today is the reading of Asim of Kufa as transmitted by Hafs; the reading of Nafi of Medina as transmitted by Warsh is common in North and West Africa. A printed Quran follows one of these readings and identifies which.

Most differences between readings are matters of pronunciation: vowel length, articulation, dialectal features of early Arabic. A smaller number involve a different grammatical form or word — transmitted as complementary, not contradictory. The best-known example is the fourth verse of the opening chapter. In the reading of Hafs it is:

مَٰلِكِ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ

"Sovereign of the Day of Recompense." (Quran 1:4)

Other canonical readings recite the first word as malik, "King," rather than the long maalik, "Master" or "Owner." King of the Day of Judgment and Master of the Day of Judgment are two true descriptions of the same God — a difference in emphasis, not doctrine. This example is typical: no article of Islamic belief or practice differs between canonical readings.

Hadith collections relate that the Quran was revealed in several modes (ahruf) to ease recitation for tribes with different dialects. Scholars have long discussed how exactly the ahruf relate to the qira'at, but the practical criteria are agreed: a reading is canonical only if it has an authentic, unbroken chain to the Prophet, conforms to the Uthmanic written skeleton, and is sound Arabic. Readings that failed these tests were set aside by Muslim scholars themselves, openly.

The honest summary: the qira'at are not textual corruption uncovered by critics. They are a documented canon of recitation, preserved with the same chains and rigor as the text itself, and written about openly since Islam's earliest centuries.

sections.title: How This Compares with Other Ancient Texts
sections.body:
Most works from the ancient world survive through one channel only: manuscript copying. Scribes copied manuscripts, later scribes copied the copies, and scholars today reconstruct the earliest recoverable wording by comparing surviving copies — a discipline called textual criticism. That is not an insult to any tradition; it is simply how texts without a living memorization institution travel through time.

The New Testament, for example, is described by Christian textual scholars themselves as a manuscript tradition: the original autographs have not survived, thousands of later Greek manuscripts exist with variations among them, and modern critical editions are careful reconstructions of the earliest attainable text. Mainstream Christian scholarship states this plainly and regards most variants as minor; the companion article on how the Bible was transmitted tells that story fairly.

The Quran's transmission has a different structure. It has two channels rather than one — complete memorization and writing, each correcting the other from the first generation. Standardization happened within about two decades of the source rather than emerging from centuries of copying. And the oral channel is institutional and continuous: every generation has produced huffaz in large numbers, certified in unbroken teacher-to-student chains, with the text performed daily in public worship where errors are caught immediately.

None of this, by itself, settles which scripture is true. What it establishes is narrower and important: the claim that the Quran read today is the Quran of the seventh century rests on an unusually strong transmission mechanism, and readers can weigh that evidence for themselves.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few misunderstandings come up so often that they deserve direct answers.

"Uthman created his own version of the Quran and burned the evidence." Uthman did not compose or edit a text; he had copies made from the collection compiled under Abu Bakr — assembled by the Prophet's own scribe under strict verification — and distributed them. The destruction of divergent private copies was a public, announced policy to end recitation disputes, supported by the companions and reported openly in Muslim sources — which is not how cover-ups behave.

"The qira'at prove there are many different Qurans." The qira'at are canonical reading traditions with documented chains, differing mostly in pronunciation, with no difference in doctrine. Muslim scholars catalogued them centuries ago; they are a studied feature of the tradition, not a leak.

"The Quran was only written down centuries after Muhammad." Writing began during the Prophet's lifetime; a complete verified collection existed within about two years of his death; standard copies circulated within about two decades. The Birmingham folios described earlier were radiocarbon dated, per the University of Birmingham, to a range overlapping the Prophet's lifetime.

"Early Qurans had no dots or vowel marks, so the text must have changed when marks were added." Early Arabic script recorded a consonantal skeleton (the rasm), and readers supplied pronunciation from the oral tradition — the primary channel all along. The dots and vowel signs added later did not create a new text; they encoded in ink the recitation the community already carried by heart.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Put the pieces side by side. A promise: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." (Quran 15:9). A mechanism: memorization and writing in parallel from the first generation, a verified collection within about two years of the Prophet's death, standardized copies within about two decades, and unbroken teacher-to-student chains ever since. And a result anyone can test: a hafiz from Indonesia and a hafiz from Senegal who learned the same canonical reading will recite the entire book identically.

For Muslims this is not a fortunate accident; it is what a fulfilled divine promise looks like. The Quran describes itself as "Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an" "In a Register well-protected;" (Quran 56:77-78) — guarded at its source with God and, Muslims hold, guarded in its earthly text among people.

So here is an invitation rather than a demand. If you are weighing Islam's claims, start where the evidence is most concrete and checkable. Read the text, listen to a memorizer recite it, and compare a seventh-century folio with a modern page. A serious search for God deserves a text you can trust — and the Quran's case for that trust is open for inspection.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: What does hafiz mean, and how many huffaz are there today?
A: A hafiz is a person who has memorized the entire Quran word for word. There is no official global census, but memorization schools operate on every continent and complete memorizers are commonly numbered in the millions. The text has never depended on a few individuals.

Q: What is tawatur?
A: Mass transmission: a report carried independently by so many people in every generation that coordinated fabrication or shared error is practically impossible. Muslim scholars hold that the Quran's text is mutawatir, transmitted this way both orally and in writing.

Q: Are the qira'at different Qurans?
A: No. They are canonical reading traditions transmitted with documented chains, differing mostly in pronunciation and occasionally in complementary word forms. No Islamic belief or practice differs between canonical readings.

Q: What is the Birmingham Quran manuscript?
A: Parchment folios at the University of Birmingham containing parts of surahs 18, 19, and 20 in early Hijazi script. Per the university, radiocarbon testing dated the parchment to 568-645 CE with 95.4 percent probability — a range overlapping the Prophet's lifetime.

Q: Did Uthman change the Quran?
A: No. He commissioned copies of the collection already compiled under Abu Bakr by the Prophet's scribe Zayd ibn Thabit and sent them to the major cities to end disputes over recitation. Muslim sources report the episode openly and record broad support from the companions.

Q: Does the Quran itself claim it will be preserved?
A: Yes. Quran 15:9 states: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." Muslims understand the memorizers, manuscripts, and unbroken chains of history as the fulfillment of that promise.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
Muhammad Mustafa al-Azami's The History of the Qur'anic Text is a detailed scholarly study of the Quran's collection and transmission. Ingrid Mattson's The Story of the Qur'an is an accessible academic overview of how the Quran was revealed, compiled, and lived with. Ahmad von Denffer's Ulum al-Qur'an is a beginner-friendly manual of the classical Quranic sciences, including the qira'at. Michael Sells's Approaching the Qur'an introduces the sound of the recited Quran to readers with no Arabic.

For the manuscript evidence discussed above, the University of Birmingham's Cadbury Research Library publishes information about its Quran folios, including the radiocarbon dating, on its official website.

title: The Miracles of Jesus
subtitle: What the Quran and the Gospels report — and what the miracles were meant to prove
summary: Islam and Christianity both affirm that Jesus (peace be upon him) performed real miracles: healing the blind and lepers, raising the dead, and feeding the faithful. The Quran adds distinctive wonders such as Jesus speaking as an infant in the cradle and giving life to a bird formed from clay. Strikingly, the Quran says each miracle happened 'by permission of Allah', while the New Testament itself describes them as works 'which God did by him'. This article catalogs the miracles in both scriptures and asks whether miracles prove Jesus's divinity — or his prophethood.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Few things surprise new readers of the Quran more than how much it honors Jesus (peace be upon him) — and how vividly it affirms his miracles. The Quran states that Jesus healed the blind and the leper, raised the dead, and formed a bird from clay that came alive when he breathed into it. It adds wonders the Bible does not record, such as Jesus speaking from the cradle as a newborn to defend his mother's honor.

The Gospels report their own catalog: healings, the feeding of thousands from a few loaves, and the raising of Lazarus from the tomb. So both traditions agree that Jesus was a true worker of miracles.

Where they differ is on what the miracles mean. The Quran repeats, again and again, that Jesus did these things 'by permission of Allah'. The New Testament itself describes Jesus as a man through whom God worked wonders, and records Jesus saying he can do nothing by himself. This article lays out the evidence from both scriptures and examines the central question: do miracles prove that Jesus is God, or that God sent him?

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
This article belongs to a series on Jesus as the Quran and the Bible present him. Our method throughout is simple: let each scripture speak in its own words first, quote it exactly, and only then compare. Every verse below was retrieved directly from the source texts — the Quran in the Uthmani Arabic script with the Saheeh International translation, and the Bible in the World English Bible translation.

Miracles are a natural meeting point between the two faiths. A Christian reading the Quran for the first time discovers that Islam does not reduce Jesus to a mere teacher of ethics: the Quran presents him as one of the mightiest messengers God ever sent, marked out by signs no ordinary man could produce. A Muslim reading the Gospels finds accounts of healing and mercy that feel deeply familiar in spirit.

Precisely because both traditions affirm the miracles, they make an excellent test case for the deeper question that separates the two faiths: who was Jesus? A prophet empowered by God, as Islam teaches — or God incarnate, as the later Christian creeds defined him? The miracles themselves, as we will see, carry an answer within the very way both scriptures describe them.

sections.title: The Question: Do Miracles Prove Divinity?
sections.body:
The argument is often put like this: 'Only God can raise the dead. Jesus raised the dead. Therefore Jesus is God.' It sounds compelling. But it stands or falls on one point: did Jesus perform miracles by his own inherent power, or did God perform them through him?

Both scriptures address this directly. The Quran answers with a repeated, deliberate phrase — bi-idhnillah, 'by permission of Allah' — attached to each miracle. The New Testament, in the very first public sermon of Christianity recorded in the book of Acts, describes the miracles as works 'which God did by him'. And the Hebrew Bible describes earlier prophets — Moses, Elijah, Elisha — performing wonders of the same kind, including raising the dead, without anyone concluding that those prophets were divine.

So the real question is not whether the miracles happened; both faiths say they did. The question is what they were for: proof of Jesus's own deity, or God's public endorsement of His messenger.

sections.title: Historical Context
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century, in Galilee and Judea, when the region was under Roman rule. Historians of early Christianity — whatever their personal beliefs — broadly agree that Jesus was known among his contemporaries as a healer, and his reputation for extraordinary deeds is among the most widely accepted features of his public life. The written Gospels, composed in Greek in the decades after his ministry, preserve that reputation in narrative form.

In the Israelite tradition Jesus's audience knew, miracles had a well-understood job: they were credentials. Moses came to Pharaoh with signs. Elijah and Elisha healed the sick and even raised the dead. A prophet's wonders were read as God's endorsement of the man's message, not as evidence that the man himself was divine. Jesus's miracles landed in exactly this context, and the earliest recorded Christian preaching, as we will see below, describes them in exactly these terms.

The Quran was revealed in the seventh century in Arabia, and it addresses Jews and Christians directly and by name. On the subject of Jesus's miracles it does something remarkable: rather than disputing them, it affirms them emphatically — including wonders not found in the Bible — and then frames every one of them with a phrase that settles the question of whose power was at work.

sections.title: The Miracles in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's fullest single catalog of Jesus's miracles appears in Surah Aal-i-Imraan (the third chapter), where Jesus announces his mission to the Children of Israel. Quran 3:49 reads:

وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنِّى قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍۢ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ أَنِّىٓ أَخْلُقُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ فَأَنفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ وَأُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةًۭ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

'And [make him] a messenger to the Children of Israel, [who will say], 'Indeed I have come to you with a sign from your Lord in that I design for you from clay [that which is] like the form of a bird, then I breathe into it and it becomes a bird by permission of Allah. And I cure the blind and the leper, and I give life to the dead - by permission of Allah. And I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses. Indeed in that is a sign for you, if you are believers.' (Quran 3:49, Saheeh International)

One verse gathers four wonders: giving life to a bird of clay, curing the blind and the leper, raising the dead, and announcing hidden knowledge. Twice in a single breath comes the refrain bi-idhnillah — 'by permission of Allah'. And notice what the verse says the miracles are: 'a sign for you, if you are believers.' A sign points beyond itself, to the One who sent it.

The fifth chapter returns to the same catalog, but this time the speaker is God Himself, addressing Jesus. Quran 5:110 reads:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَتِكَ إِذْ أَيَّدتُّكَ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ تُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًۭا ۖ وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ ۖ وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِى فَتَنفُخُ فِيهَا فَتَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَتُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَنكَ إِذْ جِئْتَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ فَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِنْ هَٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌۭ مُّبِينٌۭ

'[The Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, remember My favor upon you and upon your mother when I supported you with the Pure Spirit and you spoke to the people in the cradle and in maturity; and [remember] when I taught you writing and wisdom and the Torah and the Gospel; and when you designed from clay [what was] like the form of a bird with My permission, then you breathed into it, and it became a bird with My permission; and you healed the blind and the leper with My permission; and when you brought forth the dead with My permission; and when I restrained the Children of Israel from [killing] you when you came to them with clear proofs and those who disbelieved among them said, "This is not but obvious magic."' (Quran 5:110, Saheeh International)

Four times in this one verse: 'with My permission.' The repetition is not decoration; it is doctrine. Every miracle is affirmed in the same movement that attributes its power entirely to God.

The verse also mentions a miracle unique to the Quran: Jesus 'spoke to the people in the cradle'. Surah Maryam narrates the scene. When Mary, unmarried and accused, brought her newborn to her people, she simply pointed to the child. Quran 19:29 reads:

فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّۭا

'So she pointed to him. They said, "How can we speak to one who is in the cradle a child?"' (Quran 19:29, Saheeh International)

Then the infant spoke — and his first recorded words in the Quran define his whole identity. Quran 19:30 reads:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

'[Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet.' (Quran 19:30, Saheeh International)

The miracle that saves Mary's honor is, at the same moment, a declaration: servant of Allah, prophet.

The Quran also records a table miracle. The fifth chapter is named Al-Maaida, 'The Table Spread', after it. The disciples ask for a meal sent down from heaven, and the exchange is telling. Quran 5:112 reads:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَن يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةًۭ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ قَالَ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

'[And remember] when the disciples said, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, can your Lord send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven? [Jesus] said," Fear Allah, if you should be believers."' (Quran 5:112, Saheeh International)

The disciples explain their motive in Quran 5:113:

قَالُوا۟ نُرِيدُ أَن نَّأْكُلَ مِنْهَا وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنَا وَنَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ صَدَقْتَنَا وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّٰهِدِينَ

'They said, "We wish to eat from it and let our hearts be reassured and know that you have been truthful to us and be among its witnesses."' (Quran 5:113, Saheeh International)

Notice what Jesus does next. He does not produce the meal himself; he prays. Quran 5:114 reads:

قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ٱللَّهُمَّ رَبَّنَآ أَنزِلْ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةًۭ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ تَكُونُ لَنَا عِيدًۭا لِّأَوَّلِنَا وَءَاخِرِنَا وَءَايَةًۭ مِّنكَ ۖ وَٱرْزُقْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ

'Said Jesus, the son of Mary, "O Allah, our Lord, send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven to be for us a festival for the first of us and the last of us and a sign from You. And provide for us, and You are the best of providers."' (Quran 5:114, Saheeh International)

And God answers in Quran 5:115:

قَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مُنَزِّلُهَا عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنكُمْ فَإِنِّىٓ أُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًۭا لَّآ أُعَذِّبُهُۥٓ أَحَدًۭا مِّنَ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ

'Allah said, "Indeed, I will sent it down to you, but whoever disbelieves afterwards from among you - then indeed will I punish him with a punishment by which I have not punished anyone among the worlds."' (Quran 5:115, Saheeh International)

Many readers hear in this passage an echo of the Gospel table scenes — the blessed meals, the feeding miracles — retold with the lines of authority drawn sharply: the disciples ask, Jesus prays, God sends.

For the Quran, this is not a rule about Jesus alone but about every messenger. Quran 13:38 states the principle:

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًۭا مِّن قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ أَزْوَٰجًۭا وَذُرِّيَّةًۭ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍۢ كِتَابٌۭ

'And We have already sent messengers before you and assigned to them wives and descendants. And it was not for a messenger to come with a sign except by permission of Allah. For every term is a decree.' (Quran 13:38, Saheeh International)

And the Quran draws the conclusion explicitly, in a verse that names the miracles' limit. Quran 5:75 reads:

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

'The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded.' (Quran 5:75, Saheeh International)

A man who eats depends on food; the One who sustains all things depends on nothing. The miracle worker was mighty — but he was a messenger, in a long line of messengers.

sections.title: The Miracles in the Bible
sections.body:
The Gospels present Jesus healing the blind and lepers, feeding great crowds, and raising the dead. The question for our purposes is how the New Testament itself frames these works — and its earliest summary is strikingly clear. In the book of Acts, Peter stands up at Pentecost and delivers the first public sermon of the new community. Acts 2:22 reads:

'Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know,' (Acts 2:22, World English Bible)

Three phrases carry the theology: Jesus is 'a man', he is 'approved by God', and the wonders are works 'which God did by him'. The miracles authenticate the messenger; the power belongs to God.

Jesus says the same of himself in the Gospel of John. John 5:30 reads:

'I can of myself do nothing. As I hear, I judge, and my judgment is righteous; because I don't seek my own will, but the will of my Father who sent me.' (John 5:30, World English Bible)

The most dramatic miracle in John is the raising of Lazarus, and the narrative detail matters: before the dead man walks out, Jesus prays — aloud, deliberately, and he explains why. John 11:41-44 reads:

'So they took away the stone from the place where the dead man was lying. Jesus lifted up his eyes, and said, "Father, I thank you that you listened to me. I know that you always listen to me, but because of the multitude standing around I said this, that they may believe that you sent me." When he had said this, he cried with a loud voice, "Lazarus, come out!" He who was dead came out, bound hand and foot with wrappings, and his face was wrapped around with a cloth. Jesus said to them, "Free him, and let him go."' (John 11:41-44, World English Bible)

Jesus states the miracle's purpose in his own prayer: 'that they may believe that you sent me.' Not 'that I am God' — 'that you sent me'.

The same posture appears in the feeding of the five thousand. Matthew 14:19-20 reads:

'He commanded the multitudes to sit down on the grass; and he took the five loaves and the two fish, and looking up to heaven, he blessed, broke and gave the loaves to the disciples, and the disciples gave to the multitudes. They all ate, and were filled. They took up twelve baskets full of that which remained left over from the broken pieces.' (Matthew 14:19-20, World English Bible)

Before the bread multiplies, Jesus looks 'up to heaven' and blesses — the gesture of a servant receiving, not a source producing.

The Hebrew Bible supplies the precedent that Jesus's first audience already knew. Centuries earlier, the prophet Elisha raised a dead child. 2 Kings 4:32-35 reads:

'When Elisha had come into the house, behold, the child was dead, and lying on his bed. He went in therefore, and shut the door on them both, and prayed to Yahweh. He went up, and lay on the child, and put his mouth on his mouth, and his eyes on his eyes, and his hands on his hands. He stretched himself on him; and the child's flesh grew warm. Then he returned, and walked in the house once back and forth; and went up, and stretched himself out on him. Then the child sneezed seven times, and the child opened his eyes.' (2 Kings 4:32-35, World English Bible)

Elisha prayed, and God raised the dead through him. No one, in either faith, concludes that Elisha was divine.

Jesus even extends the miracles beyond himself. John 14:12 reads:

'Most certainly I tell you, he who believes in me, the works that I do, he will do also; and he will do greater works than these, because I am going to my Father.' (John 14:12, World English Bible)

In fairness, Christian scholarship reads these same texts within a different frame. The Gospel of John calls the miracles 'signs' that reveal Jesus's glory and his unique relationship to the Father, and classical Christian theology understands statements like 'I can of myself do nothing' as the voluntary submission of the divine Son — an expression of his humanity or of his eternal relation to the Father, not a denial of his deity. Christians do not typically rest the case for Jesus's divinity on the miracles alone; they rest it on the resurrection and on the way John's Gospel speaks of Jesus's identity. That is the mainstream Christian position, and any honest comparison must engage it rather than a caricature.
