sections.title: One Miracle, Two Conclusions
sections.body:
Set side by side, the agreements between the two accounts are remarkable. Both scriptures describe an angelic annunciation to a virgin — Luke names the angel Gabriel, and Muslim commentators have long identified the "Spirit" of Quran 19:17 as Gabriel as well, so the two traditions even agree on the messenger. Both record Mary's astonished question in nearly identical words: "How can I have a boy while no man has touched me?" in the Quran; "How can this be, seeing I am a virgin?" in Luke. Both give God's power as the only explanation. Both call Jesus the Messiah. Both exalt Mary among women — "Blessed are you among women!" in Luke; "chosen you above the women of the worlds" in the Quran.

The divergence begins at the interpretation. Luke's annunciation includes the words "will be called the Son of the Most High" and "the Son of God," and Christianity built its reading of the virgin birth around them: the miraculous conception is the moment the eternal Son entered the world as a man. Islam reads the same miracle through Quran 3:47 — "Such is Allah; He creates what He wills" — and through the Adam comparison of Quran 3:59.

The Adam argument deserves to be spelled out, because it is the Quran's own logic. If a fatherless conception proved divinity, Adam would have a stronger claim than Jesus, since Adam had neither father nor mother. Yet no one worships Adam or calls him God's literal son. A fatherless conception, by itself, proves one thing only: that God creates however He wills. The virgin birth makes Jesus a sign and a mercy — the Quran's exact words — but it does not make him God.

A Muslim reader will also note that sonship language in the Jewish scriptures was not always literal: the Hebrew Bible applies "son" language to Israel and to its kings as a mark of closeness and chosenness, not of shared divine nature. So the phrase "Son of God" in Luke's Jewish setting did not have to mean what later creeds made it mean. Christians respond that the rest of the New Testament points to a stronger, unique sonship — which shows where the argument really lies: not in the manger, but in the doctrine each community brings to it.

It is also honest to note what Christian theology itself concedes: since the Son's divinity is held to be eternal, the virgin birth is not, even for Christians, the proof of Jesus's divinity — it is its manner. The miracle itself, taken on its own terms, is fully at home in either theology. Islam's contention is that its reading requires nothing beyond what the texts describe: a creation by God's command, like Adam — an explanation both simpler and consistent with the absolute oneness of the God whom Jesus called his Lord.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
"Muslims deny the virgin birth." This is perhaps the most common misconception of all, and it is exactly backwards. The Quran teaches the virginal conception explicitly and defends Mary's chastity emphatically — she is "the one who guarded her chastity" (Quran 21:91). A Muslim who denied the virgin birth would be denying the plain text of the Quran.

"The Quran only rejects a crude idea of sonship that Christians never held." Mainstream Christian doctrine does deny any physical fatherhood on God's part, and fairness requires saying so. But the Quran's rejection is broader: it denies that God has offspring in any sense, physical or metaphysical, because attributing a son to God compromises His absolute self-sufficiency and uniqueness. The disagreement is real; it is just not the cartoon version.

"Islam diminishes Mary." The opposite is true. She is the only woman named in the Quran, a chapter of the Quran carries her name, and the Quran calls her chosen "above the women of the worlds" (Quran 3:42) and a model of devotion for believers (Quran 66:12).

"A child with no human father must be the Son of God." The Quran answers this directly with the case of Adam (Quran 3:59), who had no father and no mother. Careful Christian theology agrees that the virgin birth alone does not establish divinity — it locates Jesus's sonship in his eternal nature, not in the mode of his conception. So both traditions reject the inference here; they part ways over the separate claim of eternal sonship.

"The Quran and the Gospels disagree about who visited Mary." Luke names Gabriel; the Quran says God sent "Our Spirit," who appeared to her as "a well-proportioned man" (Quran 19:17), and Muslim commentary has overwhelmingly identified this Spirit as Gabriel. This is convergence, not contradiction.

"The Holy Spirit means the same thing in both accounts." In Christian doctrine the Holy Spirit is the third person of the Trinity. In Islamic understanding, the Spirit associated with Jesus's conception is the angel Gabriel, a created being who carries God's command. The narrative role is the same; the theology behind the term differs — a good example of why definitions matter in comparative reading.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion: A Sign for the Worlds
sections.body:
The Quran's own summary of the virgin birth is worth repeating: God made Mary and her son "a sign for the worlds" (Quran 21:91), "a sign to the people and a mercy from Us" (Quran 19:21). In Islam, that is precisely what the miracle is — a sign. It points away from itself, toward the One who performed it.

Islam's conclusion protects two things at once. It protects the honor of Jesus and Mary: he is the Messiah, a word from God, distinguished in this world and the Hereafter; she is pure, chosen above the women of the worlds. And it protects the absolute oneness of God — in Arabic, tawhid: God is one, without partners, parents, or offspring. The same God who created Adam from dust created Jesus in a virgin's womb by the same command: "Be," and it is.

To a sincere seeker, the shared miracle is an invitation. Muslims and Christians do not need to argue about whether God acted in Mary's womb; both say He did. The remaining question is which conclusion follows from that act with the fewest assumptions: that God became a man, or that God — who "creates what He wills" (Quran 3:47) — created a man miraculously, as He had done once before at the beginning of the human story. Islam holds that the second reading keeps everything the texts affirm — the angel, the virgin, the sign, the mercy, the Messiah — while keeping worship directed where, in both scriptures, Jesus himself directed it: toward God alone.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus was born of a virgin?
A: Yes, without qualification. The Quran states that Mary conceived Jesus while no man had touched her (Quran 3:47; 19:20), and belief in the virgin birth has been the unanimous position of Islamic scholarship. Denying it would contradict the plain text of the Quran.

Q: Why is Mary so important in Islam?
A: Mary (Maryam) is the only woman mentioned by name in the Quran, and Surah 19 is named after her. The Quran says God chose her and purified her above the women of the worlds (Quran 3:42) and presents her as a model of chastity, faith, and devotion for believers (Quran 66:12).

Q: If Jesus had no father, doesn't that make him the Son of God?
A: The Quran answers this with the example of Adam (Quran 3:59): Adam had neither father nor mother, yet he was a created human being. A miraculous origin shows God's power to create as He wills; it does not make the created one divine. Notably, classical Christian theology also holds that Jesus's divine sonship is eternal rather than caused by the virgin birth, so both traditions agree the miracle alone does not establish divinity.

Q: Who was the spirit or angel that appeared to Mary?
A: Luke names the angel Gabriel. The Quran says God sent 'Our Spirit,' who appeared to Mary in the form of a man (Quran 19:17), and Muslim commentators have overwhelmingly identified this Spirit as Gabriel. The two accounts converge on the same messenger.

Q: Do the Quran and the Bible describe the same annunciation?
A: The parallels are striking: an angel announces a son to a virgin, she asks how this can be when no man has touched her, and the answer appeals to God's power. Both accounts also call Jesus the Messiah and exalt Mary among women. The two faiths differ mainly in the theological conclusion they draw, not in the event they describe.

Q: Does the Quran call Jesus the Messiah?
A: Yes. In Quran 3:45 the angels announce him as 'the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary,' distinguished in this world and the Hereafter. Islam affirms Jesus as the Messiah sent to Israel while understanding that title as a mark of his mission, not of divinity.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The books below treat the virgin birth and the wider place of Jesus and Mary in Islam and Christianity. They range from accessible introductions to detailed scholarship, and they include Christian as well as Muslim authors so that each tradition can be heard in its own voice.

Geoffrey Parrinder — Jesus in the Qur'an. A respectful, widely used survey by a Christian scholar of religion covering the Quranic passages about Jesus and Mary, including the annunciation narratives.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature. A collection showing how deeply Jesus is loved and quoted in the Islamic tradition beyond the Quran itself.

Mustafa Akyol — The Islamic Jesus. An accessible modern discussion of what the Quran says about Jesus and Mary and how it relates to Jewish and Christian views.

Raymond E. Brown — The Birth of the Messiah. A classic, detailed Christian scholarly commentary on the infancy narratives of Matthew and Luke; useful for seeing how Christian scholarship itself reads these chapters.

Gabriel Said Reynolds — The Qur'an and the Bible: Text and Commentary. A verse-by-verse academic resource comparing Quranic passages with their biblical parallels, including the Mary narratives.

relatedArticles.title: Who Is Jesus?
relatedArticles.subtitle: Isa ibn Maryam in Islam and Christ in Christianity — two answers to history's most important question
relatedArticles.summary: Jesus of Nazareth — called Isa ibn Maryam (Jesus, son of Mary) in the Quran — is deeply honored by both Islam and Christianity, yet the two faiths answer the question 'Who is he?' very differently. Islam teaches that Jesus (peace be upon him) was born of the Virgin Mary by a miracle, that he is the Messiah and one of God's greatest messengers, and that he performed miracles by God's permission — but that he is neither God nor the son of God. Mainstream Christianity teaches that Jesus is the second person of the Trinity: the eternal Word of God who became human, was crucified for humanity's sins, and rose again. This article presents both positions from their own sources and then compares them against the quoted texts themselves.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Jesus of Nazareth is loved and honored by more people than any other figure in history — by roughly two billion Christians and roughly two billion Muslims. But the two faiths give different answers to the question of who he actually is.

Islam teaches that Jesus — known in Arabic as Isa ibn Maryam, 'Jesus, son of Mary' (peace be upon him) — was born of the Virgin Mary by a miracle, that he is the Messiah promised to Israel, a mighty messenger of God who performed miracles by God's permission, and a man who will be honored in this life and the next. Islam also teaches, just as firmly, that he is not God and not the son of God.

Mainstream Christianity teaches that Jesus is the second person of the Trinity — one God existing in three persons — the eternal Word of God who took on human nature, was crucified for humanity's sins, and rose from the dead.

This article presents each position as its own scholars state it, then places the quoted texts side by side so you can weigh them for yourself.

sections.title: Introduction: A Question Both Faiths Care About
sections.body:
No conversation between a Muslim and a Christian gets very far before it arrives at Jesus. That is not an accident. For Christians, Jesus is the center of the faith itself. For Muslims, belief in Jesus is not optional either: the Quran names him dozens of times, devotes an entire chapter to his mother Mary (Surah Maryam, the nineteenth chapter of the Quran), and makes belief in him one of the required articles of faith. A person cannot be a Muslim while rejecting Jesus.

This surprises many readers meeting Islam for the first time. Islam is, in a real sense, a Jesus-affirming religion. It affirms his miraculous virgin birth, his title of Messiah (al-Masih in Arabic — the anointed one promised to the Children of Israel), his miracles, and his honored rank among the greatest messengers God ever sent.

Where the two faiths part ways is on one precise point: is Jesus divine? Christianity's mainstream answer, settled in its creeds, is yes — he is God the Son, fully divine and fully human. Islam's answer is no — he is God's servant and messenger, fully human and nothing less than magnificent in that role.

Because the disagreement is precise, it can be examined honestly. In what follows, every verse of the Quran and the Bible is quoted directly from its source text, so that nothing rests on anyone's memory or paraphrase.

sections.title: The Real Question: Not Whether Jesus Matters, but Who He Is
sections.body:
It helps to clear away what is not in dispute. Islam and Christianity agree that Jesus existed, that he was born of the Virgin Mary without a human father, that he is the Messiah, that he performed genuine miracles, that he taught people to worship God, and that his story is not over — both traditions expect his return before the end of history.

That is a remarkable amount of common ground. The disagreement sits on top of it, and it can be stated in one sentence. Christianity says Jesus is the Creator who entered His creation; Islam says Jesus is the greatest of a long line of human messengers sent by the Creator.

Everything else — the Trinity, the crucifixion as atonement for sin, the meaning of the title 'Son of God' — flows from that single fork in the road.

So the fair way to proceed is to ask three questions in order. What does the Quran say Jesus is? What does the Bible say Jesus is, as mainstream Christianity reads it? And when the two sets of texts are placed side by side, which portrait do they most naturally support? The sections below take those questions one at a time.

sections.title: Historical Context: From Roman Judea to the Creeds
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century, in Judea and Galilee, lands then under Roman rule. The Judaism of his time is known to historians as Second Temple Judaism — the period when the rebuilt Temple still stood in Jerusalem — and its heartbeat was uncompromising monotheism. The daily Jewish confession, the Shema, declared that the Lord is one, and Jesus himself is recorded in Mark's Gospel reciting exactly that confession (we will quote it below). Whatever else the first hearers of Jesus understood him to be, they heard him inside that world: a Jewish teacher, preaching to Jews, about the one God of Israel.

After Jesus' time on earth ended, his followers spread across the Roman world, and a long debate began over how to describe him. Historians of doctrine document that the precise formulations Christians now recite took shape gradually over the first several centuries, as church thinkers with differing views — some holding Jesus to be subordinate to the Father, others insisting on his full deity — argued their cases.

A decisive moment came in the year 325, when the emperor Constantine hosted a council of bishops at Nicaea. The council produced the original Nicene Creed, declaring that the Father and the Son are homoousios — Greek for 'one in essence' or 'consubstantial' — and it condemned the position of Arius, a priest who taught that the Son was a created being. Later councils, including those of 431 and 451, continued to refine the doctrine, and classical Christian teaching came to affirm that Christ is one person with both a divine and a human nature. (The dates and outcomes above are confirmed by the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's history of Trinitarian doctrine.)

Christians understand this history as the church clarifying, under God's guidance, what was implicit in Jesus' words all along. Muslims read the same history differently: as evidence that the doctrine of Jesus' deity was a later development, moving step by step away from the simple monotheism Jesus preached in Galilee. The historical facts are shared; the interpretation is the debate.

sections.title: Jesus in the Quran: Servant, Messiah, Miracle
sections.body:
The Quran's account of Jesus begins with an angelic announcement to Mary:

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ يَٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍۢ مِّنْهُ ٱسْمُهُ ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ وَجِيهًۭا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْءَاخِرَةِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ

"[And mention] when the angels said, 'O Mary, indeed Allah gives you good tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary - distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near [to Allah]." (Quran 3:45)

Notice the titles packed into one verse: Messiah, son of Mary, honored in both worlds. The announcement continues:

وَيُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًۭا وَمِنَ ٱلصَّٰلِحِينَ

"He will speak to the people in the cradle and in maturity and will be of the righteous.'" (Quran 3:46)

Mary, unmarried and chaste, asks the obvious question — and receives the Quran's explanation of the virgin birth:

قَالَتْ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى وَلَدٌۭ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌۭ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًۭا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

"She said, 'My Lord, how will I have a child when no man has touched me?' [The angel] said, 'Such is Allah; He creates what He wills. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is." (Quran 3:47)

The virgin birth, in the Quran, is a creative miracle — like the creation of Adam, who had neither father nor mother — not evidence of divine sonship. God says 'Be,' and it is.

In Surah Maryam, the infant Jesus speaks from the cradle, and his very first recorded words define him:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

"[Jesus] said, 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet." (Quran 19:30)

وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّۭا

"And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive" (Quran 19:31)

وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَتِى وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِى جَبَّارًۭا شَقِيًّۭا

"And [made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a wretched tyrant." (Quran 19:32)

وَٱلسَّلَٰمُ عَلَىَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّۭا

"And peace is on me the day I was born and the day I will die and the day I am raised alive.'" (Quran 19:33)

Servant of God, prophet, blessed, a man of prayer and charity, devoted to his mother: this is the Quran's Jesus in his own miraculous words.

The Quran also affirms his miracles and divine support:

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَقَفَّيْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ بِٱلرُّسُلِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ أَفَكُلَّمَا جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُكُمُ ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ فَفَرِيقًۭا كَذَّبْتُمْ وَفَرِيقًۭا تَقْتُلُونَ

"And We did certainly give Moses the Torah and followed up after him with messengers. And We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Pure Spirit. But is it [not] that every time a messenger came to you, [O Children of Israel], with what your souls did not desire, you were arrogant? And a party [of messengers] you denied and another party you killed." (Quran 2:87)

Jesus stands here in a chain of messengers reaching back through Moses. And the Quran states his human nature with disarming simplicity:

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

"The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded." (Quran 5:75)

'They both used to eat food' — a quiet, powerful argument. One who eats is a creature with needs; the Creator needs nothing.

Finally, the Quran addresses Christians directly, with respect but without ambiguity:

يَٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌۭ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًۭا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًۭا

"O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, 'Three'; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs." (Quran 4:171)

The verse honors Jesus with three titles — messenger of God, His word, a soul from Him — and in the same breath draws the line: do not commit excess; God is one; He has no son.

sections.title: Jesus in the Bible: The Christian Reading and the Texts
sections.body:
Fairness requires stating the Christian position as Christians themselves state it. Mainstream Christianity — Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant alike — teaches that Jesus is the eternal Word of God who took on human nature: one person, fully divine and fully human. This is monotheism as Christians define it: not three gods, but one God existing in three persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. In support, Christian scholars point especially to the opening of John's Gospel, which describes God's eternal Word taking on flesh, to passages where Jesus forgives sins and accepts worship, and to the early church's practice of praying to Christ. On the classical Christian reading, Jesus' human limitations belong to his human nature, while his divine nature remained fully God.

That is the doctrine. Now let us hear the texts themselves, quoted from the World English Bible translation. In John's Gospel, Jesus prays to the Father the night before his arrest:

"This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ." (John 17:3)

Here Jesus calls the Father 'the only true God' and describes himself as the one 'whom you sent' — the very definition of a messenger.

When asked which commandment is greatest, Jesus answers with Israel's ancient confession of God's oneness:

"Jesus answered, 'The greatest is, "Hear, Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one:"'" (Mark 12:29)

After the resurrection accounts, John records Jesus saying to Mary Magdalene:

"Jesus said to her, 'Don't hold me, for I haven't yet ascended to my Father; but go to my brothers, and tell them, "I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God."'" (John 20:17)

Jesus speaks of 'my God' — he has a God whom he worships, the same God his followers worship.

When a man addresses him as 'Good teacher,' Jesus deflects the compliment toward God:

"Behold, one came to him and said, 'Good teacher, what good thing shall I do, that I may have eternal life?' He said to him, 'Why do you call me good? No one is good but one, that is, God. But if you want to enter into life, keep the commandments.'" (Matthew 19:16-17)

Jesus also describes the source of his authority in terms of dependence and obedience:

"I can of myself do nothing. As I hear, I judge, and my judgment is righteous; because I don't seek my own will, but the will of my Father who sent me." (John 5:30)

In the Acts of the Apostles, Peter — Jesus' chief disciple — introduces Jesus to a Jerusalem crowd in these words:

"'Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know,'" (Acts 2:22)

A man approved by God, whose miracles were things 'God did by him' — this is how the earliest recorded sermon describes Jesus.

And in a letter attributed to Paul, the distinction between God and Jesus is stated as a formula:

"For there is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus," (1 Timothy 2:5)

Christian interpreters have readings of each of these passages — most commonly, that Jesus speaks here from his human nature, or in his role as the sent one, without denying his divine nature. That interpretive framework is genuinely held and should be represented honestly. The question the reader must weigh is whether that framework arises from these texts or is brought to them.

sections.title: Side by Side: The Two Portraits and the Texts
sections.body:
Islam holds that Jesus is the Messiah, born of a virgin, a worker of miracles by God's permission, one of the five greatest messengers, honored in this world and the next — and a created human servant of the one God, not God incarnate and not God's literal son.

Mainstream Christianity holds that Jesus is the second person of the Trinity: the eternal Word, consubstantial with the Father (one in essence, as the Nicene Creed puts it), who became man, died on the cross as an atoning sacrifice, and rose again. Christianity insists this is monotheism: one divine being, three persons.

The texts say, in the passages quoted above: that Jesus called the Father 'the only true God' and himself the one sent (John 17:3); that he recited Israel's confession that 'the Lord is one' (Mark 12:29); that he spoke of 'my God and your God' (John 20:17); that he redirected the word 'good' to God alone (Matthew 19:16-17); that he could do nothing of himself and sought only the will of the One who sent him (John 5:30); that his chief disciple called him 'a man approved by God' through works 'God did by him' (Acts 2:22); and that an early Christian letter calls him 'the man Christ Jesus,' the mediator between the one God and mankind (1 Timothy 2:5). The Quran, for its part, says he is God's servant, prophet, Messiah, word, and messenger — and explicitly not a son of God (Quran 19:30, 4:171).

Here is the striking observation: every one of those Bible verses could be printed inside a Quranic account of Jesus without friction. The Quranic portrait — a human Messiah, empowered by God, calling people to worship the one God — matches the plain sense of these Gospel sayings. The classical Christian portrait can absorb these verses only with the help of the two-natures framework developed in the centuries after Jesus, and formalized at councils such as Nicaea in 325.

A fair-minded reader should know that Christians also cite verses they read as pointing to Jesus' divinity, chiefly in John's Gospel, and this library examines those passages in the related articles. But on the sayings quoted here — sayings from Jesus' own recorded prayers and his disciples' earliest preaching — the simpler explanation, Muslims argue, is the Quran's.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
Misconception one: 'Muslims reject Jesus.' The opposite is true. Belief in Jesus as the Messiah and a true messenger of God is a required article of Islamic faith. The Quran mentions Jesus by name far more often than it mentions Muhammad (peace be upon them both), and Muslims traditionally add 'peace be upon him' whenever they say his name. What Islam rejects is not Jesus but his deification.

Misconception two: 'Christians worship three gods.' Mainstream Christian theology explicitly denies this. The doctrine of the Trinity, as Christian scholarship defines it, is that one God exists in three persons — a form of monotheism on its own definition. The Quran's command 'do not say, "Three"' (Quran 4:171) is understood by Muslims as a warning against any formulation that divides God's absolute oneness, however it is labeled; but honest dialogue requires describing the Trinity as Christians actually teach it, not as a crude tritheism.

Misconception three: 'The Quran calls Jesus a "word from God," so it secretly affirms his divinity.' In the Quran's own explanation, Jesus is a 'word' because he came into being by God's creative command — 'He only says to it, "Be," and it is' (Quran 3:47). The title marks the miracle of his conception, not a share in God's essence.

Misconception four: 'Islam denies the virgin birth and the miracles.' It affirms both, emphatically. Jesus' virgin birth (Quran 3:45-47) and his 'clear proofs' — his miracles, performed with God's support (Quran 2:87) — are Quranic doctrine. Notably, Acts 2:22 frames the miracles the same way: works 'which God did by him.'

Misconception five: 'Denying Jesus' divinity means disrespecting him.' In the Islamic view, calling Jesus God's faithful servant is not a demotion; servanthood to God is the highest honor a creature can hold, and Jesus himself is quoted opening his mission with exactly that title: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah' (Quran 19:30).

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Islam's answer to 'Who is Jesus?' is confident because it is simple, and simple because, Muslims believe, it is what Jesus himself said. He is the Messiah, son of Mary: conceived by a miracle, strengthened by the Pure Spirit, sent to the Children of Israel with clear proofs, a man of prayer, charity, and tenderness toward his mother, upon whom is peace the day he was born, the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive.

The Quran does not lower Jesus; it locates him — at the summit of humanity, not on the throne of God. And it argues that this is exactly where the Jesus of the Gospels places himself: praying to 'the only true God,' confessing that 'the Lord is one,' calling the Father 'my God and your God,' doing nothing of himself.

To the Christian reader, Islam extends a claim worth pondering rather than an insult: that in honoring Jesus as God's servant and Messiah, Muslims are not subtracting from his glory but restoring his own testimony. To the seeker, the invitation is even simpler. Read the verses again — all of them quoted above from their sources — and ask which portrait requires the fewest added assumptions.

The Quran's final word on the matter is both a warning and a mercy: 'do not commit excess in your religion... Indeed, Allah is but one God' (Quran 4:171). Loving Jesus rightly, Islam teaches, means worshiping the God Jesus worshiped.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe in Jesus?
A: Yes — belief in Jesus is mandatory in Islam. Muslims believe he is the Messiah, born of the Virgin Mary, a mighty messenger of God who performed miracles by God's permission. Rejecting Jesus would place a person outside Islam.

Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus is God or the son of God?
A: No. The Quran teaches that Jesus is God's servant and messenger, fully human, and explicitly states that God is exalted above having a son (Quran 4:171). Islam sees the deification of Jesus as the kind of 'excess in religion' the Quran warns against.

Q: Does Islam accept the virgin birth?
A: Yes. The Quran narrates the annunciation to Mary and her question 'How will I have a child when no man has touched me?' — answered by God's creative command, 'Be,' and it is (Quran 3:47). The virgin birth is a miracle of creation, not proof of divine sonship.

Q: What does 'Messiah' mean in Islam?
A: Al-Masih (the Messiah) is a title of honor the Quran gives uniquely to Jesus, marking him as the anointed one promised to the Children of Israel. Unlike in Christian theology, the title does not imply divinity; the Quran pairs it with 'son of Mary' and 'messenger of Allah' (Quran 4:171, 5:75).

Q: Do Muslims believe Jesus was crucified?
A: Mainstream Islamic teaching, based on the Quran, holds that Jesus was not killed by crucifixion but that God saved him and raised him to Himself, and that he will return before the end of time. That subject deserves its own careful treatment and is covered in related articles in this library.

Q: If Christians and Muslims both love Jesus, why does the difference matter?
A: Because both faiths agree that who God is — and who may be worshiped — is the most important question a human being faces. If Jesus is God, refusing him worship is a grave error; if he is God's human messenger, directing worship to him is precisely the excess both the Quran and, Muslims argue, Jesus' own recorded words warn against.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works are offered as general further reading for those who want to go deeper — from Muslim, Christian, and academic perspectives. They are listed as starting points, not as proof of any specific claim made above; every scriptural quotation in this article was drawn directly from the source texts cited in each verse's attribution, and the historical dates concerning the early councils were checked against the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's treatment of the history of Trinitarian doctrine.

Tarif Khalidi — The Muslim Jesus: Sayings and Stories in Islamic Literature. Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God. Dale Tuggy — 'Trinity,' Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy. C.S. Lewis — Mere Christianity. W. Montgomery Watt — Muslim-Christian Encounters: Perceptions and Misperceptions.

relatedArticles.title: The Miracles of Jesus
relatedArticles.subtitle: What the Quran and the Gospels report — and what the miracles were meant to prove
relatedArticles.summary: Islam and Christianity both affirm that Jesus (peace be upon him) performed real miracles: healing the blind and lepers, raising the dead, and feeding the faithful. The Quran adds distinctive wonders such as Jesus speaking as an infant in the cradle and giving life to a bird formed from clay. Strikingly, the Quran says each miracle happened 'by permission of Allah', while the New Testament itself describes them as works 'which God did by him'. This article catalogs the miracles in both scriptures and asks whether miracles prove Jesus's divinity — or his prophethood.

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Few things surprise new readers of the Quran more than how much it honors Jesus (peace be upon him) — and how vividly it affirms his miracles. The Quran states that Jesus healed the blind and the leper, raised the dead, and formed a bird from clay that came alive when he breathed into it. It adds wonders the Bible does not record, such as Jesus speaking from the cradle as a newborn to defend his mother's honor.

The Gospels report their own catalog: healings, the feeding of thousands from a few loaves, and the raising of Lazarus from the tomb. So both traditions agree that Jesus was a true worker of miracles.

Where they differ is on what the miracles mean. The Quran repeats, again and again, that Jesus did these things 'by permission of Allah'. The New Testament itself describes Jesus as a man through whom God worked wonders, and records Jesus saying he can do nothing by himself. This article lays out the evidence from both scriptures and examines the central question: do miracles prove that Jesus is God, or that God sent him?

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
This article belongs to a series on Jesus as the Quran and the Bible present him. Our method throughout is simple: let each scripture speak in its own words first, quote it exactly, and only then compare. Every verse below was retrieved directly from the source texts — the Quran in the Uthmani Arabic script with the Saheeh International translation, and the Bible in the World English Bible translation.

Miracles are a natural meeting point between the two faiths. A Christian reading the Quran for the first time discovers that Islam does not reduce Jesus to a mere teacher of ethics: the Quran presents him as one of the mightiest messengers God ever sent, marked out by signs no ordinary man could produce. A Muslim reading the Gospels finds accounts of healing and mercy that feel deeply familiar in spirit.

Precisely because both traditions affirm the miracles, they make an excellent test case for the deeper question that separates the two faiths: who was Jesus? A prophet empowered by God, as Islam teaches — or God incarnate, as the later Christian creeds defined him? The miracles themselves, as we will see, carry an answer within the very way both scriptures describe them.

sections.title: The Question: Do Miracles Prove Divinity?
sections.body:
The argument is often put like this: 'Only God can raise the dead. Jesus raised the dead. Therefore Jesus is God.' It sounds compelling. But it stands or falls on one point: did Jesus perform miracles by his own inherent power, or did God perform them through him?

Both scriptures address this directly. The Quran answers with a repeated, deliberate phrase — bi-idhnillah, 'by permission of Allah' — attached to each miracle. The New Testament, in the very first public sermon of Christianity recorded in the book of Acts, describes the miracles as works 'which God did by him'. And the Hebrew Bible describes earlier prophets — Moses, Elijah, Elisha — performing wonders of the same kind, including raising the dead, without anyone concluding that those prophets were divine.

So the real question is not whether the miracles happened; both faiths say they did. The question is what they were for: proof of Jesus's own deity, or God's public endorsement of His messenger.

sections.title: Historical Context
sections.body:
Jesus lived in the first century, in Galilee and Judea, when the region was under Roman rule. Historians of early Christianity — whatever their personal beliefs — broadly agree that Jesus was known among his contemporaries as a healer, and his reputation for extraordinary deeds is among the most widely accepted features of his public life. The written Gospels, composed in Greek in the decades after his ministry, preserve that reputation in narrative form.

In the Israelite tradition Jesus's audience knew, miracles had a well-understood job: they were credentials. Moses came to Pharaoh with signs. Elijah and Elisha healed the sick and even raised the dead. A prophet's wonders were read as God's endorsement of the man's message, not as evidence that the man himself was divine. Jesus's miracles landed in exactly this context, and the earliest recorded Christian preaching, as we will see below, describes them in exactly these terms.

The Quran was revealed in the seventh century in Arabia, and it addresses Jews and Christians directly and by name. On the subject of Jesus's miracles it does something remarkable: rather than disputing them, it affirms them emphatically — including wonders not found in the Bible — and then frames every one of them with a phrase that settles the question of whose power was at work.

sections.title: The Miracles in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's fullest single catalog of Jesus's miracles appears in Surah Aal-i-Imraan (the third chapter), where Jesus announces his mission to the Children of Israel. Quran 3:49 reads:

وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنِّى قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍۢ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ أَنِّىٓ أَخْلُقُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ فَأَنفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ وَأُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةًۭ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

'And [make him] a messenger to the Children of Israel, [who will say], 'Indeed I have come to you with a sign from your Lord in that I design for you from clay [that which is] like the form of a bird, then I breathe into it and it becomes a bird by permission of Allah. And I cure the blind and the leper, and I give life to the dead - by permission of Allah. And I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses. Indeed in that is a sign for you, if you are believers.' (Quran 3:49, Saheeh International)

One verse gathers four wonders: giving life to a bird of clay, curing the blind and the leper, raising the dead, and announcing hidden knowledge. Twice in a single breath comes the refrain bi-idhnillah — 'by permission of Allah'. And notice what the verse says the miracles are: 'a sign for you, if you are believers.' A sign points beyond itself, to the One who sent it.

The fifth chapter returns to the same catalog, but this time the speaker is God Himself, addressing Jesus. Quran 5:110 reads:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَتِكَ إِذْ أَيَّدتُّكَ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ تُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًۭا ۖ وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ ۖ وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِى فَتَنفُخُ فِيهَا فَتَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَتُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَنكَ إِذْ جِئْتَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ فَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِنْ هَٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌۭ مُّبِينٌۭ

'[The Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, remember My favor upon you and upon your mother when I supported you with the Pure Spirit and you spoke to the people in the cradle and in maturity; and [remember] when I taught you writing and wisdom and the Torah and the Gospel; and when you designed from clay [what was] like the form of a bird with My permission, then you breathed into it, and it became a bird with My permission; and you healed the blind and the leper with My permission; and when you brought forth the dead with My permission; and when I restrained the Children of Israel from [killing] you when you came to them with clear proofs and those who disbelieved among them said, "This is not but obvious magic."' (Quran 5:110, Saheeh International)

Four times in this one verse: 'with My permission.' The repetition is not decoration; it is doctrine. Every miracle is affirmed in the same movement that attributes its power entirely to God.

The verse also mentions a miracle unique to the Quran: Jesus 'spoke to the people in the cradle'. Surah Maryam narrates the scene. When Mary, unmarried and accused, brought her newborn to her people, she simply pointed to the child. Quran 19:29 reads:

فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّۭا

'So she pointed to him. They said, "How can we speak to one who is in the cradle a child?"' (Quran 19:29, Saheeh International)

Then the infant spoke — and his first recorded words in the Quran define his whole identity. Quran 19:30 reads:

قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا

'[Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet.' (Quran 19:30, Saheeh International)

The miracle that saves Mary's honor is, at the same moment, a declaration: servant of Allah, prophet.

The Quran also records a table miracle. The fifth chapter is named Al-Maaida, 'The Table Spread', after it. The disciples ask for a meal sent down from heaven, and the exchange is telling. Quran 5:112 reads:

إِذْ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَن يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةًۭ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ قَالَ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

'[And remember] when the disciples said, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, can your Lord send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven? [Jesus] said," Fear Allah, if you should be believers."' (Quran 5:112, Saheeh International)

The disciples explain their motive in Quran 5:113:

قَالُوا۟ نُرِيدُ أَن نَّأْكُلَ مِنْهَا وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنَا وَنَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ صَدَقْتَنَا وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّٰهِدِينَ

'They said, "We wish to eat from it and let our hearts be reassured and know that you have been truthful to us and be among its witnesses."' (Quran 5:113, Saheeh International)

Notice what Jesus does next. He does not produce the meal himself; he prays. Quran 5:114 reads:

قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ٱللَّهُمَّ رَبَّنَآ أَنزِلْ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةًۭ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ تَكُونُ لَنَا عِيدًۭا لِّأَوَّلِنَا وَءَاخِرِنَا وَءَايَةًۭ مِّنكَ ۖ وَٱرْزُقْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ

'Said Jesus, the son of Mary, "O Allah, our Lord, send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven to be for us a festival for the first of us and the last of us and a sign from You. And provide for us, and You are the best of providers."' (Quran 5:114, Saheeh International)

And God answers in Quran 5:115:

قَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مُنَزِّلُهَا عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنكُمْ فَإِنِّىٓ أُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًۭا لَّآ أُعَذِّبُهُۥٓ أَحَدًۭا مِّنَ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ

'Allah said, "Indeed, I will sent it down to you, but whoever disbelieves afterwards from among you - then indeed will I punish him with a punishment by which I have not punished anyone among the worlds."' (Quran 5:115, Saheeh International)

Many readers hear in this passage an echo of the Gospel table scenes — the blessed meals, the feeding miracles — retold with the lines of authority drawn sharply: the disciples ask, Jesus prays, God sends.

For the Quran, this is not a rule about Jesus alone but about every messenger. Quran 13:38 states the principle:

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًۭا مِّن قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ أَزْوَٰجًۭا وَذُرِّيَّةًۭ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍۢ كِتَابٌۭ

'And We have already sent messengers before you and assigned to them wives and descendants. And it was not for a messenger to come with a sign except by permission of Allah. For every term is a decree.' (Quran 13:38, Saheeh International)

And the Quran draws the conclusion explicitly, in a verse that names the miracles' limit. Quran 5:75 reads:

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

'The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded.' (Quran 5:75, Saheeh International)

A man who eats depends on food; the One who sustains all things depends on nothing. The miracle worker was mighty — but he was a messenger, in a long line of messengers.

sections.title: The Miracles in the Bible
sections.body:
The Gospels present Jesus healing the blind and lepers, feeding great crowds, and raising the dead. The question for our purposes is how the New Testament itself frames these works — and its earliest summary is strikingly clear. In the book of Acts, Peter stands up at Pentecost and delivers the first public sermon of the new community. Acts 2:22 reads:

'Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know,' (Acts 2:22, World English Bible)

Three phrases carry the theology: Jesus is 'a man', he is 'approved by God', and the wonders are works 'which God did by him'. The miracles authenticate the messenger; the power belongs to God.

Jesus says the same of himself in the Gospel of John. John 5:30 reads:

'I can of myself do nothing. As I hear, I judge, and my judgment is righteous; because I don't seek my own will, but the will of my Father who sent me.' (John 5:30, World English Bible)

The most dramatic miracle in John is the raising of Lazarus, and the narrative detail matters: before the dead man walks out, Jesus prays — aloud, deliberately, and he explains why. John 11:41-44 reads:

'So they took away the stone from the place where the dead man was lying. Jesus lifted up his eyes, and said, "Father, I thank you that you listened to me. I know that you always listen to me, but because of the multitude standing around I said this, that they may believe that you sent me." When he had said this, he cried with a loud voice, "Lazarus, come out!" He who was dead came out, bound hand and foot with wrappings, and his face was wrapped around with a cloth. Jesus said to them, "Free him, and let him go."' (John 11:41-44, World English Bible)

Jesus states the miracle's purpose in his own prayer: 'that they may believe that you sent me.' Not 'that I am God' — 'that you sent me'.

The same posture appears in the feeding of the five thousand. Matthew 14:19-20 reads:

'He commanded the multitudes to sit down on the grass; and he took the five loaves and the two fish, and looking up to heaven, he blessed, broke and gave the loaves to the disciples, and the disciples gave to the multitudes. They all ate, and were filled. They took up twelve baskets full of that which remained left over from the broken pieces.' (Matthew 14:19-20, World English Bible)

Before the bread multiplies, Jesus looks 'up to heaven' and blesses — the gesture of a servant receiving, not a source producing.

The Hebrew Bible supplies the precedent that Jesus's first audience already knew. Centuries earlier, the prophet Elisha raised a dead child. 2 Kings 4:32-35 reads:

'When Elisha had come into the house, behold, the child was dead, and lying on his bed. He went in therefore, and shut the door on them both, and prayed to Yahweh. He went up, and lay on the child, and put his mouth on his mouth, and his eyes on his eyes, and his hands on his hands. He stretched himself on him; and the child's flesh grew warm. Then he returned, and walked in the house once back and forth; and went up, and stretched himself out on him. Then the child sneezed seven times, and the child opened his eyes.' (2 Kings 4:32-35, World English Bible)

Elisha prayed, and God raised the dead through him. No one, in either faith, concludes that Elisha was divine.

Jesus even extends the miracles beyond himself. John 14:12 reads:

'Most certainly I tell you, he who believes in me, the works that I do, he will do also; and he will do greater works than these, because I am going to my Father.' (John 14:12, World English Bible)

In fairness, Christian scholarship reads these same texts within a different frame. The Gospel of John calls the miracles 'signs' that reveal Jesus's glory and his unique relationship to the Father, and classical Christian theology understands statements like 'I can of myself do nothing' as the voluntary submission of the divine Son — an expression of his humanity or of his eternal relation to the Father, not a denial of his deity. Christians do not typically rest the case for Jesus's divinity on the miracles alone; they rest it on the resurrection and on the way John's Gospel speaks of Jesus's identity. That is the mainstream Christian position, and any honest comparison must engage it rather than a caricature.

sections.title: Reading the Two Accounts Side by Side
sections.body:
Set the two scriptures beside each other and a remarkable convergence appears. The Quran says Jesus gave life to the clay bird, cured the blind and the leper, and raised the dead 'by permission of Allah' (Quran 3:49) — 'with My permission', God says four times over (Quran 5:110). The New Testament's first sermon calls the miracles 'wonders and signs which God did by him' (Acts 2:22). Jesus himself says, 'I can of myself do nothing' (John 5:30), and before raising Lazarus he thanks the Father 'that you listened to me' (John 11:41). On the mechanics of the miracles — whose power was at work — the Quran and the earliest Christian preaching speak with one voice: the power was God's.

The two scriptures even agree on the purpose. The Quran calls the wonders 'a sign for you, if you are believers' (Quran 3:49). Jesus, in John, prays aloud 'that they may believe that you sent me' (John 11:42). In both texts the miracle is an argument for sent-ness — for prophethood — addressed to an audience deciding whether to trust the man who stands before them.

The precedent of earlier prophets seals the logical point. Elisha prayed and a dead child revived (2 Kings 4:32-35). Moses came to Pharaoh with signs. If raising the dead proved divinity, the proof would prove far too much: it would deify Elisha and Elijah, and — by Jesus's own words that believers 'will do greater works than these' (John 14:12) — his followers too. No Christian accepts those conclusions, which shows that the inference from miracle to divinity is not one the Bible itself teaches.

Where the traditions genuinely part ways is elsewhere. Christian theology reads Jesus's dependence on the Father as the internal life of the Trinity: the Son eternally receives from the Father, so dependence is compatible with deity. Islam replies that this reading arrives after the fact: taken plainly, a man who prays, receives permission, and credits God is showing us what a servant of God looks like — which is exactly what the infant Jesus of the Quran announces: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah' (Quran 19:30). It is also worth saying honestly that the Christian case for Jesus's divinity does not mainly rest on the miracles; it rests on the resurrection and on the exalted language of John's Gospel. Those questions deserve their own careful treatment — see our articles on the crucifixion and on whether Jesus claimed to be God. But on the ground this article covers, the miracles themselves, the two scriptures point the same way.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A first misconception: 'Muslims deny the miracles of Jesus.' The truth is the reverse. The Quran affirms every major category of miracle the Gospels report — healing the blind and the leper, raising the dead, wondrous provision of food — and adds wonders of its own: the newborn Jesus speaking in the cradle (Quran 19:29-30) and the clay bird brought to life (Quran 3:49). Within Islam these are real events, affirmed by God's own word, not legends to be explained away.

A second misconception: 'If Jesus raised the dead, he must be God.' As we have seen, both scriptures block this inference. The Quran attaches 'by permission of Allah' to the miracles; Acts says God did them 'by him'; Jesus says he can do nothing of himself; and Elisha raised the dead centuries earlier without anyone worshiping him. A miracle demonstrates that God is acting; it does not identify the man through whom God acts as God Himself.

A third misconception, raised from the other direction: 'The Quran borrowed the clay-bird story from a Christian legend.' It is true, and should be said plainly, that a similar story of the child Jesus bringing clay birds to life appears in the Infancy Gospel of Thomas, an early non-canonical Christian writing that circulated long before the Quran, and secular scholars often describe the Quran as engaging stories its first audience already knew. The Islamic response is twofold. First, the Quran presents itself as confirming and correcting earlier tradition; if the event truly happened, its survival in Christian memory — even outside the canonical Gospels — is precisely what one would expect, and retelling a true event is not borrowing a fiction. Second, the Quran's framing is pointedly its own: where a legend might display a boy wielding wondrous power, the Quran twice inserts 'by permission of Allah' into the telling (Quran 3:49), stripping the story of any suggestion of independent divine power. The Quran does not copy; it corrects.

A fourth misconception: 'Christians claim the miracles alone prove Jesus is God.' Thoughtful Christian theologians generally do not argue this — they know their own Bible describes prophets and apostles working wonders by God's power. The serious Christian arguments lie elsewhere, in the resurrection and in John's portrait of Jesus. Recognizing this keeps the conversation honest: the miracles are common ground between the two faiths, not a battleground.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
In Islamic theology a prophet's miracle is called a mu'jizah — literally, something that disables imitation — and its function is fixed: it is God's public endorsement of His messenger. By that measure the Quran gives Jesus extraordinary honor. He is one of the greatest of the resolute messengers, whose signs began at birth and ran through his whole mission: he spoke in the cradle, gave life to clay, opened blind eyes, cleansed lepers, raised the dead, and was answered from heaven with a table spread before his disciples. Islam does not diminish the miracles of Jesus. It insists on them.

But Islam also insists on what the miracles meant, and here it appeals to the pattern both scriptures share: permission, prayer, dependence. 'It was not for a messenger to come with a sign except by permission of Allah' (Quran 13:38). 'Which God did by him' (Acts 2:22). The consistent witness of the texts is tawhid — the absolute oneness of God — with Jesus as God's servant and messenger, exactly as his first recorded words in the Quran declare: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah' (Quran 19:30).

For the sincere seeker, this is good news rather than a demotion. It means the wonder you feel reading about Lazarus walking out of the tomb, or the crowds fed from five loaves, needs no revision — only redirection. The miracles were always signposts. Islam's invitation is to follow them where they point: past the blessed hands of the messenger, to the God who healed and raised and provided through him — the God Jesus prayed to, and the God he called his Lord.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does the Quran really affirm the miracles of Jesus?
A: Yes, emphatically. The Quran states that Jesus healed the blind and the leper, raised the dead, brought a clay bird to life, announced hidden knowledge, spoke as an infant in the cradle, and was answered with a table sent down from heaven (Quran 3:49, 5:110, 5:112-115, 19:29-30). Muslims accept all of these as real events.

Q: Do miracles prove that Jesus is God?
A: Not according to either scripture. The Quran says each miracle happened 'by permission of Allah', and the New Testament describes them as wonders 'which God did by him' (Acts 2:22), while Jesus says, 'I can of myself do nothing' (John 5:30). Earlier prophets such as Elisha also raised the dead (2 Kings 4:32-35), and no one concludes they were divine.

Q: Which miracles appear in the Quran but not in the Bible?
A: Two stand out: Jesus speaking from the cradle as a newborn to defend his mother (Quran 19:29-30), and the clay bird that came alive when he breathed into it (Quran 3:49). The table sent down from heaven (Quran 5:112-115) has no exact biblical parallel, though many readers hear echoes of the Gospel feeding narratives and the Last Supper.

Q: Why does the Quran keep repeating 'by permission of Allah'?
A: Because the phrase settles the very question this article examines. In one verse God says 'with My permission' four times (Quran 5:110). The repetition affirms the miracles fully while attributing their power entirely to God — honoring Jesus as a mighty messenger without deifying him.

Q: Didn't the Quran take the clay-bird story from a Christian legend?
A: A similar story appears in the Infancy Gospel of Thomas, a non-canonical Christian text older than the Quran, and scholars note the parallel. Muslims respond that the Quran confirms and corrects earlier tradition: if the event was real, its memory surviving among Christians is expected, and the Quran retells it with its own theological frame — 'by permission of Allah' — which the legend lacks.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below come from Muslim, Christian, and secular academic authors, so a reader can test this article's claims against several perspectives. Khalidi gathers the rich Islamic literary tradition about Jesus; Reynolds sets the Quran beside the Bible passage by passage; Akyol writes an accessible Muslim account of Jesus; Ehrman and Vermes represent critical historical scholarship on how Jesus was remembered and how claims about him developed.

As always on this site, we encourage readers to open the primary sources themselves: read Surah Aal-i-Imraan and Surah Al-Maaida alongside the Gospel accounts, and weigh what each text actually says about where the power behind the miracles came from.

relatedArticles.title: Did Jesus Claim to Be God?
relatedArticles.subtitle: What the Gospels record, what the Quran declares, and how to weigh Jesus’s own words
relatedArticles.summary: Whether Jesus himself ever claimed to be God is the pivotal question between Islam and Christianity. This article presents the Gospel passages Christians cite for his divinity, with their mainstream Christian interpretation, alongside the many sayings in which Jesus distinguishes himself from God. It notes honestly that no Gospel records Jesus saying “I am God” or “worship me,” and that the most exalted language appears in the latest Gospel. The Quran’s verdict is then given in Jesus’s own voice: worship God, “my Lord and your Lord.”

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body: Did Jesus ever say, in his own words, “I am God — worship me”? This is perhaps the single most important question separating Islam and Christianity, because so much else — the Trinity, the meaning of the crucifixion, the nature of salvation — depends on the answer. This article examines the Gospel passages Christians cite as claims to divinity, the passages in which Jesus plainly distinguishes himself from God, and the Quran’s account of what Jesus actually taught. The Islamic position is that Jesus, peace be upon him, was the Messiah and a mighty messenger who called people to worship the one God alone — and that his own recorded words, read as a whole, support exactly that.

sections.title: Two Faiths, One Question
sections.body:
Muslims and Christians agree on a remarkable amount about Jesus of Nazareth. Both honour him as the Messiah, born miraculously of the Virgin Mary, a worker of wonders by God’s permission, and a teacher of profound righteousness. The Quran names Jesus in dozens of verses and devotes an entire chapter to his mother, Maryam (Mary). No one can be a Muslim without loving and believing in Jesus.

The disagreement begins at one precise point: is Jesus God? Christianity, as defined by its historic creeds, answers yes — Jesus is God the Son, the second person of the Trinity, fully divine and fully human. Islam answers no — Jesus is a beloved prophet and messenger, honoured above almost all of humanity, but a servant of God like every other created being.

Because the stakes could not be higher, the question deserves a careful answer from the sources themselves — beginning with the words of Jesus, as each tradition records them.

sections.title: What Exactly Is Being Asked?
sections.body:
It is important to be precise about what is being asked. The question is not whether the New Testament contains exalted language about Jesus; it does. Nor is it whether later church councils declared him divine; they did. The question is narrower and more decisive: did Jesus himself, in his own recorded words, ever clearly claim to be God and instruct people to worship him?

Here a simple textual fact should be stated plainly, because careful readers on both sides acknowledge it: nowhere in the four Gospels does Jesus utter the sentence “I am God,” and nowhere does he command “worship me.” Mainstream Christian scholarship does not generally dispute this. Instead, it argues that Jesus claimed divinity indirectly — through titles he accepted, actions such as forgiving sins, and statements whose implications his hearers understood. Muslims respond that for a doctrine on which, in Christian teaching, eternal salvation depends, God would not leave humanity to rely on inference — and that the indirect statements read very differently beside everything else Jesus said.

Both readings are laid out below, each in its strongest form.

sections.title: How and When the Gospels Were Written
sections.body:
To weigh the Gospel evidence fairly, a few background facts accepted by mainstream historical scholarship are needed.

Jesus taught in Aramaic in first-century Palestine, but the four Gospels were written in Greek, decades after his ministry, by authors drawing on earlier oral and written tradition. Scholars broadly agree on their relative order: Mark is generally regarded as the earliest Gospel, commonly dated to around 70–75 CE; Matthew and Luke followed over roughly the next two decades; and John is usually dated to around 95 CE or slightly later, making it the latest of the four.

That order matters, because the Gospels do not all speak about Jesus in the same register. In Mark, the earliest account, Jesus prays to God, says that no one knows the Hour but the Father, and deflects even the compliment “good.” The most elevated divine-sounding sayings — “before Abraham came into existence, I AM,” “I and the Father are one,” “he who has seen me has seen the Father” — appear only in John, the latest Gospel. Critical scholars of the New Testament widely note this trajectory: the highest christology (the understanding of Christ’s status) appears in the latest source. Christian scholars respond that John preserves genuine early tradition, and some argue devotion to Jesus arose very early; that debate is ongoing, but where the strongest sayings sit in the timeline is not in dispute.

Finally, the formal doctrine that Jesus is fully God was defined at the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE, roughly three centuries after Jesus — amid serious disagreement among Christians themselves, since the priest Arius and his many followers held that the Son was a created being.

sections.title: The Quran: Jesus in His Own Words
sections.body:
The Quran does not treat the message of Jesus as a mystery to be reconstructed. It states that message directly, and it places it on Jesus’s own lips. Announcing his mission to the Children of Israel, Jesus says:

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۗ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

“Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path."” (Quran 3:51)

The same declaration is recorded in Surah Az-Zukhruf, where the Quran summarises both his credentials and his call:

وَلَمَّا جَآءَ عِيسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَٰتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

“And when Jesus brought clear proofs, he said, "I have come to you with wisdom and to make clear to you some of that over which you differ, so fear Allah and obey me.” (Quran 43:63)

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ

“Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is a straight path."” (Quran 43:64)

Notice what the Quranic Jesus asks for: obedience to himself as God’s messenger, and worship directed to God alone. The two are never confused. On that basis the Quran issues one of its most solemn warnings — grounded, again, in Jesus’s own words:

لَقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ يَٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍۢ

“They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the Messiah, the son of Mary" while the Messiah has said, "O Children of Israel, worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord." Indeed, he who associates others with Allah - Allah has forbidden him Paradise, and his refuge is the Fire. And there are not for the wrongdoers any helpers.” (Quran 5:72)

The Quran then looks forward to the Day of Judgment, when God will question Jesus before humanity about what was done in his name:

وَإِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ءَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ ٱتَّخِذُونِى وَأُمِّىَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ سُبْحَٰنَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِحَقٍّ ۚ إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُۥ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُۥ ۚ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِى وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِكَ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ

“And [beware the Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'" He will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it. You know what is within myself, and I do not know what is within Yourself. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen.” (Quran 5:116)

And Jesus’s answer, in the Quran, is a complete summary of his ministry:

مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ شَهِيدٌ

“I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness.” (Quran 5:117)

In the Quran’s telling, then, Jesus never claimed divinity, never sought worship, and will himself disown any such claim before God. His message was the message of every prophet: worship the one God, “my Lord and your Lord.”

sections.title: The Gospels: Both Sets of Sayings
sections.body:
Christians who affirm the divinity of Jesus point above all to the Gospel of John, and three passages carry most of the weight.

The first is John 8:58: “Jesus said to them, “Most certainly, I tell you, before Abraham came into existence, I AM.”” In mainstream Christian interpretation, “I AM” deliberately echoes God’s self-identification to Moses at the burning bush in the book of Exodus, so Jesus is claiming the divine name — and the crowd’s attempt to stone him shows they understood it that way.

The second is John 10:30–33: “I and the Father are one.” Therefore Jews took up stones again to stone him. Jesus answered them, “I have shown you many good works from my Father. For which of those works do you stone me?” The Jews answered him, “We don’t stone you for a good work, but for blasphemy: because you, being a man, make yourself God.”” Here, Christians note, the audience itself accuses Jesus of making himself God — and he does not simply say, “you have misunderstood me.”

The third is John 14:9: “Jesus said to him, “Have I been with you such a long time, and do you not know me, Philip? He who has seen me has seen the Father. How do you say, ‘Show us the Father?’” On the Christian reading, Jesus is the perfect visible revelation of the invisible Father.

These interpretations deserve to be stated fairly, and they have been. But alongside them stand other sayings of Jesus — spread across the Gospels, including John itself — that point in a different direction. In Mark 10:18, when a man addresses him as “good teacher,” Jesus replies: “Jesus said to him, “Why do you call me good? No one is good except one—God.” In John 14:28 he tells his disciples: “You heard how I told you, ‘I go away, and I come to you.’ If you loved me, you would have rejoiced, because I said ‘I am going to my Father;’ for the Father is greater than I.” In Mark 13:32, speaking of the Last Day, he says: “But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.” — a plain statement that the Son’s knowledge is limited. After the events of Easter morning, in John 20:17, he says: “Jesus said to her, “Don’t hold me, for I haven’t yet ascended to my Father; but go to my brothers, and tell them, ‘I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God.’”” And in John 17:3, praying to the Father, he defines salvation itself: “This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ.”

Christian theology harmonises these sayings through later doctrinal categories: the Father is “greater” in role but not in nature; the Son’s ignorance of the Hour belongs to his human nature. These harmonisations are coherent once the doctrine of the two natures is assumed. The Muslim observation is that the doctrine must be assumed first — the plain sense of the words describes a servant of God who prays, obeys, depends, and worships.

sections.title: Weighing the Totality of Jesus’s Words
sections.body:
Set the two portraits side by side and something striking emerges: the Jesus of the Quran and the Jesus of the earliest Gospel material sound remarkably alike.

In Mark, Jesus deflects the word “good” toward God, confesses that only the Father knows the Hour, and prays to God in his hardest moments. In the Quran, Jesus says, “Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him.” The registers match. The explicit claims of divinity that Christians cite come overwhelmingly from John, written around six decades after Jesus’s ministry — and even John preserves “the Father is greater than I,” “the only true God,” and “my God and your God.”

That last phrase deserves a pause. In John 20:17 Jesus speaks of “my Father and your Father… my God and your God.” In Quran 3:51 and 43:64 he speaks of “my Lord and your Lord.” In these passages the two scriptures attribute to Jesus the very same self-understanding: he stands with humanity on the worshipping side of the line between Creator and creature.

The Islamic argument, then, is not built on ignoring the Bible. It is built on the totality of Jesus’s recorded words: a man who prayed to God, called God “my God,” disclaimed unlimited knowledge and goodness, and defined eternal life as knowing “the only true God” — and the one whom that God sent.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few recurring misunderstandings deserve direct answers.

First: “Muslims deny Jesus.” They do not. Belief in Jesus as the Messiah and one of the greatest of all messengers is an article of faith in Islam. What Muslims deny is that he is God — which, Muslims argue, is also what he himself denied.

Second: “‘I and the Father are one’ can only mean identity of essence.” Yet in the same Gospel, Jesus prays for his disciples “that they may all be one; even as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be one in us; that the world may believe that you sent me. The glory which you have given me, I have given to them; that they may be one, even as we are one;” (John 17:21–22). Whatever oneness Jesus shares with the Father in John’s language, he asks the same oneness for his followers — which fits unity of purpose and will far more naturally than unity of divine essence.

Third: “the Quran misunderstands the Trinity, because in 5:116 it speaks of Jesus and Mary.” The verse does not claim to define the church’s creed. It is a question God puts to Jesus about what people actually did — taking him and his mother as objects of devotion “besides Allah.” The Quran addresses worship as practised, and the exaltation of Mary in popular devotion was and is a real phenomenon; the verse condemns directing worship to anyone other than God, however it is theologised.

Fourth: “Jesus is called the Son of God, so he must be divine.” In biblical idiom, “son of God” was an honorific applied to kings, to the righteous, and to Israel collectively; Jewish hearers did not take it as a claim to be the Creator. The Quran rejects the title precisely because, in later usage, it came to mean something no prophet taught.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Islam’s verdict on this question is not an attack on Jesus; it is a defence of him. The Quran insists that Jesus said nothing more and nothing less than what God sent him to say: “I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord” (Quran 5:117).

On the Islamic reading, the evidence lines up. No Gospel records Jesus saying “I am God” or “worship me.” The earliest Gospel presents a praying, dependent servant of God. The loftiest language appears in the latest Gospel, and even there Jesus calls the Father “the only true God” and “my God.” The formal doctrine of his deity was settled by councils centuries later. The Quran cuts through that development and restores the original voice: “Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is a straight path.” (Quran 43:64)

For the sincere seeker, the invitation of Islam is simple and, Muslims would say, deeply loyal to Jesus: worship the God Jesus worshipped, pray to the God Jesus prayed to, and follow him as he asked to be followed — as the Messiah and messenger of the one God. That, in the Quran’s words, is the straight path.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims believe in Jesus?
A: Yes. Belief in Jesus as the Messiah and one of God’s greatest messengers is required of every Muslim. The Quran affirms his virgin birth and his miracles by God’s permission.

Q: Does Jesus ever say “I am God” anywhere in the Bible?
A: No. The sentence appears nowhere in the four Gospels, and Jesus never commands anyone to worship him. Christian scholars argue his divinity is claimed indirectly; Muslims argue the indirect passages are outweighed by his plain statements of dependence on God.

Q: What about “before Abraham came into existence, I AM” (John 8:58)?
A: Christians read it as invoking the divine name from Exodus. Muslims note it is an ambiguous phrase found only in the latest Gospel, which also records Jesus calling the Father “the only true God” (John 17:3) and “my God” (John 20:17).

Q: Why does Jesus sound so exalted in the Gospel of John?
A: Mainstream critical scholarship dates John latest of the four Gospels, around 95 CE or after, and observes that the most elevated language about Jesus appears there rather than in the earlier accounts. Muslims see in this the gradual exaltation of a prophet by later generations.

Q: What did Jesus ask people to do, according to the Quran?
A: To worship God alone: “Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path.” (Quran 3:51)

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works, from Christian, critical-historical, and Muslim perspectives, allow the reader to go deeper into how the understanding of Jesus developed and what his own words show.

Bart D. Ehrman — How Jesus Became God traces, from a critical-historical standpoint, how a Jewish preacher came to be regarded as divine, including the rising christology across the Gospels. Larry W. Hurtado — Lord Jesus Christ: Devotion to Jesus in Earliest Christianity presents the strongest scholarly case that devotion to Jesus arose early, and is included here for balance. Raymond E.
